why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the cylinder does more than spin. Its centre also moves forward, so translation is present.
Option B is incorrect because rolling requires the cylinder to rotate as it moves. Ignoring the spinning motion gives an incomplete description.
Option D is incorrect because oscillatory motion involves repeated to-and-fro movement about a mean position. A rolling cylinder does not exhibit this type of motion.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB describes an object as 'rolling', immediately ask yourself two questions:

• Is the object moving from one place to another?
• Is it also turning about its own axis?

If the answer to both questions is yes, the object undergoes combined translational and rotational motion.
explanation.final_answer: C. The cylinder undergoes both translational and rotational motions simultaneously.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pushing a tin can across the floor.

As the can moves forward, its centre changes position. At the same time, the can keeps spinning around its axis.

These two motions happen together. The forward movement is translational motion, while the spinning is rotational motion.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Translational Motion to learn the differences between pure translation, pure rotation and combined motion. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional JAMB-standard worked examples and practice questions.

question:
In an automated warehouse, an automated guided vehicle (AGV) transports cartons along a straight aisle at a constant speed of 22 m s⁻¹ for 16 s.

What distance does the AGV travel during this motion?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the distance travelled by an object undergoing translational motion when its speed and the time of travel are known.
explanation.principle:
Translational motion occurs when every point of an object moves the same distance in the same direction over the same time interval. When an object moves at a constant speed, the distance travelled depends on both the speed and the time taken.

Distance is the total length of the path covered by an object, irrespective of direction.

The relationship is:

Distance = Speed × Time

where:
• Distance is measured in metres (m).
• Speed is measured in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
• Time is measured in seconds (s).
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Speed, v = 22 m s⁻¹
Time, t = 16 s

Step 2: State the appropriate formula.

Distance = Speed × Time

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

Distance = 22 × 16

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

Distance = 352 m

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

352 m corresponds to Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (338 m) results from an incorrect multiplication of the given values.
Option C (368 m) is greater than the correct product and does not satisfy the relationship Distance = Speed × Time.
Option D (176 m) is exactly half of the correct value and could result from mistakenly using only half the time.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever speed and time are given, first determine whether the question requires distance, speed or time.

Remember the three related equations:
• Distance = Speed × Time
• Speed = Distance ÷ Time
• Time = Distance ÷ Speed

Always check that the units are compatible before performing any calculation.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine the AGV moving steadily along a warehouse aisle. If it covers 22 metres every second and continues for 16 seconds, it travels 22 × 16 = 352 metres altogether.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Translational Motion for a complete lesson on translational motion, including distance, displacement, speed and velocity calculations, worked examples and JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional concept reviews and examination-focused exercises.

question:
While crossing a river, a ferry is carried slightly downstream by the river current as it moves toward the opposite bank. Throughout the journey, the ferry remains upright without turning about its own axis.

Which statement best describes the motion of the ferry?

options.A: It undergoes curvilinear translational motion because every point on the ferry moves together along a curved path.
options.B: It undergoes rotational motion because its path across the river is curved.
options.C: It undergoes oscillatory motion because the river current changes its direction continuously.
options.D: It remains at rest because its orientation does not change during the crossing.

answer_text: It undergoes curvilinear translational motion because every point on the ferry moves together along a curved path.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to identify translational motion in a real-life marine transportation scenario where a ferry follows a curved path due to the effect of river current.
explanation.principle:
Translational motion occurs when every point of an object moves through the same distance in the same direction during a given interval of time. The object moves as a whole without rotating about its own axis.

A beginner may ask, 'Must translational motion always be in a straight line?'

No. Translational motion can be:

• Rectilinear, when the object moves along a straight path.
• Curvilinear, when the object follows a curved path.

The important requirement is that the body moves as one unit without spinning about its own axis. In this scenario, the river current changes the ferry's path, making it curved, but since the ferry does not rotate, its motion remains translational.
steps:
Step 1: Observe the ferry's motion.

The ferry moves from one bank to the other while being carried slightly downstream by the current, producing a curved path.

Step 2: Determine whether the ferry rotates.

The question states that the ferry remains upright and does not turn about its own axis. Therefore, rotational motion is absent.

Step 3: Classify the motion.

Since the ferry moves as a single body and every point on it changes position together along a curved path, it undergoes curvilinear translational motion.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Option A correctly describes the motion.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because a curved path does not automatically mean rotational motion. Rotation involves turning about an internal axis.
Option C is incorrect because oscillatory motion is a repeated to-and-fro motion about a fixed equilibrium position, which does not occur here.
Option D is incorrect because the ferry is clearly changing its position across the river. An object can move without changing its orientation.
explanation.exam_tip: A common JAMB misconception is to associate every curved path with rotational motion. Remember that an object can undergo translational motion along either a straight or a curved path, provided it does not rotate about its own axis.
explanation.final_answer: A. It undergoes curvilinear translational motion because every point on the ferry moves together along a curved path.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine placing a cup on a tray and carrying the tray around a gentle bend without twisting it. Although the tray follows a curved path, the cup moves together with the tray without spinning. The ferry behaves in the same way, so its motion is curvilinear translation.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Translational Motion to study rectilinear and curvilinear translation, their characteristics, practical examples in transportation and engineering, and additional JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive concept reviews and worked examples.

question:
An agricultural engineer observes a tractor spraying crops as it moves steadily across a large farmland. Throughout the operation, every point on the tractor advances the same distance in the same direction over equal time intervals, while the tractor itself does not spin about its own body.

Which statement best describes the motion of the tractor?

options.D: It remains at rest because its orientation is unchanged.

explanation.question_restate: This question examines your ability to identify translational motion from a practical agricultural operation. Rather than performing calculations, you are expected to recognise the defining characteristics of pure translational motion.
explanation.principle:
Motion is the change in the position of an object with time.

Translational motion occurs when every point of an object moves the same distance in the same direction during the same time interval.

In pure translational motion, the entire body changes position without rotating about its own axis. The object's orientation remains unchanged while it moves from one location to another.

Examples include a patient trolley pushed along a corridor, a baggage cart moving through an airport, a warehouse robot travelling along an aisle and a tractor moving straight across a field.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the type of movement described.

The tractor moves steadily across the farmland, meaning its position changes continuously.

Step 2: Examine how different parts of the tractor move.

The question states that every point on the tractor moves the same distance in the same direction over equal time intervals. This is the defining feature of translational motion.

Step 3: Check for rotation.

The tractor is specifically stated not to spin about its own body. Therefore, rotational motion is absent.

Step 4: Eliminate incorrect options.

Since there is no spinning, rotational motion is incorrect.

The tractor is not moving back and forth about a fixed position, so oscillatory motion is also incorrect.

Because the tractor changes its position, it is certainly not at rest.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

The motion described is pure translational motion.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because rotational motion involves turning about an axis. The question clearly states that the tractor does not spin about its own body.
Option C is incorrect because oscillatory motion is a repeated to-and-fro movement about a mean position, which is not occurring here.
Option D is incorrect because the tractor changes its position across the farmland. An unchanged orientation does not mean the object is at rest.
explanation.exam_tip:
When identifying translational motion, focus on how every point of the object moves.

If all parts of the object move together in the same direction by the same amount without the body rotating about its own axis, the motion is translational.

Do not confuse translational motion with rotational motion simply because an object is moving.
explanation.final_answer: A. It undergoes pure translational motion.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pushing a cupboard straight across a room without turning it. Every part of the cupboard moves together in the same direction, and it does not spin.

The tractor in this question behaves in the same way. It moves as one whole object across the farmland, so it undergoes pure translational motion.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Translational Motion to learn the characteristics of translational motion, how it differs from rotational and oscillatory motion, and how to identify it in transportation, agriculture, engineering, healthcare and industrial applications. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional JAMB-style practice questions and detailed concept reviews.

question:
A rail maintenance vehicle moves in a straight line along a section of railway track, covering a distance of 432 m at a constant speed of 24 m s⁻¹.

How long does it take the vehicle to complete the journey?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the time taken by a rail maintenance vehicle to travel a known distance at a constant speed during translational motion.
explanation.principle:
Translational motion is the motion of an object in which every point of the object moves the same distance in the same direction over the same time interval.

When an object moves with constant speed in a straight line, the relationship among distance, speed and time is given by:

Speed = Distance ÷ Time

This formula can be rearranged to find time:

Time = Distance ÷ Speed

Where:
• Distance is measured in metres (m).
• Speed is measured in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
• Time is measured in seconds (s).
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Distance = 432 m
Speed = 24 m s⁻¹

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Time

Step 3: Use the appropriate formula.

Time = Distance ÷ Speed

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

Time = 432 ÷ 24

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

Time = 18 s

Step 6: Compare with the options.

18 s corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (16 s) is incorrect. It results from an incorrect calculation of the division.
Option C (20 s) is incorrect. It does not satisfy the relationship between distance, speed and time for the given values.
Option D (24 s) is incorrect. It may arise from confusing the numerical value of the speed with the required time.
explanation.exam_tip:
Remember the speed triangle:

Speed = Distance ÷ Time
Distance = Speed × Time
Time = Distance ÷ Speed

Before substituting values, always identify what quantity the question asks you to calculate and use the correctly rearranged formula.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine a maintenance vehicle travelling along a railway track.

If it covers 24 metres every second, then after 18 seconds it will have travelled:

24 × 18 = 432 m.

Therefore, the journey takes 18 seconds.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Translational Motion for a complete revision of translational motion, including calculations involving distance, displacement, speed, velocity and time, with worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional concept summaries and examination-focused practice.

question:
An engineer is inspecting an industrial robotic carriage that moves components from one workstation to another along a guided track. Four trainees make the following statements about the carriage's motion:

Statement I: Every point on the carriage moves the same distance in the same direction during pure translational motion.

Statement II: An object following a curved path cannot undergo translational motion.

Statement III: A body may undergo translational motion without rotating about its own axis.

Statement IV: During pure translational motion, the orientation of the body remains unchanged throughout the motion.

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of the defining characteristics of translational motion by asking you to evaluate several statements describing the motion of an industrial robotic carriage.
explanation.principle:
Translational motion occurs when every point of a body moves through the same distance in the same direction during the same time interval.

A beginner may ask, 'What makes translational motion different from rotational motion?'

In translational motion, the entire body changes position together without one part moving differently from another. During pure translation, the body does not rotate about its own axis, so its orientation remains unchanged.

Another beginner may ask, 'Can translational motion occur along a curved path?'

Yes. Translational motion may be rectilinear (straight-line) or curvilinear (curved-path) provided the body translates as a whole without rotating. Therefore, a curved path does not automatically mean rotational motion.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

This statement is correct. In pure translational motion, every point on the body moves the same distance in the same direction during the same time interval.

Step 2: Examine Statement II.

This statement is incorrect. Translational motion is not limited to straight-line motion. An object can undergo curvilinear translational motion if it moves as a whole without rotating.

Step 3: Examine Statement III.

This statement is correct. Pure translational motion occurs without rotation about the body's own axis.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

This statement is correct. Since the body does not rotate during pure translation, its orientation remains unchanged throughout the motion.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Statements I, III and IV are correct, while Statement II is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Statement II, which wrongly assumes that motion along a curved path cannot be translational.
Option C is incorrect because Statement II is false, even though Statements III and IV are correct.
Option D is incorrect because it includes the incorrect Statement II while omitting the correct Statement IV.
explanation.exam_tip: A common JAMB misconception is to associate every curved path with rotational motion. Remember that translational motion can be either rectilinear or curvilinear. The key feature is that the body moves as a whole, with all its points undergoing the same displacement pattern and, in pure translation, without changing orientation.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, III and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine a robotic carriage sliding smoothly along a factory guide rail. Every part of the carriage moves together from one position to another. If the carriage does not twist or spin, it is undergoing pure translational motion. Even if the guide rail bends gently, the carriage can still translate provided it does not rotate about its own axis.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Translational Motion to study the characteristics of translational motion, rectilinear and curvilinear translation, pure translation, combined translation and rotation, and authentic JAMB-style worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive concept reviews and additional practice questions.

question:
An engineer is evaluating the movement of an automated warehouse shuttle that transports cartons between storage racks. During operation, every point on the shuttle moves the same distance in the same direction at each instant while the shuttle follows a designated path without rotating about its own axis.

Which statement best explains why the shuttle is undergoing translational motion?

options.A: Its centre of mass changes position while every part of the shuttle undergoes the same displacement without changing its orientation.
options.B: It continually rotates about its own axis while remaining at the same location.
options.C: Different parts of the shuttle move through different distances at the same time.
options.D: It repeatedly swings to and fro about a fixed point without changing its overall position.

answer_text: Its centre of mass changes position while every part of the shuttle undergoes the same displacement without changing its orientation.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify the best explanation of translational motion in a practical engineering application rather than simply recalling its definition.
explanation.principle:
Motion is the change in position of an object with time.

Translational motion is a type of motion in which every point of a body moves through the same distance in the same direction during the same time interval. The body changes its position as a whole without rotating about its own axis.

A beginner may ask, 'Why is every point important?'

Imagine carrying a closed box across a room while keeping it upright. Every corner of the box moves together in the same direction and by the same amount. This is translational motion.

If the box is simultaneously spinning, then the motion is no longer pure translation because rotation has been introduced.

Translational motion may occur along either a straight path (rectilinear translation) or a curved path (curvilinear translation), provided the body does not rotate about its own axis during the motion.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the description of the shuttle.

The question states that every point on the shuttle moves the same distance in the same direction and that the shuttle does not rotate about its own axis.

Step 2: Recall the defining characteristic of translational motion.

A body is in translational motion when all its points experience identical displacement while maintaining the same orientation.

Step 3: Evaluate Option A.

This option correctly states that the shuttle changes position as a whole and that every part undergoes the same displacement without changing orientation. This is the defining feature of translational motion.

Step 4: Evaluate the remaining options.

Options B, C and D each describe other forms of motion or incorrect characteristics that do not satisfy the definition of pure translational motion.

Step 5: Select the best explanation.

Therefore, Option A provides the most accurate explanation of why the warehouse shuttle is undergoing translational motion.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because rotation about an object's own axis is rotational motion, not pure translational motion.
Option C is incorrect because in translational motion every point of the body moves through the same displacement. Different displacements indicate that the body is not undergoing pure translation.
Option D is incorrect because swinging to and fro about a fixed point describes oscillatory motion rather than translational motion.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB often tests the defining characteristics of translational motion using practical situations rather than direct definitions.

Remember these key ideas:

• Every point of the body moves through the same displacement.
• The body changes position as a whole.
• Pure translational motion does not involve rotation about the body's own axis.
• A translational path may be straight or curved provided the body's orientation remains unchanged.
explanation.final_answer: A. Its centre of mass changes position while every part of the shuttle undergoes the same displacement without changing its orientation.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pushing a cupboard straight across a smooth floor without turning it. Every part of the cupboard moves together in the same direction and by the same amount. That is translational motion.

The warehouse shuttle behaves in the same way, so Option A is the correct explanation.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Translational Motion for a complete lesson on pure translation, rectilinear and curvilinear translation, differences between translational, rotational and oscillatory motion, practical engineering applications, and additional JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed concept reviews and examination-focused exercises.

question:
An express train travels in a straight route between two cities, covering a total distance of 648 km in 9 hours.

What is the average speed of the train?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the average speed of an express train from the total distance travelled and the total time taken.
explanation.principle:
Speed describes how fast an object moves. It is a scalar quantity because it has magnitude only and no direction.

Average speed is defined as the total distance travelled divided by the total time taken.

The formula is:

Average Speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time

where:

• Average Speed is measured in km h⁻¹ or m s⁻¹.
• Total Distance is the entire length of the journey.
• Total Time is the total duration of the journey.

Average speed does not depend on the direction of motion. It simply tells us how much distance is covered per unit time.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Total distance = 648 km

Total time = 9 h

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

Average Speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

Average Speed = 648 ÷ 9

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

Average Speed = 72 km h⁻¹

Step 5: Compare the result with the options provided.

72 km h⁻¹ corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (60 km h⁻¹) results from an incorrect division and underestimates the train's speed.
Option B (66 km h⁻¹) is obtained from an incorrect calculation and does not satisfy the speed formula.
Option D (81 km h⁻¹) overestimates the average speed and is not obtained from dividing 648 km by 9 h.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question asks for average speed, remember the simple relationship:

Average Speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time.

Always ensure that the distance and time are expressed in compatible units before performing the calculation.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine a train completing a journey of 648 km in 9 hours. If it covered the same amount of distance every hour, it would travel:

648 ÷ 9 = 72 km each hour.

Therefore, its average speed is 72 km h⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed to learn the meaning of speed, the difference between speed and velocity, average speed calculations, SI units, unit conversions, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided practice and examination tips.

question: A medical drone travels at a constant speed of 28 m s⁻¹ while delivering an emergency blood sample to a nearby hospital. If the journey lasts for 45 s, what distance does the drone cover?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the distance travelled by an object when its speed and the time taken are known. The context involves a medical drone delivering an emergency blood sample.
explanation.principle:
Speed describes how fast an object moves. It is defined as the distance travelled per unit time.

The relationship between speed, distance and time is:

Speed = Distance ÷ Time

From this relationship, we obtain:

Distance = Speed × Time

where:

• Distance is measured in metres (m).
• Speed is measured in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
• Time is measured in seconds (s).

This formula is used whenever the speed remains constant throughout the motion.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Speed, v = 28 m s⁻¹

Time, t = 45 s

Required quantity = Distance, d

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

Distance = Speed × Time

Therefore,

d = vt

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

Distance = 28 × 45

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

28 × 45 = 1,260

Therefore,

Distance = 1,260 m

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

Option B corresponds to 1,260 m.

Therefore, Option B is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (630 m) is incorrect. It is exactly half of the correct distance and may result from an incorrect multiplication or using half the time.
Option C (840 m) is incorrect. This value could arise from multiplying the speed by 30 s instead of the given 45 s.
Option D (73 m) is incorrect. It results from an incorrect operation and does not satisfy the relationship between speed, time and distance.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question provides speed and time, think immediately of the formula:

Distance = Speed × Time.

Before calculating, always ensure the units are compatible. When speed is in m s⁻¹ and time is in seconds, the answer will be in metres.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine the drone travels 28 metres every second.

After 45 seconds, it would have travelled:

28 × 45 = 1,260 metres.

So, the drone covers 1,260 m during the journey.

question: During an emergency response, an ambulance travels 875 m at a constant speed of 7 m s⁻¹. How long does it take the ambulance to cover the distance?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the time taken for an ambulance to travel a known distance when its speed is constant.
explanation.principle:
Speed describes how fast an object moves. It is defined as the distance travelled per unit time.

The relationship among speed, distance and time is expressed as:

Speed = Distance ÷ Time

where:
• Speed (v) is measured in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
• Distance (d) is measured in metres (m).
• Time (t) is measured in seconds (s).

If the required quantity is time, rearrange the formula to:

Time = Distance ÷ Speed

This formula is used whenever the distance travelled and the speed are known.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Distance, d = 875 m
Speed, v = 7 m s⁻¹

Required quantity:
Time, t

Step 2: Select the appropriate formula.

Time = Distance ÷ Speed

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

t = 875 ÷ 7

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

875 ÷ 7 = 125

Therefore,
Time = 125 s.

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

Option C corresponds to 125 s and is therefore the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (115 s) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect division of the given values.
Option B (120 s) is incorrect because it underestimates the calculated time for the given speed and distance.
Option D (130 s) is incorrect because it overestimates the actual time obtained using the speed formula.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question asks for time, first check whether the distance and speed are given. If they are, use:

Time = Distance ÷ Speed

Always ensure the units are consistent before substituting values. If distance is in metres and speed is in m s⁻¹, the answer will be in seconds.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Suppose an ambulance moves at a steady speed of 7 metres every second. To travel a total distance of 875 metres, divide the distance by the speed:

875 ÷ 7 = 125 seconds.

So, the ambulance takes 125 seconds to reach its destination.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed to learn how to calculate speed, distance and time, solve unit conversion problems, and practise additional JAMB-style questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed explanations and guided practice.

question: Traffic officers monitoring vehicles on a highway record that a car is travelling at 25 m s⁻¹. What is the speed of the car in km h⁻¹?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to convert a speed expressed in metres per second (m s⁻¹) into kilometres per hour (km h⁻¹), a common skill required in JAMB Physics.
explanation.principle:
Speed is the distance travelled per unit time and is a scalar quantity because it has magnitude only.

The SI unit of speed is metre per second (m s⁻¹), while road speed limits and vehicle speedometers commonly display speed in kilometres per hour (km h⁻¹).

To convert from m s⁻¹ to km h⁻¹, multiply the value by 3.6.

This conversion factor comes from the relationships:

• 1 km = 1000 m
• 1 h = 3600 s

Therefore:

1 m s⁻¹ = (3600 ÷ 1000) km h⁻¹ = 3.6 km h⁻¹.

Hence,

Speed (km h⁻¹) = Speed (m s⁻¹) × 3.6.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantity.

Speed = 25 m s⁻¹.

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Convert the speed to km h⁻¹.

Step 3: Apply the conversion formula.

Speed (km h⁻¹) = 25 × 3.6.

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

25 × 3.6 = 90.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

90 km h⁻¹ corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (72 km h⁻¹) is incorrect because it results from using an incorrect conversion factor.
Option B (81 km h⁻¹) is incorrect because the multiplication has been carried out wrongly.
Option D (100 km h⁻¹) is incorrect because it overestimates the converted speed and does not follow the correct conversion rule.
explanation.exam_tip:
Memorise these two important speed conversions:

• m s⁻¹ → km h⁻¹ : Multiply by 3.6.
• km h⁻¹ → m s⁻¹ : Divide by 3.6.

JAMB frequently tests these conversions either directly or within longer calculation problems.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine looking at a car's speed on a scientific instrument, which reads 25 m s⁻¹, but the highway speed limit is written in km h⁻¹. To compare the two, simply multiply 25 by 3.6.

25 × 3.6 = 90.

So the car is travelling at 90 km h⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed for a complete lesson on the definition of speed, SI units, unit conversions, average speed calculations and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice and detailed explanations.

question: An airport shuttle bus travels at a constant speed of 126 km h⁻¹ while transporting passengers between two terminals. What is this speed in m s⁻¹?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to convert a speed given in kilometres per hour (km h⁻¹) into metres per second (m s⁻¹), which is the SI unit of speed.
explanation.principle:
Speed is the distance travelled per unit time.

The SI unit of speed is metres per second (m s⁻¹), whereas road and transport speeds are often expressed in kilometres per hour (km h⁻¹).

To convert from km h⁻¹ to m s⁻¹, use the relationship:

1 km = 1000 m
1 h = 3600 s

Therefore,

1 km h⁻¹ = (1000/3600) m s⁻¹ = 5/18 m s⁻¹.

Hence,

Speed (m s⁻¹) = Speed (km h⁻¹) × 5/18.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantity.

Speed = 126 km h⁻¹.

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Convert the speed to m s⁻¹.

Step 3: Apply the conversion formula.

Speed = 126 × 5/18.

Step 4: Simplify the calculation.

126 ÷ 18 = 7

7 × 5 = 35.

Step 5: State the answer.

The shuttle bus travels at 35.0 m s⁻¹.

Therefore, the correct option is B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (31.5 m s⁻¹) results from an incorrect conversion factor or arithmetic error.
Option C (38.5 m s⁻¹) is obtained by using the conversion formula incorrectly.
Option D (45.4 m s⁻¹) is much greater than the correct converted value and reflects a calculation mistake.
explanation.exam_tip:
For JAMB, remember these two common conversions:

• km h⁻¹ → m s⁻¹ : multiply by 5/18.
• m s⁻¹ → km h⁻¹ : multiply by 18/5.

These conversion factors appear frequently in speed and motion questions.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine the shuttle's speed is written on a road sign as 126 km h⁻¹. A physics calculation, however, uses metres and seconds. Multiplying by 5/18 changes the unit to m s⁻¹, giving 35.0 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed → Unit Conversion to learn how to convert between km h⁻¹ and m s⁻¹, with worked examples and JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided lessons and revision exercises.

question:
A delivery truck transports goods from a distribution centre to two different towns. It travels 360 km in 4 hours during the first stage of the journey and then covers another 240 km in 5 hours because of heavy traffic.

What is the average speed of the truck for the entire journey?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the average speed of an object that completes a journey in two stages with different travelling times.
explanation.principle:
Average speed describes how fast an object moves over an entire journey.

It is defined as the total distance travelled divided by the total time taken.

Average speed is a scalar quantity because it has magnitude only and no direction.

The formula is:

Average Speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time

where:
• Total Distance is the sum of all distances covered.
• Total Time is the sum of all time intervals spent travelling.

A common mistake is to average the two stage speeds directly. This is incorrect unless both stages take exactly the same conditions in a way that justifies it. Always use the total distance and the total time.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

First stage distance = 360 km
First stage time = 4 h

Second stage distance = 240 km
Second stage time = 5 h

Step 2: Calculate the total distance travelled.

Total distance = 360 + 240 = 600 km

Step 3: Calculate the total time taken.

Total time = 4 + 5 = 9 h

Step 4: Apply the average speed formula.

Average speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time

= 600 ÷ 9

= 66.7 km h⁻¹ (to one decimal place).

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

66.7 km h⁻¹ corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (60 km h⁻¹) is obtained from an incorrect calculation of the total distance or total time.
Option C (75 km h⁻¹) is the arithmetic mean of the two stage speeds (90 km h⁻¹ and 48 km h⁻¹ is not 75, but students often average incorrectly instead of using total distance and total time).
Option D (90 km h⁻¹) is only the speed during the first stage and ignores the slower second stage.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a journey has two or more stages, never average the individual speeds directly. JAMB expects you to first determine the total distance travelled and the total time taken before applying the average speed formula.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine checking how fast the truck travelled for the whole trip rather than for each part separately. Add all the distances together, add all the times together, then divide the total distance by the total time.

600 km ÷ 9 h = 66.7 km h⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed for a detailed explanation of average speed, multi-stage journeys, common mistakes, worked examples and additional JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive revision and exam-focused exercises.

question:
During an athletics training session, a coach reminds the athletes that speed and velocity are not the same physical quantity.

Which of the following correctly describes speed?

options.A: A scalar quantity that describes the distance travelled per unit time
options.B: A vector quantity that describes the displacement covered per unit time
options.C: A scalar quantity that describes the displacement covered per unit time
options.D: A vector quantity that describes the distance travelled per unit time

answer_text: A scalar quantity that describes the distance travelled per unit time

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the nature of speed. Specifically, it asks whether speed is a scalar or a vector quantity and what physical quantity it relates to.
explanation.principle:
Speed is one of the fundamental quantities used to describe motion.

A beginner may ask, 'What is speed?'

Speed is the rate at which an object covers distance. It tells us how fast an object moves without indicating the direction of motion.

Mathematically,

Speed = Distance ÷ Time

where:
• Distance is the total path travelled.
• Time is the duration of the motion.

Another beginner may ask, 'Why is speed called a scalar quantity?'

A scalar quantity has magnitude only and does not require a direction for its complete description. Knowing that a runner moves at 8 m s⁻¹ tells us how fast the runner is moving but not the direction.

Velocity, on the other hand, is a vector quantity because it combines both magnitude and direction and is based on displacement rather than distance.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the quantity being described.

The question asks for the correct description of speed.

Step 2: Recall the defining characteristics of speed.

Speed is based on the total distance travelled per unit time and has magnitude only.

Step 3: Examine the options.

Option A correctly states that speed is a scalar quantity based on distance travelled per unit time.

Options B and D incorrectly describe speed as a vector quantity.

Option C is incorrect because displacement is associated with velocity, not speed.

Step 4: Select the correct answer.

Therefore, the correct option is A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because displacement per unit time defines velocity, which is a vector quantity.
Option C is incorrect because speed is calculated using distance, not displacement, even though it is correctly identified as a scalar quantity.
Option D is incorrect because speed has no direction and is therefore not a vector quantity.
common_misconceptions: Many students mistakenly think speed and velocity mean exactly the same thing.
Some learners confuse distance with displacement when defining speed.
Others incorrectly believe that every quantity describing motion must have a direction.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB asks you to distinguish between speed and velocity, remember:

• Speed → Scalar → Distance ÷ Time.
• Velocity → Vector → Displacement ÷ Time.

A quick memory aid is:
'S' in Speed stands for Scalar, while 'V' in Velocity stands for Vector.
explanation.final_answer: A. A scalar quantity that describes the distance travelled per unit time
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two athletes running around a circular track.

If you only know that one athlete runs at 7 m s⁻¹, you know how fast the athlete is moving, but you do not know the direction of motion. Since no direction is involved, speed is a scalar quantity.

This is why Option A is correct.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed to learn the meaning of speed, the difference between speed and velocity, the speed formula, SI units, worked examples, and authentic JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed concept explanations and revision exercises.

question: A packaging factory uses a conveyor belt to transport sealed cartons from the filling section to the sealing section. The cartons move at a constant speed of 1.8 m s⁻¹. If the two sections are 27 m apart, how long does it take a carton to travel from the filling section to the sealing section?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to apply the relationship between speed, distance and time in a practical manufacturing process involving a conveyor belt.
explanation.principle:
Speed describes how fast an object moves and is defined as the distance travelled per unit time.

It is a scalar quantity because it has magnitude only and no direction.

The mathematical relationship is:

Speed = Distance ÷ Time

From this relationship, time can be obtained by rearranging the formula:

Time = Distance ÷ Speed

where:

• Distance is measured in metres (m).
• Speed is measured in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
• Time is measured in seconds (s).

Conveyor belts in manufacturing are often designed to move at nearly constant speed so that products arrive at each stage of production at predictable intervals.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Distance = 27 m
Speed = 1.8 m s⁻¹

Step 2: Identify the quantity required.

Time taken by a carton to travel between the two sections.

Step 3: Apply the appropriate formula.

Time = Distance ÷ Speed

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

Time = 27 ÷ 1.8

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

Time = 15 s

Step 6: Compare the result with the options.

The correct option is B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (12 s) results from an incorrect calculation of the division.
Option C (18 s) may be obtained by using an incorrect formula or arithmetic error.
Option D (49 s) is far greater than the correct value and does not agree with the given speed and distance.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question asks for time, first check whether distance and speed have been provided. If they are, use:

Time = Distance ÷ Speed.

Also ensure that the units are consistent before substituting into the formula.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine standing beside a moving conveyor belt in a factory. If the belt moves 1.8 metres every second and the next machine is 27 metres away, the carton simply needs:

27 ÷ 1.8 = 15 seconds

to reach the next stage of production.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed to learn how to calculate speed, distance and time, solve practical manufacturing and transportation problems, and practise additional JAMB-style questions with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
Records from an inland water ferry show that it travelled 936 m across a river channel in 24 s while maintaining a steady motion.

What was the average speed of the ferry during the journey?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the average speed of an inland water ferry from the distance it travelled and the time taken for the journey.
explanation.principle:
Speed describes how fast an object moves. It is defined as the distance travelled per unit time.

A beginner may ask, 'What is distance?'

Distance is the total length of the path covered by a moving object, regardless of direction. It is a scalar quantity because it has magnitude only.

A beginner may also ask, 'What is average speed?'

Average speed is the total distance travelled divided by the total time taken.

The formula is:

Speed = Distance ÷ Time

where:
• Speed is measured in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
• Distance is measured in metres (m).
• Time is measured in seconds (s).

This formula applies whenever the total distance travelled and the corresponding time are known.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Distance travelled = 936 m
Time taken = 24 s

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

Average Speed = Distance ÷ Time

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

Average Speed = 936 ÷ 24

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

936 ÷ 24 = 39

Step 5: Attach the correct SI unit.

Average Speed = 39 m s⁻¹

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (22 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect division of the distance by the time.
Option B (34 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because the calculation does not agree with the speed formula.
Option D (48 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it overestimates the speed and would correspond to travelling a greater distance in the same time.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question asks for average speed, first identify the total distance travelled and the total time taken. Then apply:

Speed = Distance ÷ Time

Always ensure the units are consistent before performing the calculation. If distance is in metres and time is in seconds, the answer should be in m s⁻¹.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine watching a ferry move from one river terminal to another. If it covers 936 m in 24 s, you can find how fast it travelled by dividing the distance by the time.

936 ÷ 24 = 39.

So, the ferry's average speed is 39 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed to learn how to calculate speed, determine distance or time from the speed formula, solve average-speed problems, and practise additional JAMB-style questions. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for step-by-step lessons and exam-focused practice.

question:
A laboratory technician investigates the motion of a dynamics trolley moving along a straight horizontal track. The total distance travelled by the trolley is recorded at different times as shown below.

| Time (s) | Distance (m) |
|----------|--------------|
| 0        | 0            |
| 4        | 12           |
| 8        | 24           |
| 12       | 36           |
| 16       | 48           |

Based on the data, which conclusion is most appropriate about the motion of the trolley?

options.A: The trolley moves with a constant speed of 3 m s⁻¹.
options.B: The trolley accelerates uniformly throughout the experiment.
options.C: The trolley's speed decreases steadily with time.
options.D: The trolley is at rest during the first 4 seconds.

answer_text: The trolley moves with a constant speed of 3 m s⁻¹.

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to analyse a table of experimental data to determine whether the trolley's speed changes or remains constant during its motion.
explanation.principle:
Speed is the distance travelled per unit time.

It is calculated using:

Speed = Distance ÷ Time

where:

• Speed is measured in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
• Distance is measured in metres (m).
• Time is measured in seconds (s).

When equal distances are covered in equal time intervals, the object is moving with constant (uniform) speed. A table of distance against time can therefore be used to determine the nature of an object's motion without the need for a graph.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the first interval.

From 0 s to 4 s:
Distance travelled = 12 m

Speed = 12 ÷ 4 = 3 m s⁻¹.

Step 2: Examine the second interval.

From 4 s to 8 s:
Additional distance = 24 − 12 = 12 m
Time taken = 4 s

Speed = 12 ÷ 4 = 3 m s⁻¹.

Step 3: Examine the remaining intervals.

From 8 s to 12 s:
36 − 24 = 12 m in 4 s
Speed = 3 m s⁻¹.

From 12 s to 16 s:
48 − 36 = 12 m in 4 s
Speed = 3 m s⁻¹.

Step 4: Interpret the result.

Since the trolley covers the same distance (12 m) during every equal time interval (4 s), its speed remains constant throughout the experiment.

Therefore, the motion is uniform, and the correct conclusion is that the trolley moves with a constant speed of 3 m s⁻¹.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the data do not show increasing speed. Uniform acceleration would produce progressively larger distances in equal time intervals.
Option C is incorrect because the speed remains the same in every interval; it does not decrease.
Option D is incorrect because the trolley covers 12 m during the first 4 s, showing that it is moving rather than at rest.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB presents a distance-time table, compare the distances covered during equal time intervals.

• Equal distances in equal times → constant (uniform) speed.
• Increasing distances in equal times → increasing speed.
• Decreasing distances in equal times → decreasing speed.

Do not simply look at the total distance; examine what happens in each interval.
explanation.final_answer: A. The trolley moves with a constant speed of 3 m s⁻¹.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine pushing a toy car so that it travels exactly 12 metres every 4 seconds. Since it covers the same distance in the same amount of time repeatedly, it is moving at a constant speed of 3 m s⁻¹. That is exactly what the table shows.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed for a complete lesson on speed, distance-time tables, uniform motion, experimental data interpretation and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice and beginner-friendly explanations.

question:
An inspection robot travels through an oil pipeline to examine its inner surface. During one inspection run, it covers a distance of 736 m in 23 s at a nearly constant rate.

What is the speed of the inspection robot?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the speed of an object from the distance it travels and the time taken. The practical context is an inspection robot moving through an oil pipeline.
explanation.principle:
Speed describes how fast an object moves. It is defined as the distance travelled per unit time.

Before using the formula, it is important to understand the quantities involved.

• Distance is the total length of the path travelled by the object.
• Time is the duration taken to cover that distance.
• Speed is a scalar quantity, meaning it has magnitude only and no direction.

The relationship between these quantities is:

Speed = Distance ÷ Time

Symbolically,

v = d/t

where:

• v = speed (m s⁻¹)
• d = distance (m)
• t = time (s)

Since the distance is given in metres and the time in seconds, the answer will naturally be in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Distance, d = 736 m

Time, t = 23 s

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

Speed = Distance ÷ Time

v = d/t

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

v = 736/23

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

v = 32 m s⁻¹

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options provided.

The correct option is C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (28 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect division of the distance by the time.
Option B (30 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it underestimates the calculated speed and does not satisfy v = d/t.
Option D (34 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it overestimates the speed obtained from the given data.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question gives distance and time, think immediately of the speed formula:

Speed = Distance ÷ Time.

Always check that the units are consistent before calculating. If distance is in metres and time is in seconds, the answer should be in m s⁻¹.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine the inspection robot moving steadily inside a long pipeline. To know how fast it moves, simply divide the total distance travelled by the time taken.

736 ÷ 23 = 32.

Therefore, the robot's speed is 32 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed for a complete lesson on calculating speed, determining distance and time, SI units, average speed, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice and beginner-friendly explanations.

question: A combine harvester moves across a rectangular farmland at a constant speed of 3.5 m s⁻¹ while harvesting crops. If it operates continuously for 240 s without stopping or changing speed, what total distance does it cover?

explanation.question_restate: This question applies the concept of speed to an agricultural operation. You are required to determine the total distance travelled by a combine harvester moving at a constant speed over a given time.
explanation.principle:
Speed describes how fast an object moves and is defined as the distance travelled per unit time.

It is a scalar quantity because it has magnitude only and no direction.

The mathematical relationship is:

Speed = Distance ÷ Time

Rearranging the formula to find distance gives:

Distance = Speed × Time

where:

• Distance is measured in metres (m).
• Speed is measured in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
• Time is measured in seconds (s).

This formula is appropriate whenever an object moves at a constant speed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Speed = 3.5 m s⁻¹

Time = 240 s

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Distance travelled.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

Distance = Speed × Time

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

Distance = 3.5 × 240

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

Distance = 840 m

Step 6: Compare your result with the options provided.

840 m corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (700 m) results from an incorrect multiplication or premature rounding.
Option B (770 m) is obtained from using an incorrect value for either the speed or the time.
Option D (980 m) overestimates the distance and does not satisfy the relationship Distance = Speed × Time for the given data.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever speed and time are given, first determine what the question requires.

• To find distance, multiply speed by time.
• To find speed, divide distance by time.
• To find time, divide distance by speed.

Always ensure that the units are compatible before performing calculations.
explanation.simple_explanation:
The harvester moves 3.5 metres every second. In 240 seconds, it covers:

3.5 × 240 = 840 metres.

Therefore, the correct answer is 840 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed to learn how to calculate speed, distance and time, solve practical JAMB-style questions, and apply speed concepts in transportation, agriculture, manufacturing and other real-life situations. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided practice and revision.

question:
During a flood rescue operation, a fire service vehicle travelled 42 km from the fire station to an affected community in 35 minutes. A trainee concluded that because the vehicle moved very fast, its speed alone was sufficient to describe the journey.

Which statement best interprets the situation?

options.A: The speed indicates how fast the vehicle travelled but does not specify the direction of motion.
options.B: The speed completely describes the vehicle's motion because it includes both magnitude and direction.
options.C: The speed is equal to the vehicle's displacement divided by the time taken.
options.D: The speed must be zero because the vehicle eventually stopped at the rescue location.

answer_text: The speed indicates how fast the vehicle travelled but does not specify the direction of motion.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of what speed represents in a practical rescue operation. It tests whether you can distinguish speed from velocity rather than perform a numerical calculation.
explanation.principle:
Speed is a scalar quantity that tells us how fast an object moves. It is defined as the distance travelled per unit time.

A scalar quantity has magnitude only.

Velocity, on the other hand, is a vector quantity because it describes both the magnitude of motion and its direction.

Although the vehicle covered 42 km in 35 minutes, the information about speed only indicates how rapidly it travelled. Without stating the direction of travel, the motion is not completely described.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the physical quantity under discussion.

The question focuses on speed, not velocity.

Step 2: Recall the definition of speed.

Speed is the distance travelled per unit time and has magnitude only.

Step 3: Evaluate the trainee's conclusion.

The trainee is incorrect because speed alone does not include direction, which is required to fully describe motion.

Step 4: Examine the options.

Option A correctly states that speed tells how fast the vehicle travelled but gives no information about direction.

Therefore, Option A is the correct interpretation.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because speed does not include direction. A quantity that includes both magnitude and direction is velocity.
Option C is incorrect because displacement divided by time gives average velocity, not speed. Speed is calculated using distance travelled divided by time.
Option D is incorrect because stopping at the destination does not mean the vehicle's speed throughout the journey was zero. It merely indicates that its final speed became zero after the trip.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB asks about speed and velocity, remember this simple distinction:

• Speed = distance ÷ time (scalar, no direction).
• Velocity = displacement ÷ time (vector, includes direction).

If a statement mentions 'how fast' without referring to direction, it is describing speed.
explanation.final_answer: A. The speed indicates how fast the vehicle travelled but does not specify the direction of motion.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine telling your friend that a rescue vehicle travelled at a high speed. Your friend knows how fast it moved but still does not know whether it travelled north, south, east or west.

That is why speed alone cannot completely describe motion. Direction must be included before the quantity becomes velocity.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed to learn the meaning of speed, its SI unit, the difference between speed and velocity, average speed calculations, and practical JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided practice and concept-based revision.

question:
A mining haul truck transports ore from a loading point to a processing plant. During one trip, it travels 252 km in 4 h and then covers another 168 km in 3 h after slowing down because of rough terrain.

What is the average speed of the haul truck for the entire journey?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the average speed of a mining haul truck that completes a journey in two stages with different distances and travel times.
explanation.principle:
Speed describes how fast an object moves. It is a scalar quantity because it has magnitude only and no direction.

When an object travels in more than one stage, the average speed is obtained by dividing the total distance travelled by the total time taken.

The formula is:

Average Speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time

where:
• Total Distance is the sum of all distances travelled.
• Total Time is the sum of all the times taken.

A common mistake is to calculate the arithmetic mean of the individual speeds. This is only valid in special situations and should not be used unless the travel times or distances satisfy the required conditions.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

First stage:
Distance = 252 km
Time = 4 h

Second stage:
Distance = 168 km
Time = 3 h.

Step 2: Calculate the total distance.

Total Distance = 252 km + 168 km = 420 km.

Step 3: Calculate the total time.

Total Time = 4 h + 3 h = 7 h.

Step 4: Apply the average speed formula.

Average Speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time
= 420 km ÷ 7 h
= 60 km h⁻¹.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

The calculated value is 60 km h⁻¹, which corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (54 km h⁻¹) results from an incorrect computation of either the total distance or the total time.
Option C (63 km h⁻¹) may be obtained by incorrectly averaging the two stage speeds or making an arithmetic error.
Option D (70 km h⁻¹) is obtained by dividing the total distance by only the first stage time instead of the total journey time.
explanation.exam_tip: For journeys completed in several stages, always add all the distances together and all the times together before applying the average speed formula. Never average individual speeds directly unless the question specifically justifies doing so.
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of the journey as one complete trip instead of two separate parts. First, find the total distance travelled. Next, find the total time taken. Finally, divide the total distance by the total time to obtain the average speed.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed for a complete lesson on speed, average speed, multi-stage journeys, SI units, worked examples and authentic JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided revision.

question:
A campus electric shuttle transports students between lecture halls. During one trip, it travels 260 m in 20 s and then continues for another 390 m in 30 s.

What is the average speed of the shuttle for the entire journey?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the average speed of an object that travels unequal distances during different time intervals. To obtain the correct answer, you must use the total distance travelled and the total time taken for the complete journey.
explanation.principle:
Speed is a scalar quantity that describes how fast an object moves. It is defined as the distance travelled per unit time.

For journeys made up of more than one stage, the average speed is **not** obtained by averaging the speeds of the individual stages unless the time intervals are identical.

Instead, average speed is calculated using:

Average speed = Total distance travelled ÷ Total time taken

where:

• Total distance is the sum of all distances covered.
• Total time is the sum of the corresponding time intervals.

This method works regardless of whether the distances or times are equal.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the given values.

First distance = 260 m

First time = 20 s

Second distance = 390 m

Second time = 30 s.

Step 2: Determine the total distance travelled.

Total distance = 260 + 390 = 650 m.

Step 3: Determine the total time taken.

Total time = 20 + 30 = 50 s.

Step 4: Apply the average speed formula.

Average speed = Total distance ÷ Total time

= 650 ÷ 50

= 13 m s⁻¹.

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

13 m s⁻¹ corresponds to Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (10 m s⁻¹) is incorrect. It results from using an incorrect distance or time in the calculation instead of the totals.
Option B (11 m s⁻¹) is incorrect. It reflects an arithmetic error during division or an incorrect combination of the journey data.
Option D (15 m s⁻¹) is incorrect. It overestimates the average speed because the total distance and total time were not correctly combined.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a journey has two or more stages, first add all the distances together and then add all the times together before dividing. Do not simply average the speeds of the separate stages unless specifically instructed and the conditions justify it.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of the shuttle making one complete trip instead of two separate ones.

It travelled a total of 650 m in a total of 50 s.

Since speed tells us how much distance is covered each second,

650 ÷ 50 = 13.

So the shuttle's average speed for the whole journey is 13 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed for a complete lesson on average speed, multi-stage journeys, unequal-distance problems, common examination mistakes, and additional JAMB-style worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for guided practice and concept mastery.

question:
An automated warehouse uses robotic carts to transport packages between storage shelves and loading bays. During a safety briefing, the supervisor makes the following statements about speed:

I. Speed describes how fast an object moves regardless of its direction.

II. Two robotic carts travelling at the same speed but in different directions have different speeds.

III. The SI unit of speed is metre per second (m s⁻¹).

IV. Average speed is obtained by dividing the total distance travelled by the total time taken.

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to evaluate statements about speed by identifying those that correctly describe its meaning, unit and method of calculation.
explanation.principle:
Speed is a scalar quantity that indicates how fast an object moves. A scalar quantity has magnitude only and does not include direction.

Speed is defined as the distance travelled per unit time.

Mathematically,

Speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time

The SI unit of speed is metre per second (m s⁻¹).

Because speed has no direction, two objects moving at the same rate but in different directions still have the same speed. Direction only becomes important when discussing velocity, which is a vector quantity.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

'Speed describes how fast an object moves regardless of its direction.'

This is correct because speed depends only on the magnitude of motion and not on direction.

Step 2: Examine Statement II.

'Two robotic carts travelling at the same speed but in different directions have different speeds.'

This is incorrect.

Their directions are different, but if they move equally fast, their speeds are the same. It is their velocities that differ.

Step 3: Examine Statement III.

'The SI unit of speed is metre per second (m s⁻¹).'

This is correct.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

'Average speed is obtained by dividing the total distance travelled by the total time taken.'

This is the correct definition of average speed.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Statements I, III and IV are correct, while Statement II is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Statement II, which wrongly assumes that direction changes speed. Direction changes velocity, not speed.
Option C is incorrect because Statement II is false, while Statements I and III, which are correct, have been omitted.
Option D is incorrect because Statement II is incorrect. Including one false statement makes the entire combination incorrect.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB frequently tests the distinction between speed and velocity.

Remember these key facts:

• Speed is a scalar quantity.
• Velocity is a vector quantity.
• Speed depends on distance.
• Velocity depends on displacement.
• Average speed = Total distance ÷ Total time.
• The SI unit of speed is m s⁻¹.

Whenever a question introduces direction, first determine whether it is referring to speed or velocity before choosing your answer.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, III and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two warehouse robots moving at exactly the same rate. One moves north while the other moves south.

Although they travel in different directions, they are moving equally fast, so they have the same speed.

Direction does not affect speed; it affects velocity. Therefore, Statements I, III and IV are correct, while Statement II is incorrect.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Speed for a complete lesson on the meaning of speed, scalar quantities, average speed, SI units, unit conversions, worked examples and JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive revision and exam-focused exercises.

question:
A high-speed passenger train travels 540 m due north in 18 s along a straight railway track.

What is the average velocity of the train?

explanation.question_restate:
This question tests your ability to calculate the average velocity of an object moving in a straight line with a specified direction.

Unlike speed, velocity requires both magnitude and direction.
explanation.principle:
Velocity is the rate at which displacement changes with time.

Displacement is the shortest straight-line distance from the starting point to the finishing point together with the direction of motion.

Velocity is therefore a vector quantity because it possesses both magnitude and direction.

The average velocity of an object is calculated using:

Average velocity = Displacement ÷ Time

where:

• Displacement is measured in metres (m).
• Time is measured in seconds (s).
• Velocity is expressed in metres per second (m s⁻¹).

Whenever a direction such as north, south, east or west is given, it must be included in the final answer because direction is an essential part of velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Displacement = 540 m due north

Time = 18 s

Step 2: State the appropriate formula.

Average velocity = Displacement ÷ Time

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

Average velocity = 540 ÷ 18

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

540 ÷ 18 = 30

Step 5: Attach the correct SI unit and direction.

Average velocity = 30 m s⁻¹ due north.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it gives only the magnitude. Velocity must include both magnitude and direction.
Option C is incorrect because the calculated value is not 28 m s⁻¹.
Option D is incorrect because the displacement divided by the time does not give 32 m s⁻¹.
explanation.exam_tip:
A common JAMB mistake is to calculate the numerical value correctly but omit the direction.

Remember:

• Speed = Distance ÷ Time (scalar).
• Velocity = Displacement ÷ Time (vector).

Whenever a question asks for velocity, always check whether the direction should be included.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine travelling from one station directly to another without changing direction.

If you cover 540 m towards the north in 18 seconds, you move 30 metres every second in that same direction.

Because the question asks for velocity, the direction 'due north' must be included.

Hence, the average velocity is 30 m s⁻¹ due north.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Velocity to learn the meaning of velocity, the difference between speed and velocity, average velocity calculations, displacement, direction in motion, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional exam-focused practice and concept reinforcement.

question:
An autonomous delivery robot moves due east at a constant velocity of 16 m s⁻¹ for 55 s while transporting packages within a smart warehouse.

What is the robot's displacement at the end of the motion?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate displacement when the velocity and time of motion are known. Since velocity already includes direction, the final displacement must also include the correct direction.
explanation.principle:
Velocity is defined as the rate of change of displacement with time.

Unlike speed, velocity is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.

Displacement refers to the straight-line change in position from the starting point to the ending point, together with its direction.

When an object moves with constant velocity in a straight line, its displacement is obtained using:

Displacement = Velocity × Time

or

s = vt

where:

• s = displacement (m)
• v = velocity (m s⁻¹)
• t = time (s)
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known quantities.

Velocity, v = 16 m s⁻¹ east

Time, t = 55 s

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Displacement, s

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

s = vt

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

s = 16 × 55

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

s = 880 m

Step 6: Include the direction.

Since the robot travelled due east throughout the journey, its displacement is 880 m east.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (871 m east) is incorrect because it results from an arithmetic error during multiplication.
Option C (39 m east) is incorrect because it is obtained by wrongly subtracting or manipulating the given values instead of multiplying velocity by time.
Option D (880 m) has the correct magnitude but omits the direction. Since displacement is a vector quantity, its direction must be stated.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB asks for displacement using velocity and time, remember that velocity already contains direction.

First calculate the magnitude using s = vt, then attach the correct direction.

Do not confuse displacement with distance, and never omit the direction when giving a displacement.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine the robot moving along a straight warehouse aisle without turning.

If it moves 16 metres every second for 55 seconds, it covers:

16 × 55 = 880 metres.

Because it always moves east, its displacement is 880 m east.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Velocity to learn how velocity differs from speed, how to calculate displacement, determine time from velocity, interpret direction in motion, and solve additional JAMB-style questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive concept reviews and worked examples.

question:
During a coastguard rescue mission, a rescue boat travels 960 m due west at a constant velocity of 12 m s⁻¹.

How long does the boat take to reach the stranded vessel?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the time taken for a rescue boat to cover a known displacement when its constant velocity is given.
explanation.principle:
Velocity is the rate at which displacement changes with time.

Unlike speed, velocity has both magnitude and direction. In calculations involving time, the direction indicates the vector nature of the motion but does not affect the numerical value of the time.

The relationship between displacement, velocity and time is:

Velocity = Displacement ÷ Time

where:

• Velocity (v) is measured in metres per second (m s⁻¹).
• Displacement (s) is measured in metres (m).
• Time (t) is measured in seconds (s).

Rearranging the formula to make time the subject gives:

Time = Displacement ÷ Velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Displacement, s = 960 m west

Velocity, v = 12 m s⁻¹

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Time, t

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

Time = Displacement ÷ Velocity

Step 4: Substitute the values.

t = 960 ÷ 12

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

960 ÷ 12 = 80

Therefore, the rescue boat takes 80 s to reach the stranded vessel.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (60 s) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect calculation of the displacement divided by the velocity.
Option B (72 s) is incorrect because it does not satisfy the relationship between displacement, velocity and time.
Option D (92 s) is incorrect because it is obtained from an arithmetic error. Using this time with the given velocity would produce a displacement greater than 960 m.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever you are asked to calculate time in linear motion, first identify the known quantities and then rearrange the velocity formula correctly.

Remember:

Time = Displacement ÷ Velocity

Always check that the units are compatible before substituting values.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine the rescue boat moving steadily towards the stranded vessel without changing its speed.

If it covers 12 m every second, then the number of seconds required to cover 960 m is:

960 ÷ 12 = 80.

So, the journey takes 80 seconds.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Velocity to learn how velocity, displacement and time are related, including worked JAMB-style examples on finding unknown quantities using the velocity equation. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided practice and examination tips.

question:
During a 100 m athletics training session, a coach records the motion of four sprinters as follows:

Sprinter I: Runs at 8.5 m s⁻¹.

Sprinter II: Runs at 8.5 m s⁻¹ due east.

Sprinter III: Covers 100 m in 12 s.

Sprinter IV: Runs faster than the others.

Which sprinter's motion is described in terms of velocity?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of velocity by asking you to identify which description includes both the magnitude of motion and its direction.
explanation.principle:
Velocity is the rate of change of displacement with time. It is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.

A beginner may ask, 'What makes velocity different from speed?'

Speed tells us only how fast an object moves.

Velocity tells us both how fast the object moves and the direction in which it moves.

Therefore, any complete description of velocity must include a numerical value (or magnitude) together with a specified direction such as north, south, east or west.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Sprinter I.

'Runs at 8.5 m s⁻¹' gives only the magnitude of motion. No direction is stated, so this describes speed, not velocity.

Step 2: Examine Sprinter II.
