Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and exam-focused revision.

question:
A campus shuttle operates along a straight road between two terminals without changing its speed or direction. During routine monitoring, it is observed that the shuttle covers 180 m in the first 15 s, another 180 m in the next 15 s, and the same distance again in the following 15 s.

What does this observation indicate about the shuttle's motion?

options.A: The shuttle is moving with uniform motion because it covers equal distances in equal time intervals.
options.B: The shuttle is accelerating because it continues to move throughout the journey.
options.C: The shuttle is slowing down because the distance covered remains unchanged.
options.D: The shuttle is moving with non-uniform motion because its position changes continuously.

answer_text: The shuttle is moving with uniform motion because it covers equal distances in equal time intervals.

explanation.question_restate:
This question tests your ability to recognise one of the defining characteristics of uniform motion from a real-life situation rather than by performing calculations.

The shuttle covers the same distance of 180 m during each successive 15-second interval. You are required to identify what this pattern tells you about its motion.
explanation.principle:
Uniform motion is motion in which an object moves with constant velocity.

For motion to be uniform, both the magnitude of the velocity (speed) and its direction must remain unchanged.

One important characteristic of uniform motion is that the object covers equal distances in equal intervals of time.

Since the shuttle travels along a straight road without changing speed or direction, its velocity remains constant. Consequently, its acceleration is zero because there is no change in velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the distances travelled.

The shuttle covers 180 m during each 15-second interval.

The distances are equal.

Step 2: Examine the time intervals.

Each distance is covered in the same time of 15 s.

The time intervals are also equal.

Step 3: Apply the defining characteristic of uniform motion.

When equal distances are covered in equal intervals of time, the object is moving with constant velocity (provided the direction remains unchanged).

The question states that the shuttle does not change its speed or direction.

Therefore, the shuttle is in uniform motion.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Option A correctly describes the observed motion.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because an object does not accelerate simply because it is moving. Acceleration occurs only when velocity changes.
Option C is incorrect because covering equal distances in equal times indicates constant speed, not slowing down.
Option D is incorrect because a change in position occurs in every type of motion. Non-uniform motion specifically involves a change in speed, direction or both, which is not observed here.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a JAMB question mentions that equal distances are covered in equal time intervals, immediately think of uniform motion.

Also remember:

• Uniform motion means constant velocity.
• Constant velocity means constant speed in an unchanged direction.
• Zero acceleration does not mean the object has stopped moving; it simply means its velocity is not changing.
explanation.final_answer: A. The shuttle is moving with uniform motion because it covers equal distances in equal time intervals.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine travelling in a shuttle that moves steadily along a straight road. If it covers exactly the same distance every 15 seconds without speeding up, slowing down or turning, its motion is uniform.

That is exactly what happens in this question, so the correct answer is Option A.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Uniform Motion to learn the characteristics of uniform motion, constant velocity, zero acceleration, distance-time relationships, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional concept reviews and examination-focused practice.

question:
An engineering team monitored an agricultural irrigation vehicle moving along a straight farm lane.

The distance-time graph recorded for the vehicle is described as follows:

• The graph starts at the origin.
• It is a straight line throughout the journey.
• The distance increases by 45 m every 9 s.

What does this graph indicate about the motion of the irrigation vehicle?

options.A: The vehicle is moving with constant velocity and zero acceleration.
options.B: The vehicle is speeding up because the distance covered keeps increasing.
options.C: The vehicle is slowing down because time is increasing continuously.
options.D: The vehicle is accelerating uniformly since the graph is a straight line.

answer_text: The vehicle is moving with constant velocity and zero acceleration.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret a distance-time graph. Instead of calculating values, you are required to identify the type of motion represented by a straight-line distance-time graph with a constant slope.
explanation.principle:
A distance-time graph shows how the distance travelled by an object changes with time.

For a beginner, you may ask, 'What does the slope of a distance-time graph represent?'

The slope (or gradient) of a distance-time graph represents the speed or velocity of the object when it moves along a straight path without changing direction.

When the graph is a straight line with a constant slope:

• Equal distances are covered in equal intervals of time.
• The speed (and velocity, since the direction is unchanged) remains constant.
• There is no change in velocity.
• Therefore, the acceleration is zero.

In this question, the vehicle covers 45 m every 9 s throughout its journey, showing that its velocity remains constant.
steps:
Step 1: Observe the shape of the graph.

The graph is a straight line from the origin with a constant slope.

Step 2: Interpret the equal distance intervals.

The vehicle covers 45 m every 9 s repeatedly. This means equal distances are covered in equal intervals of time.

Step 3: Determine the nature of the motion.

Since the speed does not change and the vehicle continues along a straight path, its velocity remains constant. Uniform motion therefore occurs.

Step 4: Consider the acceleration.

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity.

Because the velocity remains constant, there is no change in velocity.

Hence, the acceleration is zero.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

The graph represents motion with constant velocity and zero acceleration.

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because increasing distance with time does not necessarily mean the object is speeding up. The constant slope shows the speed remains unchanged.
Option C is incorrect because time always increases during motion. This alone does not indicate that the object is slowing down.
Option D is incorrect because a straight-line distance-time graph represents constant velocity, not uniform acceleration. Uniform acceleration would produce a curved distance-time graph.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB presents a distance-time graph, first examine its slope.

• A straight line with a constant slope indicates uniform motion.
• A steeper straight line represents a higher constant speed.
• A curved distance-time graph indicates changing speed (non-uniform motion).

Do not confuse a straight-line distance-time graph with a velocity-time graph. They convey different information.
explanation.final_answer: A. The vehicle is moving with constant velocity and zero acceleration.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine walking along a straight road and covering exactly the same distance every minute.

Because you keep moving at the same rate without speeding up or slowing down, your motion is uniform.

A straight-line distance-time graph represents this kind of steady motion, so the acceleration is zero.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Uniform Motion to learn how to interpret distance-time and velocity-time graphs, distinguish uniform motion from non-uniform motion, and solve authentic JAMB-style graph interpretation questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed explanations and additional practice.

question:
During an athletics training session, a coach observes an athlete running around a standard oval track. The athlete maintains the same speed throughout the run but continuously changes direction while moving along the curved sections of the track.

Which statement best explains the athlete's motion?

options.A: The athlete is in uniform motion because the speed remains constant throughout the run.
options.B: The athlete is in uniform motion because no external force acts while running.
options.C: The athlete is not in uniform motion because the direction of motion changes even though the speed remains constant.
options.D: The athlete is not moving with constant speed because changing direction automatically changes the speed.

answer_text: The athlete is not in uniform motion because the direction of motion changes even though the speed remains constant.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests whether you can distinguish between constant speed and constant velocity. Although the athlete keeps the same speed, the path followed is curved, causing the direction of motion to change continuously.
explanation.principle:
Uniform motion is motion with constant velocity.

Velocity is a vector quantity. It has both magnitude (speed) and direction.

For motion to be uniform:

• the speed must remain constant,
• the direction must remain constant, and
• the acceleration must be zero.

Many beginners assume that constant speed automatically means uniform motion. This is incorrect. Whenever the direction changes, the velocity changes, even if the speed remains unchanged.

Running around an oval or circular track is a common example. The athlete may maintain the same speed, but because the direction changes at every point on the curve, the velocity is continually changing. Therefore, the motion is not uniform.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what remains unchanged.

The question states that the athlete maintains the same speed throughout the run. Therefore, the magnitude of the velocity remains constant.

Step 2: Determine whether the direction changes.

The athlete runs around an oval track. Along the curved sections, the direction of motion changes continuously.

Step 3: Recall the definition of uniform motion.

Uniform motion requires constant velocity, not merely constant speed. Since velocity depends on both magnitude and direction, a change in direction means the velocity has changed.

Step 4: Evaluate the options.

Option C correctly states that the motion is not uniform because the direction changes despite the speed remaining constant.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because constant speed alone does not guarantee uniform motion. Direction must also remain constant.

Option B is incorrect because an external force is required to continuously change the direction of motion on a curved path. Moreover, the absence or presence of force is not the defining criterion for uniform motion.
Option D is incorrect because changing direction does not necessarily change speed. An object can maintain constant speed while continuously changing direction.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB asks about uniform motion, first ask yourself two questions:

• Is the speed constant?
• Is the direction also constant?

Uniform motion requires both answers to be 'Yes.' If either the speed or the direction changes, the motion is not uniform.

Remember:

• Constant speed along a straight line can be uniform motion.
• Constant speed along a curved or circular path is not uniform motion because the direction changes continuously.
explanation.final_answer: C. The athlete is not in uniform motion because the direction of motion changes even though the speed remains constant.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine driving a car around a roundabout at exactly the same speed.

Even though the speedometer shows the same reading, you keep turning the steering wheel. Every turn changes the direction in which the car moves.

Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, the velocity changes whenever the direction changes.

That is why motion around a curved path is not uniform motion, even if the speed never changes.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Uniform Motion to learn the difference between speed and velocity, the conditions for uniform motion, zero acceleration, distance-time and velocity-time graphs, and why constant speed on a curved path does not represent uniform motion. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional JAMB-style practice questions and concept reviews.

question:
An engineering team monitors an oil pipeline inspection robot moving in a straight pipeline. During a routine inspection, the robot travels 525 m in 35 s without changing either its speed or direction.

What is the constant speed of the robot?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the constant speed of an object undergoing uniform motion from the measured distance travelled and the time taken.
explanation.principle:
Uniform motion occurs when an object moves with constant velocity, meaning both its speed and direction remain unchanged throughout the motion.

In a straight-line journey where the direction does not change, the speed is calculated using:

v = d/t

where:

• v = speed (m s⁻¹)
• d = distance travelled (m)
• t = time taken (s)

This relationship shows that speed tells us how much distance is covered every second.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Distance travelled, d = 525 m

Time taken, t = 35 s

Step 2: Recall the formula.

v = d/t

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

v = 525/35

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

v = 15 m s⁻¹

Step 5: Compare with the options.

The calculated speed is 15 m s⁻¹.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (12 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it underestimates the speed obtained from the given distance and time.
Option C (18 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it would require the robot to cover a greater distance than 525 m in 35 s.
Option D (20 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it overestimates the speed and does not satisfy the relationship between the given distance and time.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question provides only distance travelled and time taken during steady motion, immediately think of the formula:

v = d/t

Always check that the distance is in metres and the time is in seconds so that the answer is obtained directly in m s⁻¹.
explanation.simple_explanation:
If the robot covers 525 metres in 35 seconds while moving steadily, it travels the same distance every second.

Dividing the total distance by the total time gives:

525 ÷ 35 = 15.

So the robot moves at a constant speed of 15 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Uniform Motion for a complete lesson on calculating speed, distance and time in uniform motion, interpreting motion graphs, and solving additional JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed concept reviews and exam-focused exercises.

question:
An engineering team monitoring a metro rail service records the following motion:

'A velocity-time graph consists of a horizontal line at 18 m s⁻¹ extending uniformly from 0 s to 12 s.'

Which conclusion can be drawn correctly from the graph?

options.A: The train accelerates uniformly throughout the 12 s.
options.B: The train moves with constant velocity and zero acceleration.
options.C: The train gradually loses speed while covering equal distances.
options.D: The train remains at rest because its acceleration is zero.

answer_text: The train moves with constant velocity and zero acceleration.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret a velocity-time graph. A horizontal line on such a graph indicates that the object's velocity does not change with time. You are required to identify what this says about the train's motion.
explanation.principle:
A velocity-time graph shows how velocity changes with time.

The slope (gradient) of a velocity-time graph represents acceleration.

When the graph is a horizontal line, its slope is zero. Therefore, the acceleration is zero.

Since the velocity remains unchanged throughout the motion, the object is moving with constant velocity, which is the defining characteristic of uniform motion.

A beginner may ask, 'Can an object move even when its acceleration is zero?'

Yes. Zero acceleration simply means the velocity is not changing. The object may still be moving at a constant velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the graph described.

The graph is a horizontal line at 18 m s⁻¹ from 0 s to 12 s.

This means the train maintains the same velocity throughout the entire interval.

Step 2: Recall the meaning of the slope of a velocity-time graph.

Acceleration equals the slope (gradient) of the graph.

Since the graph is horizontal, its slope is zero.

Step 3: Determine the acceleration.

Acceleration = 0 m s⁻².

Step 4: Interpret the motion.

The train continues moving at a constant velocity of 18 m s⁻¹ while experiencing zero acceleration.

Therefore, it is undergoing uniform motion.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

Option B correctly describes the motion.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because a horizontal velocity-time graph has zero slope, indicating zero acceleration rather than uniform acceleration.
Option C is incorrect because a reduction in speed would appear as a downward-sloping line, not a horizontal one.
Option D is incorrect because zero acceleration does not imply zero velocity. The train is moving steadily at 18 m s⁻¹.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB Physics, remember these key graph interpretations:

• Horizontal distance-time graph → object at rest.
• Straight sloping distance-time graph → constant speed.
• Horizontal velocity-time graph → constant velocity and zero acceleration.
• The slope of a velocity-time graph always represents acceleration.
explanation.final_answer: B. The train moves with constant velocity and zero acceleration.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine a train travelling steadily without speeding up or slowing down. Every second it moves at exactly 18 m s⁻¹.

Because its velocity never changes, its acceleration is zero, even though it is still moving.

That is exactly what a horizontal velocity-time graph represents.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Uniform Motion → Velocity-Time Graphs to learn how to interpret velocity-time graphs, determine acceleration from gradients, calculate displacement from graph areas, and solve authentic JAMB-style questions. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and concept reviews.

question: During the commissioning of an automated packaging line, cartons are transported along a straight conveyor at a constant velocity of 3.8 m s⁻¹. If a carton remains on the conveyor for 40 s before reaching the sealing unit, what distance does it travel?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to apply the concept of uniform motion to an industrial automation system by calculating the distance travelled when an object moves with constant velocity.
explanation.principle:
Uniform motion is motion in which an object moves with constant velocity. This means that both the speed and the direction remain unchanged throughout the motion.

When velocity is constant, equal distances are covered in equal intervals of time and the acceleration is zero.

The relationship connecting distance, velocity and time is:

v = d/t

where:

• v = velocity (m s⁻¹)
• d = distance travelled (m)
• t = time taken (s)

Rearranging the formula to make distance the subject gives:

d = vt
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Velocity, v = 3.8 m s⁻¹

Time, t = 40 s

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Distance travelled, d.

Step 3: Apply the appropriate formula.

 d = vt

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

 d = 3.8 × 40

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

 d = 152 m

Step 6: Compare the result with the options.

152 m corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (112 m) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect multiplication and does not satisfy d = vt.
Option C (168 m) is incorrect because it overestimates the distance travelled for the given velocity and time.
Option D (190 m) is incorrect because it would require either a higher constant velocity or a longer travel time than stated in the question.
explanation.exam_tip:
For uniform motion, always remember the simple relationship:

Distance = Velocity × Time.

Before substituting values, check that the units are consistent. Velocity should be in metres per second (m s⁻¹) and time in seconds (s), so the distance obtained will be in metres (m).
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine the conveyor carries every carton at exactly the same speed without speeding up or slowing down. Since it moves at 3.8 metres every second for 40 seconds, multiply 3.8 by 40.

Distance = 3.8 × 40 = 152 m.

Therefore, the carton travels 152 m before reaching the sealing unit.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Uniform Motion to learn how to calculate speed, distance and time, recognise zero acceleration, interpret motion graphs and solve additional JAMB-style questions on steady motion. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive revision and guided practice.

question:
An engineering team is testing a highway vehicle fitted with a cruise-control system. Once activated, the vehicle travels along a straight section of road without changing either its speed or direction.

Four trainees make the following statements about the vehicle's motion:

I. Since the vehicle is moving, it must have a positive acceleration.

II. The vehicle's acceleration is zero because its velocity remains constant.

III. Zero acceleration means the vehicle has stopped moving.

IV. The vehicle continues to cover equal distances in equal intervals of time.

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question evaluates your understanding of zero acceleration during uniform motion. It requires you to determine which statements correctly describe a vehicle moving with constant velocity on a straight road.
explanation.principle:
Uniform motion occurs when an object moves with constant velocity.

Velocity is a vector quantity, meaning it has both magnitude (speed) and direction.

If neither the speed nor the direction changes, the velocity remains constant.

Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity.

Mathematically,

a = Δv/Δt

where:
• a = acceleration
• Δv = change in velocity
• Δt = time taken for the change.

When there is no change in velocity (Δv = 0), the acceleration is zero.

An object can therefore continue moving at a constant velocity while having zero acceleration. One important characteristic of uniform motion is that equal distances are covered in equal intervals of time.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

'Since the vehicle is moving, it must have a positive acceleration.'

This statement is incorrect.

Motion does not necessarily imply acceleration. An object moving with constant velocity has zero acceleration.

Step 2: Examine Statement II.

'The vehicle's acceleration is zero because its velocity remains constant.'

This statement is correct.

Since neither the speed nor the direction changes, there is no change in velocity.

Step 3: Examine Statement III.

'Zero acceleration means the vehicle has stopped moving.'

This statement is incorrect.

Zero acceleration simply means the velocity is not changing. The object may still be moving at a constant velocity.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

'The vehicle continues to cover equal distances in equal intervals of time.'

This is one of the defining characteristics of uniform motion and is therefore correct.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Only Statements II and IV are correct.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because Statement I is false. A moving object does not always have positive acceleration, and Statement III is also false.
Option C is incorrect because it includes Statement I, which wrongly assumes that motion always requires acceleration.
Option D is incorrect because although Statements II and IV are correct, Statement III is false since zero acceleration does not imply zero velocity.
explanation.exam_tip:
JAMB frequently tests the distinction between velocity and acceleration.

Remember these important facts:

• Constant velocity means zero acceleration.
• Zero acceleration does not mean zero speed.
• Equal distances covered in equal intervals of time indicate uniform motion.
• A moving object requires acceleration only when its velocity changes, either in magnitude or direction.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine setting a car's cruise control on a straight expressway.

If the car keeps the same speed and continues in the same direction, its velocity remains constant. Since the velocity does not change, the acceleration is zero.

The car is still moving, and it keeps covering equal distances in equal intervals of time.

Therefore, only Statements II and IV are correct.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Uniform Motion to revise constant velocity, zero acceleration, distance-time relationships, velocity-time graphs, and the differences between constant speed and constant velocity. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed explanations, worked examples, and additional JAMB-style practice questions.

question: A coastal survey vessel travels across calm water in a straight line at a constant velocity of 8 m s⁻¹ for 75 s while collecting seabed data. Assuming the vessel maintains the same speed and direction throughout the journey, what displacement does it cover during this period?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to apply the concept of uniform motion by calculating the displacement of a survey vessel moving with constant velocity over a given time.
explanation.principle:
Uniform motion occurs when an object moves with constant velocity. This means that both its speed and direction remain unchanged throughout the motion.

Because the direction does not change, the displacement is obtained directly from the relationship:

v = d/t

where:

• v = velocity (m s⁻¹)
• d = displacement (m)
• t = time (s)

Rearranging the formula gives:

 d = vt

This equation is valid only when the velocity remains constant during the motion.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Velocity, v = 8 m s⁻¹
Time, t = 75 s

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

Displacement = Velocity × Time

 d = vt

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

 d = 8 × 75

Step 4: Perform the calculation.

 d = 600 m

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

The correct displacement is 600 m.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (83 m) is incorrect because it does not result from multiplying the given velocity by the time.
Option C (67 m) is incorrect because it greatly underestimates the distance travelled during 75 seconds at 8 m s⁻¹.
Option D (9.4 m) is incorrect because it may result from an incorrect mathematical operation such as dividing instead of multiplying.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever an object moves with constant velocity in a straight line, remember that displacement is obtained by multiplying velocity by time.

Do not confuse displacement with speed. Velocity already includes direction, so when the direction remains unchanged, displacement and distance have the same numerical value.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine a survey boat moving steadily without speeding up, slowing down or changing direction.

If it travels 8 metres every second, then in 75 seconds it covers:

8 × 75 = 600 metres.

So, the vessel's displacement is 600 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Uniform Motion to learn how to calculate distance, displacement, speed and time in uniform motion, understand zero acceleration, interpret motion graphs and solve additional JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed lessons and exam-focused revision.

question:
A hospital uses an autonomous medicine delivery robot that travels along a straight corridor between the pharmacy and the wards. During one delivery, the robot moves without changing either its speed or its direction until it reaches its destination.

Which conclusion about the robot's motion is correct?

options.A: Its velocity is changing because it is in motion.
options.B: Its acceleration is zero because its velocity remains constant.
options.C: Its acceleration is constant because its speed is increasing uniformly.
options.D: Its motion is non-uniform because it continues to cover more distance with time.

answer_text: Its acceleration is zero because its velocity remains constant.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to interpret a real-life situation involving uniform motion. You are required to identify the correct conclusion when an object moves in a straight line without changing its speed or direction.
explanation.principle:
Uniform motion occurs when an object moves with constant velocity.

Velocity describes both the speed of an object and the direction in which it moves.

For motion to remain uniform:

• the speed must remain constant;
• the direction must remain constant; and
• the acceleration must be zero because acceleration is the rate of change of velocity.

Acceleration does not measure whether an object is moving; it measures whether its velocity is changing. Therefore, an object can move continuously while having zero acceleration if its velocity remains unchanged.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the type of motion described.

The robot moves along a straight corridor without changing either its speed or its direction.

This is uniform motion because its velocity remains constant.

Step 2: Recall the meaning of acceleration.

Acceleration is the rate at which velocity changes.

If there is no change in velocity, the acceleration is zero.

Step 3: Evaluate the options.

Option B correctly states that the robot's acceleration is zero because its velocity remains constant.

This matches the definition of uniform motion.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because an object's velocity does not change simply because it is moving. If both speed and direction remain constant, the velocity also remains constant.
Option C is incorrect because the question clearly states that the robot does not change its speed. Therefore, its speed is not increasing.

Option D is incorrect because covering more distance as time passes is normal for every moving object. Uniform motion depends on covering equal distances in equal time intervals, not on whether the total distance increases.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a JAMB question states that an object moves in a straight line without changing its speed or direction, immediately think of uniform motion.

Remember these key facts:

• Constant velocity.
• Zero acceleration.
• Equal distances covered in equal time intervals.

Do not confuse 'moving' with 'accelerating.' A body may be moving continuously while its acceleration is zero.
explanation.final_answer: B. Its acceleration is zero because its velocity remains constant.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pushing a trolley straight down a smooth hallway at exactly the same speed all the way.

Since neither the speed nor the direction changes, the trolley's velocity stays constant.

Because acceleration only occurs when velocity changes, the acceleration is zero throughout the journey.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Uniform Motion for a complete explanation of constant velocity, zero acceleration, equal distances in equal time intervals, and the interpretation of real-life examples and graphs. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional JAMB-style practice questions and detailed concept reviews.

question:
Engineers monitoring an autonomous drone corridor observe two delivery drones travelling along separate straight flight paths.

Drone P maintains the same speed and the same direction throughout its journey.

Drone Q maintains the same direction but its speed increases gradually with time.

Which statement correctly compares the motion of the two drones?

options.A: Drone P is in uniform motion, while Drone Q is in non-uniform motion because its velocity changes.
options.B: Both drones are in uniform motion because they move in the same direction.
options.C: Drone P is in non-uniform motion because every moving object has acceleration.
options.D: Both drones are in non-uniform motion because motion cannot occur without acceleration.

answer_text: Drone P is in uniform motion, while Drone Q is in non-uniform motion because its velocity changes.

explanation.question_restate: This question compares two moving drones to determine which one exhibits uniform motion and which one exhibits non-uniform motion based on changes in velocity.
explanation.principle:
Uniform motion occurs when an object moves with constant velocity.

Velocity is a vector quantity, meaning it has both magnitude (speed) and direction.

For motion to be uniform, neither the speed nor the direction should change.

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. Therefore, when velocity remains constant, acceleration is zero.

If either the speed or the direction changes, the velocity changes and the motion becomes non-uniform.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Drone P.

Drone P maintains the same speed and the same direction throughout its motion.

Its velocity remains constant.

Therefore, Drone P is in uniform motion and has zero acceleration.

Step 2: Examine Drone Q.

Although Drone Q continues in the same direction, its speed increases gradually.

An increase in speed means the magnitude of its velocity changes.

Therefore, Drone Q has acceleration and is in non-uniform motion.

Step 3: Compare the two motions.

Drone P has constant velocity.

Drone Q has changing velocity.

Hence, only Drone P exhibits uniform motion.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Option A correctly describes both motions.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because moving in the same direction alone does not guarantee uniform motion. The speed must also remain constant.
Option C is incorrect because an object can continue moving with zero acceleration if its velocity remains constant.
Option D is incorrect because acceleration is not required to maintain uniform motion. Acceleration is only present when velocity changes.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB Physics, always remember that uniform motion refers to constant velocity—not merely movement.

Ask yourself two questions:

• Is the speed constant?
• Is the direction constant?

If the answer to both is 'Yes', the motion is uniform and the acceleration is zero.
explanation.final_answer: A. Drone P is in uniform motion, while Drone Q is in non-uniform motion because its velocity changes.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two cyclists riding along a straight road.

One rides at exactly the same speed all the way. The other starts slowly and keeps pedalling faster.

The first cyclist is in uniform motion because the speed and direction remain unchanged.

The second cyclist is in non-uniform motion because the speed changes, causing the velocity to change.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Uniform Motion to learn the characteristics of uniform motion, the meaning of constant velocity, the relationship between velocity and acceleration, and how to distinguish uniform motion from non-uniform motion using practical examples and JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive revision and exam-focused exercises.

question:
An engineering supervisor reviews the operation of an automated carrier in a smart logistics distribution centre. Four trainees make the following statements about the carrier while it moves in a straight line with uniform motion:

Statement I: 'The carrier covers equal distances in equal intervals of time.'

Statement II: 'Its acceleration is zero because its velocity remains constant.'

Statement III: 'The carrier must continuously accelerate in order to keep moving at a constant velocity.'

Statement IV: 'If the carrier changes direction while maintaining the same speed, it is still in uniform motion.'

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question evaluates your understanding of the defining characteristics of uniform motion by asking you to identify which statements correctly describe an object moving with constant velocity in a straight line.
explanation.principle:
Uniform motion is motion in which an object moves with constant velocity.

Velocity is a vector quantity, meaning it has both magnitude (speed) and direction.

For motion to remain uniform:

• the speed must remain constant,
• the direction must remain unchanged,
• equal distances are covered in equal intervals of time, and
• acceleration is zero because there is no change in velocity.

A common misunderstanding is to think that a moving object must always be accelerating. In reality, acceleration occurs only when velocity changes in magnitude, direction or both.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

The carrier covers equal distances in equal intervals of time.

This is a defining characteristic of uniform motion.

Therefore, Statement I is correct.

Step 2: Examine Statement II.

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity.

Since the carrier's velocity remains constant, there is no change in velocity and its acceleration is zero.

Therefore, Statement II is correct.

Step 3: Examine Statement III.

The statement claims that continuous acceleration is required to maintain constant velocity.

This is incorrect.

An object moving with constant velocity has zero acceleration because its velocity is not changing.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

Although the speed remains the same, changing direction changes the velocity.

Once velocity changes, the motion is no longer uniform.

Therefore, Statement IV is incorrect.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Only Statements I and II are correct.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Statement III, which wrongly assumes that continuous acceleration is required to maintain constant velocity.
Option C is incorrect because Statements III and IV are both incorrect. Uniform motion does not require continuous acceleration, and changing direction changes velocity.
Option D is incorrect because although Statements I and II are correct, Statement IV is false since a change in direction means the velocity has changed.
explanation.exam_tip:
When answering JAMB questions on uniform motion, always remember that velocity includes both speed and direction.

An object is in uniform motion only when both remain constant.

If either the speed or the direction changes, the velocity changes and acceleration is no longer zero.

Do not confuse constant speed with constant velocity.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine an automated carrier moving along a perfectly straight track at exactly the same speed throughout its journey.

It covers equal distances every second and its direction never changes.

Because its velocity stays the same, its acceleration is zero.

If the carrier turns a corner, even without changing speed, its direction changes. That means its velocity changes, so it is no longer in uniform motion.

Therefore, only Statements I and II are correct.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Uniform Motion for a complete revision of constant velocity, zero acceleration, distance-time graphs, velocity-time graphs, and the distinction between constant speed and constant velocity. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, concept summaries, and additional JAMB-style practice questions.

question:
As vehicles move through a busy urban traffic intersection, a traffic engineer observes that some vehicles continually speed up, slow down or change direction to respond to traffic signals and other road users.

Which characteristic best identifies this as non-uniform motion?

options.A: The vehicle covers equal distances in equal time intervals.
options.B: The vehicle's velocity changes during motion.
options.C: The vehicle moves with constant speed in a straight line.
options.D: The vehicle remains at rest for part of the journey.

answer_text: The vehicle's velocity changes during motion.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the defining feature of non-uniform motion by using the familiar situation of vehicles moving through a busy traffic intersection.
explanation.principle:
Motion is described as non-uniform whenever the velocity of an object changes with time.

Before answering this question, it is important to understand what velocity means.

Velocity is the rate of change of displacement and has both magnitude (speed) and direction.

A beginner may ask, 'What makes motion non-uniform?'

Motion becomes non-uniform whenever there is a change in velocity. This change may occur because:

• the speed increases,
• the speed decreases,
• the direction changes, or
• both speed and direction change simultaneously.

Whenever velocity changes, acceleration is present, even if the object is slowing down.

In everyday life, most moving vehicles experience non-uniform motion because they frequently accelerate, brake or turn.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Option A.

Equal distances covered in equal time intervals describe uniform motion, not non-uniform motion.

Therefore, Option A is incorrect.

Step 2: Examine Option B.

A change in velocity is the defining characteristic of non-uniform motion.

Velocity changes whenever speed changes, direction changes or both change.

Therefore, Option B is correct.

Step 3: Examine Option C.

Moving with constant speed in a straight line means the velocity remains constant.

This describes uniform motion.

Therefore, Option C is incorrect.

Step 4: Examine Option D.

Remaining at rest does not define non-uniform motion. An object at rest is not in motion.

Therefore, Option D is incorrect.

Step 5: Select the correct answer.

Since non-uniform motion is identified by a change in velocity, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because equal distances covered in equal time intervals are a characteristic of uniform motion.
Option C is incorrect because constant speed in a straight line means both speed and direction remain unchanged, giving constant velocity.
Option D is incorrect because being at rest is not a characteristic of non-uniform motion. The question concerns objects that are already moving.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB Physics, remember that velocity depends on both speed and direction.

Whenever either speed or direction changes, the velocity changes, and the motion becomes non-uniform.

A common misconception is to think that only changing speed produces non-uniform motion. A vehicle travelling at constant speed around a bend is also in non-uniform motion because its direction changes continuously.
explanation.final_answer: B. The vehicle's velocity changes during motion.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine driving through a city.

You may accelerate when the traffic light turns green, slow down near a pedestrian crossing or turn into another street.

Each of these actions changes your velocity.

Because the velocity keeps changing, the motion is non-uniform.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion to learn the characteristics of changing motion, acceleration, practical examples, graphical interpretation and JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive concept reviews and examination-focused exercises.

question:
An airport operations manager observes a shuttle bus travelling from the terminal to a remote parking area. During four successive intervals of 20 s each, the bus covers 120 m, 165 m, 215 m and 270 m respectively.

What does this observation suggest about the motion of the shuttle bus?

options.A: The shuttle bus is moving with uniform motion because it travels continuously.
options.B: The shuttle bus is moving with non-uniform motion because it covers unequal distances in equal time intervals.
options.C: The shuttle bus is at rest because its speed is changing.
options.D: The shuttle bus has zero acceleration because it travels in the same direction.

answer_text: The shuttle bus is moving with non-uniform motion because it covers unequal distances in equal time intervals.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify non-uniform motion from observations made over equal time intervals. The key idea is to determine whether the object covers equal or unequal distances in the same amount of time.
explanation.principle:
Motion is described as uniform when an object covers equal distances in equal intervals of time while moving in a straight line. If the distances covered during equal time intervals are unequal, the object's velocity is changing, indicating non-uniform motion.

Non-uniform motion occurs whenever velocity changes. This change may result from an increase in speed, a decrease in speed, a change in direction, or a combination of both.

In this question, the time interval remains constant at 20 s, but the distances covered keep increasing. This means the shuttle bus is travelling faster as time progresses, so its velocity is changing. Therefore, the motion is non-uniform and the bus is accelerating.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the time intervals.

Each interval lasts 20 s, so the time intervals are equal.

Step 2: Compare the distances travelled.

The shuttle bus covers:
• 120 m
• 165 m
• 215 m
• 270 m

These distances are different and increase from one interval to the next.

Step 3: Apply the definition of uniform motion.

In uniform motion, equal distances are covered in equal time intervals.

Since this condition is not satisfied, the motion cannot be uniform.

Step 4: Draw the correct conclusion.

Because the shuttle bus covers unequal distances in equal time intervals, its velocity is changing. Therefore, it is undergoing non-uniform motion.

Hence, Option B is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because continuous movement alone does not imply uniform motion. Uniform motion requires equal distances to be covered in equal time intervals.
Option C is incorrect because the shuttle bus is clearly moving. A change in speed does not mean an object is at rest.

Option D is incorrect because travelling in the same direction does not necessarily mean acceleration is zero. Since the distance covered every 20 s increases, the speed is increasing, indicating positive acceleration.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question states that equal time intervals are involved, immediately compare the distances covered.

• Equal distances in equal time intervals → Uniform motion.
• Unequal distances in equal time intervals → Non-uniform motion.

JAMB frequently tests this concept through practical situations rather than direct definitions.
explanation.final_answer: B. The shuttle bus is moving with non-uniform motion because it covers unequal distances in equal time intervals.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine walking with a friend for four periods of 20 seconds each.

If you walk 120 m, then 165 m, then 215 m, and finally 270 m, you are travelling farther during each equal period of time. This means you are speeding up.

Since your speed is changing, your motion is non-uniform.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion to learn how to distinguish uniform and non-uniform motion using everyday examples, distance-time relationships, velocity changes and JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed concept reviews and examination-focused exercises.

question:
While testing an electric delivery van, an engineer observes that its velocity increases uniformly from 5 m s⁻¹ to 17 m s⁻¹ over a period of 6 s after leaving a charging station.

What is the average acceleration of the van during this interval?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the average acceleration of an object whose velocity increases uniformly over a given time interval.
explanation.principle:
Acceleration is the rate at which velocity changes with time.

Before using the formula, it is important to understand the quantities involved.

Velocity is the speed of an object in a specified direction.

Acceleration tells us how quickly the velocity changes. If the velocity increases, the acceleration is positive. If the velocity decreases, the acceleration is negative (deceleration).

The formula for average acceleration is:

a = (v − u) / t

where:

• a = average acceleration (m s⁻²)
• u = initial velocity (m s⁻¹)
• v = final velocity (m s⁻¹)
• t = time taken (s)

This formula works because acceleration is defined as the change in velocity divided by the time taken for that change.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Initial velocity, u = 5 m s⁻¹
Final velocity, v = 17 m s⁻¹
Time taken, t = 6 s

Step 2: State the required quantity.

Required: Average acceleration, a.

Step 3: Apply the formula.

a = (v − u) / t

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

a = (17 − 5) / 6
= 12 / 6
= 2.0 m s⁻²

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

The calculated acceleration is 2.0 m s⁻², which corresponds to Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (1.0 m s⁻²) is incorrect because it underestimates the change in velocity, possibly due to dividing incorrectly.
Option C (3.0 m s⁻²) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect calculation of the change in velocity or time.
Option D (3.7 m s⁻²) is incorrect because it does not follow the acceleration formula and may result from dividing the final velocity directly by the time.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, always write down the initial velocity, final velocity and time before substituting into the formula.

Remember:

• Use the change in velocity (v − u), not the final velocity alone.
• A positive answer indicates speeding up.
• The SI unit of acceleration is m s⁻².
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine the delivery van gradually gains speed after leaving the charging station.

It increases its velocity by 12 m s⁻¹ in 6 seconds.

Since acceleration is the change in velocity divided by time:

12 ÷ 6 = 2.0 m s⁻².

Therefore, the average acceleration is 2.0 m s⁻².
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion to learn how to calculate average acceleration, distinguish acceleration from deceleration, and solve additional JAMB-style problems. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for step-by-step lessons and practice questions.

question:
An automated warehouse robot moves along a straight aisle while transporting packages. During three successive equal time intervals of 4 s each, it covers distances of 28 m, 41 m and 57 m respectively.

What does this observation indicate about the robot's motion?

options.A: The robot is moving with uniform motion because it travels in a straight line.
options.B: The robot is moving with non-uniform motion because it covers unequal distances in equal time intervals.
options.C: The robot is stationary during part of its motion.
options.D: The robot maintains a constant velocity throughout the journey.

answer_text: The robot is moving with non-uniform motion because it covers unequal distances in equal time intervals.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret information that would normally be presented on a distance-time graph. Instead of seeing the graph, you are given the distances travelled during equal time intervals and asked to determine the nature of the motion.
explanation.principle:
Motion is described as uniform when an object covers equal distances in equal intervals of time. Conversely, motion is non-uniform when unequal distances are covered in equal intervals of time.

A distance-time graph helps us identify the type of motion.

• A straight line with a constant slope represents uniform motion because the speed remains constant.

• A graph whose slope changes (becomes steeper or less steep) represents non-uniform motion because the speed is changing.

The slope of a distance-time graph represents speed. Therefore, whenever the slope changes, the speed also changes.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the given data.

Equal time interval = 4 s each.

Distances travelled = 28 m, 41 m and 57 m.

Step 2: Compare the distances.

The robot does not cover the same distance every 4 s.

Instead, the distances increase from 28 m to 41 m and then to 57 m.

Step 3: Interpret the motion.

Since unequal distances are covered in equal time intervals, the speed is changing.

A changing speed means the velocity is changing (assuming motion is along the same straight path), so the motion is non-uniform.

Step 4: Relate this to the distance-time graph.

If these values were plotted on a distance-time graph, the graph would become progressively steeper with time.

A changing slope confirms that the motion is non-uniform.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

Option B correctly describes the observed motion.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because moving in a straight line alone does not guarantee uniform motion. Uniform motion requires equal distances to be covered in equal time intervals.
Option C is incorrect because nothing in the data suggests that the robot stopped. The distances covered continue to increase throughout the motion.
Option D is incorrect because constant velocity would require equal distances to be travelled in equal time intervals. The given data clearly shows otherwise.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB Physics, whenever equal time intervals produce unequal distances, immediately think of non-uniform motion.

Remember:

• Equal distances in equal time → Uniform motion.
• Unequal distances in equal time → Non-uniform motion.
• On a distance-time graph, the slope represents speed. A changing slope indicates changing speed.
explanation.final_answer: B. The robot is moving with non-uniform motion because it covers unequal distances in equal time intervals.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine walking for 4 minutes at a time.

If you walk 28 m in the first 4 minutes, 41 m in the next 4 minutes and 57 m in the last 4 minutes, you are covering more distance during each equal time interval.

This means your speed is changing, so your motion is non-uniform.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion to learn how to interpret distance-time and velocity-time graphs, recognise changing speed, identify acceleration, and solve authentic JAMB-style questions. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and exam-focused practice.

question: A high-speed rail operator observes that a train increases its speed steadily after departing a station before later travelling at a constant speed. Which stage of the journey best illustrates non-uniform motion?

options.A: The train remains stationary at the platform before departure.
options.B: The train moves at a constant speed along a straight section of the track.
options.C: The train increases its speed steadily after leaving the station.
options.D: The train waits at the next station for passengers to board.

answer_text: The train increases its speed steadily after leaving the station.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify non-uniform motion in a practical transportation system. You are required to recognise the stage during which the train's velocity changes.
explanation.principle:
Motion is described as either uniform or non-uniform depending on whether the velocity remains constant.

Before answering this question, it is important to understand the meaning of velocity.

Velocity is the speed of an object in a specified direction.

Non-uniform motion occurs whenever velocity changes with time.

A change in velocity can occur because:

• the speed changes,
• the direction changes, or
• both change simultaneously.

Whenever velocity changes, the object experiences acceleration.

Uniform motion, on the other hand, occurs only when both the speed and direction remain constant throughout the motion.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Option A.

A stationary train is not moving, so it does not represent motion at all.

Therefore, Option A is incorrect.

Step 2: Examine Option B.

A train travelling at a constant speed in a straight line has constant velocity.

This is an example of uniform motion rather than non-uniform motion.

Therefore, Option B is incorrect.

Step 3: Examine Option C.

When the train increases its speed after leaving the station, its velocity changes with time.

Since the velocity is changing, the train is accelerating.

This is the defining characteristic of non-uniform motion.

Therefore, Option C is correct.

Step 4: Examine Option D.

A train waiting at a station is stationary.

Since there is no motion, this cannot be classified as non-uniform motion.

Therefore, Option D is incorrect.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

Only the stage during which the train is increasing its speed represents non-uniform motion.

Hence, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because a stationary object is not in motion and therefore cannot be classified as exhibiting uniform or non-uniform motion.
Option B is incorrect because constant speed in a straight line means constant velocity, which is uniform motion.
Option D is incorrect because a train waiting at a station is at rest and does not exhibit motion.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB Physics, remember that non-uniform motion is identified by a change in velocity.

Do not think only about speeding up.

Whenever an object speeds up, slows down or changes direction, its velocity changes and it is undergoing non-uniform motion.

A common mistake is to confuse constant speed in a straight line with non-uniform motion. Constant speed in a straight line is uniform motion because the velocity does not change.
explanation.final_answer: C. The train increases its speed steadily after leaving the station.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine riding on a train.

As it leaves the station, you feel yourself being pushed slightly backward because the train is speeding up. During this period, the train's velocity is changing, so it is in non-uniform motion.

Once the train reaches a steady cruising speed on a straight track, the motion becomes uniform because the velocity remains constant.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion for a detailed explanation of changing velocity, acceleration, practical transportation examples, graphical interpretation, and additional JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for concept summaries, worked examples and exam-focused revision.

question:
During a 100 m sprint, an athletics coach observes that an athlete leaves the starting blocks, steadily increases speed for several seconds, and then maintains an almost constant speed before crossing the finish line.

Which statement best explains why the athlete experiences acceleration only during the first part of the race?

options.A: Acceleration occurs because the athlete is covering equal distances in equal intervals of time throughout the race.
options.B: Acceleration is present only while the athlete's velocity is changing; once the velocity becomes nearly constant, the acceleration is approximately zero.
options.C: Acceleration exists throughout the race because the athlete continues moving until the finish line.
options.D: Acceleration depends only on the total distance covered and not on changes in velocity.

answer_text: Acceleration is present only while the athlete's velocity is changing; once the velocity becomes nearly constant, the acceleration is approximately zero.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of when acceleration occurs during motion. It requires you to distinguish between motion with changing velocity and motion with nearly constant velocity in a real-life athletics event.
explanation.principle:
Acceleration is the rate at which velocity changes with time.

Velocity describes both the speed of an object and the direction in which it moves.

Mathematically,

a = Δv / Δt

where:
• a = acceleration
• Δv = change in velocity
• Δt = time interval.

Whenever an object's velocity changes, acceleration is present. If the velocity remains constant, the acceleration is zero.

In a sprint race, an athlete first increases speed after leaving the starting blocks. During this period, the velocity changes continuously, so acceleration exists. Once the athlete reaches an almost constant speed, the velocity changes very little, making the acceleration approximately zero.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the stage immediately after the start.

The athlete increases speed, meaning the velocity changes with time.

Therefore, acceleration is present.

Step 2: Consider the later stage of the race.

The athlete moves at an almost constant speed in the same direction.

Since the velocity is nearly constant, the acceleration becomes approximately zero.

Step 3: Evaluate the options.

Option B correctly states that acceleration exists only while the velocity is changing.

This agrees with the definition of acceleration.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because equal distances covered in equal time intervals describe uniform motion, not acceleration.
Option C is incorrect because an object can continue moving without accelerating if its velocity remains constant.
Option D is incorrect because acceleration depends on changes in velocity, not simply on the total distance travelled.
explanation.exam_tip:
A common JAMB misconception is to assume that every moving object is accelerating. Remember that motion and acceleration are different ideas.

• Moving with constant velocity means zero acceleration.
• Speeding up, slowing down or changing direction means acceleration is present.

Whenever you see the phrase 'changing velocity,' immediately think of acceleration.
explanation.final_answer: B. Acceleration is present only while the athlete's velocity is changing; once the velocity becomes nearly constant, the acceleration is approximately zero.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine riding a bicycle.

When you begin pedalling, you gradually speed up. Your speed is changing, so you are accelerating.

After reaching a comfortable speed and maintaining it, you are still moving, but your speed is no longer changing. At that stage, your acceleration is zero.

The athlete in the race behaves in the same way, so Option B is correct.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion to learn how changes in speed or direction produce acceleration. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, worked examples, graph interpretation, and JAMB-style practice questions.

question: During a braking test, an autonomous emergency vehicle reduces its velocity uniformly from 30 m s⁻¹ to 12 m s⁻¹ in 6 s. What is the average acceleration of the vehicle during the braking period?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the average acceleration of an autonomous emergency vehicle that slows down uniformly during braking. Since the vehicle is losing speed, the acceleration will be negative.
explanation.principle:
Acceleration is the rate at which velocity changes with time.

Velocity is a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction. Whenever the velocity of an object changes, either by increasing, decreasing or changing direction, the object experiences acceleration.

When a moving object slows down while continuing in the same direction, the acceleration is negative. This negative acceleration is commonly called deceleration, although it is still a form of acceleration.

The average acceleration is calculated using:

a = (v - u) / t

where:

• a = average acceleration
• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity
• t = time taken
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Initial velocity, u = 30 m s⁻¹
Final velocity, v = 12 m s⁻¹
Time taken, t = 6 s

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

Average acceleration:

a = (v - u) / t

Step 3: Substitute the values.

a = (12 - 30) / 6
= -18 / 6
= -3.0 m s⁻²

Step 4: Interpret the result.

The negative sign indicates that the vehicle is slowing down. Therefore, its average acceleration during braking is -3.0 m s⁻².

Step 5: Select the correct option.

Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it ignores the negative sign. During braking, acceleration is negative because the velocity decreases.
Option C is incorrect because it results from an incorrect calculation of the change in velocity or division.
Option D is incorrect because it is both numerically incorrect and has the wrong sign for a braking motion.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question involves braking or slowing down, expect the acceleration to be negative if the positive direction is taken as the direction of motion. Always calculate the change in velocity first before dividing by the time taken.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine riding in a car that slows down before reaching a traffic light. The car's speed decreases every second. This decrease in velocity means the car has negative acceleration (deceleration). Using the acceleration formula gives -3.0 m s⁻².
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion → Average Acceleration for a complete explanation of acceleration, deceleration, velocity changes, worked examples and additional JAMB-style practice. question:
From the velocity-time graph described below, a metro train moves with velocities of 7 m s⁻¹, 11 m s⁻¹, 16 m s⁻¹ and 22 m s⁻¹ at successive equal time intervals after leaving a station.

Which conclusion about the train's motion is most appropriate?

options.A: The train is moving with uniform velocity because its speed remains positive.
options.B: The train is undergoing non-uniform motion because its velocity changes with time.
options.C: The train is at rest during part of its journey because the graph rises upward.
options.D: The train covers equal distances in equal time intervals because its velocity keeps increasing.

answer_text: The train is undergoing non-uniform motion because its velocity changes with time.

explanation.question_restate: This question describes a velocity-time graph for a metro train. Instead of performing calculations, you are required to interpret what the changing velocities reveal about the nature of the train's motion.
explanation.principle:
Motion is described using quantities such as distance, speed and velocity.

Velocity is the speed of an object in a specified direction.

A beginner may ask, 'What makes motion non-uniform?'

Non-uniform motion occurs whenever the velocity of an object changes with time.

The change may result from:

• an increase in speed,
• a decrease in speed,
• a change in direction, or
• both speed and direction changing.

A velocity-time graph shows how velocity varies with time.

If the graph is horizontal, the velocity is constant and the motion is uniform.

If the graph rises or falls, the velocity is changing, indicating acceleration or deceleration. Therefore, the motion is non-uniform.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the given velocities.

The train has velocities of 7 m s⁻¹, 11 m s⁻¹, 16 m s⁻¹ and 22 m s⁻¹ at equal time intervals.

These values are different, showing that the velocity is changing with time.

Step 2: Interpret the velocity-time graph.

Since the graph rises from one interval to the next, the train is speeding up.

A rising velocity-time graph indicates positive acceleration.

Step 3: Identify the type of motion.

Because the velocity is not constant, the motion is non-uniform.

Therefore, Option B is the correct conclusion.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because uniform velocity requires the velocity to remain constant. Here, the velocity increases with time.
Option C is incorrect because an upward-sloping velocity-time graph does not indicate that the train is at rest. The listed velocities are all greater than zero.
Option D is incorrect because equal distances are covered in equal time intervals only during uniform motion. Since the velocity changes, the distances covered in equal time intervals also change.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB presents a velocity-time graph, first determine whether the graph is horizontal, rising or falling.

• Horizontal graph → constant velocity → uniform motion.
• Rising graph → increasing velocity → positive acceleration.
• Falling graph → decreasing velocity → deceleration (negative acceleration).

Remember that any change in velocity means the motion is non-uniform.
explanation.final_answer: B. The train is undergoing non-uniform motion because its velocity changes with time.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine riding on a metro train that keeps gaining speed after leaving a station.

Each moment, the train is travelling faster than before.

Since its velocity is changing, it is not moving uniformly. Instead, it is undergoing non-uniform motion.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion → Velocity-Time Graphs for a complete explanation of how to interpret velocity-time graphs, identify acceleration and distinguish uniform from non-uniform motion. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional JAMB-style worked examples and graph interpretation practice.

question:
An engineering technician observes an automated packaging machine on a production line. As each package approaches the sealing unit, the conveyor belt slows down to ensure accurate sealing and then speeds up again after the package leaves the station.

Which statement best describes the motion of the conveyor belt during this operation?

options.A: It is in uniform motion because it continues moving in the same direction throughout.
options.B: It is in non-uniform motion because its velocity changes as it slows down and speeds up.
options.C: It is at rest because it briefly reduces its speed before accelerating again.
options.D: It is in uniform motion because equal distances are covered regardless of the change in speed.

answer_text: It is in non-uniform motion because its velocity changes as it slows down and speeds up.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to recognise non-uniform motion in an industrial automation system. You are required to identify how changes in speed affect the nature of motion.
explanation.principle:
Motion is said to be **uniform** when an object moves with constant velocity, meaning both its speed and direction remain unchanged.

**Non-uniform motion** occurs whenever the velocity changes. Velocity can change because:

• the speed changes,
• the direction changes, or
• both speed and direction change.

In this situation, the conveyor belt first slows down and later speeds up while moving in the same direction. Since its speed is changing, its velocity is also changing. Therefore, the motion is non-uniform, and acceleration is present during both the slowing down (deceleration) and speeding up processes.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what happens to the conveyor belt.

It slows down before the sealing unit and speeds up afterwards.

Step 2: Determine whether the velocity remains constant.

Since the speed changes, the velocity is not constant even though the direction remains the same.

Step 3: Recall the definition of non-uniform motion.

Any motion in which velocity changes with time is non-uniform motion.

Step 4: Select the option that correctly describes the situation.

Option B correctly states that the conveyor belt is in non-uniform motion because its velocity changes.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because moving in the same direction alone does not guarantee uniform motion. The speed must also remain constant.
Option C is incorrect because reducing speed does not mean the conveyor belt is at rest. It continues moving throughout the process.
Option D is incorrect because uniform motion requires constant velocity. A change in speed means equal distances are generally not covered in equal time intervals.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB describes an object that speeds up, slows down or changes direction, immediately think about velocity.

If velocity changes in any way, the motion is non-uniform and acceleration is present. Remember that deceleration is also a form of acceleration because it involves a change in velocity.
explanation.final_answer: B. It is in non-uniform motion because its velocity changes as it slows down and speeds up.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine walking through a doorway. You naturally slow down to pass through safely and then walk faster again afterwards.

Even though you keep moving forward, your speed changes. That means your velocity changes, so your motion is non-uniform.

The conveyor belt behaves in the same way during the packaging process.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion to learn how changing speed or direction affects velocity, recognise acceleration in everyday situations, interpret motion graphs, and solve JAMB-style application questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional concept reviews and practice exercises.

question: While approaching a designated landing zone, a drone delivering a medical parcel gradually reduces its speed from 15 m s⁻¹ to 6 m s⁻¹ before touching down. Which statement best describes the drone's motion during this period?

options.A: The drone is in non-uniform motion because its velocity is changing, so it is experiencing deceleration.
options.B: The drone is in uniform motion because it is still moving in the same direction.
options.C: The drone has zero acceleration because it has not yet come to rest.
options.D: The drone is covering equal distances in equal time intervals because it is descending smoothly.

answer_text: The drone is in non-uniform motion because its velocity is changing, so it is experiencing deceleration.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of how a change in speed affects the type of motion of an object. It requires you to recognise that a drone slowing down while moving is undergoing non-uniform motion.
explanation.principle:
Non-uniform motion occurs whenever an object's velocity changes with time.

Velocity is a vector quantity, meaning it has both magnitude (speed) and direction.

If either the speed changes, the direction changes, or both change, the velocity changes.

Whenever velocity changes, the object experiences acceleration.

If the speed decreases, the acceleration is called deceleration (negative acceleration).

In this case, the drone's speed decreases from 15 m s⁻¹ to 6 m s⁻¹ while preparing to land. Since its velocity is changing, the motion is non-uniform.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what changes during the motion.

The drone's speed decreases from 15 m s⁻¹ to 6 m s⁻¹.

Step 2: Recall the definition of non-uniform motion.

An object is in non-uniform motion whenever its velocity changes with time.

Step 3: Determine whether acceleration is present.

Because the velocity is changing, acceleration exists.

Since the speed is decreasing, the acceleration is negative and is known as deceleration.

Step 4: Evaluate the options.

Only Option A correctly states that the drone is undergoing non-uniform motion because its velocity is changing.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because moving in the same direction does not guarantee uniform motion. A change in speed means the velocity has changed.
Option C is incorrect because an object can have acceleration even while it is still moving. Acceleration depends on a change in velocity, not on whether the object has stopped.
Option D is incorrect because a smoothly slowing object generally does not cover equal distances in equal time intervals. Equal distances in equal time intervals are characteristic of uniform motion.
explanation.exam_tip: A common JAMB misconception is that acceleration means only 'speeding up.' Remember that slowing down is also a form of acceleration because the velocity is changing. Whenever you notice words such as 'slows down,' 'brakes,' or 'reduces speed,' think of deceleration and non-uniform motion.
explanation.final_answer: A. The drone is in non-uniform motion because its velocity is changing, so it is experiencing deceleration.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine riding a bicycle toward a gate. You squeeze the brakes and gradually slow down before stopping. Even though you are still moving forward, your speed is changing. That means your velocity is changing, so your motion is non-uniform.

The drone behaves in exactly the same way as it slows from 15 m s⁻¹ to 6 m s⁻¹ before landing.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion for a complete revision of changing velocity, acceleration, deceleration, distance-time graphs, velocity-time graphs and practical JAMB-style applications. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed explanations, worked examples and additional practice questions.

question:
