While observing movements within a busy harbour, a marine operations supervisor noticed that one service boat travelled in a straight channel at a constant speed, whereas another repeatedly accelerated, slowed down and changed direction while assisting larger vessels.

Which statement best compares the motions of the two boats?

options.A: The first boat exhibits uniform motion because its velocity remains constant, while the second exhibits non-uniform motion because its velocity changes.
options.B: Both boats exhibit uniform motion because they are moving on water.
options.C: The first boat exhibits non-uniform motion because every moving object experiences acceleration.
options.D: The second boat exhibits uniform motion because changing direction does not affect velocity.

answer_text: The first boat exhibits uniform motion because its velocity remains constant, while the second exhibits non-uniform motion because its velocity changes.

explanation.question_restate: This question compares uniform motion with non-uniform motion using the practical example of two harbour service boats. You are required to identify which description correctly explains the motion of each boat.
explanation.principle:
Motion is described using velocity, which is a vector quantity having both magnitude (speed) and direction.

Uniform motion occurs when an object moves with constant velocity. This means both its speed and direction remain unchanged over time.

Non-uniform motion occurs whenever velocity changes. The change may result from:

• an increase in speed,
• a decrease in speed,
• a change in direction, or
• both speed and direction changing simultaneously.

Whenever velocity changes, the object is accelerating. Acceleration therefore includes both speeding up and slowing down, as well as changing direction.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the first service boat.

It travels at a constant speed in a straight channel.

Since both its speed and direction remain constant, its velocity remains constant.

Therefore, it is in uniform motion.

Step 2: Examine the second service boat.

It accelerates, slows down and changes direction while assisting larger vessels.

Each of these actions changes its velocity.

Therefore, it is in non-uniform motion.

Step 3: Evaluate the options.

Option A correctly distinguishes between constant velocity (uniform motion) and changing velocity (non-uniform motion).

Hence, Option A is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the type of surface on which an object moves does not determine whether its motion is uniform or non-uniform. The determining factor is whether its velocity remains constant.
Option C is incorrect because an object moving with constant velocity has zero acceleration. Not every moving object is accelerating.
Option D is incorrect because velocity depends on both speed and direction. A change in direction changes velocity, making the motion non-uniform.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever you compare uniform and non-uniform motion, focus on velocity rather than speed alone.

Remember:

• Constant speed in a straight line means constant velocity.
• Any change in speed or direction changes velocity.
• A change in velocity means acceleration is present.

JAMB often uses changing direction as a distractor because many candidates mistakenly consider only changes in speed.
explanation.final_answer: A. The first boat exhibits uniform motion because its velocity remains constant, while the second exhibits non-uniform motion because its velocity changes.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine two boats.

One sails straight ahead at the same speed without turning. Its velocity does not change, so it is moving uniformly.

The other speeds up, slows down and turns while helping ships dock. Since its velocity keeps changing, it is in non-uniform motion.

Therefore, Option A correctly compares the two motions.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion to learn how to distinguish uniform motion from non-uniform motion using real-life examples, motion graphs and JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for concept reviews, worked examples and examination-focused exercises.

question:
An engineering supervisor is evaluating the understanding of four trainee technicians about the motion of an AI-guided inspection robot moving through a factory. The robot sometimes speeds up, sometimes slows down, and occasionally changes direction while inspecting equipment.

The trainees make the following statements:

I. The robot is in non-uniform motion whenever its velocity changes.

II. If the robot moves at a constant speed but continuously changes direction, its motion is still non-uniform.

III. A decrease in the robot's speed means it has no acceleration because it is slowing down.

IV. During non-uniform motion, the robot generally covers unequal distances in equal time intervals.

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question evaluates your understanding of the characteristics of non-uniform motion by asking you to determine which statements correctly describe the motion of an AI-guided inspection robot.
explanation.principle:
Motion is described using quantities such as distance, displacement, speed, velocity and acceleration.

Velocity is the rate of change of displacement and includes both magnitude (speed) and direction.

Acceleration is the rate at which velocity changes.

A change in velocity may result from:

• a change in speed,
• a change in direction, or
• both occurring together.

Therefore, any motion in which velocity changes is called non-uniform motion.

Many beginners wrongly believe that acceleration only occurs when an object speeds up. In reality, slowing down (deceleration) is also a form of acceleration because the velocity is changing.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

'The robot is in non-uniform motion whenever its velocity changes.'

This is correct because non-uniform motion is defined by changing velocity.

Step 2: Examine Statement II.

'If the robot moves at a constant speed but continuously changes direction, its motion is still non-uniform.'

This statement is correct.

Although the speed remains constant, the direction changes. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, the velocity changes, making the motion non-uniform.

Step 3: Examine Statement III.

'A decrease in the robot's speed means it has no acceleration because it is slowing down.'

This statement is incorrect.

Slowing down means the velocity is changing with time. This is negative acceleration (deceleration), which is still acceleration.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

'During non-uniform motion, the robot generally covers unequal distances in equal time intervals.'

This statement is correct because changing velocity usually causes different distances to be covered during successive equal time intervals.

Step 5: Determine the correct combination.

Statements I, II and IV are correct, while Statement III is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Statement III, which wrongly assumes that slowing down means there is no acceleration. It also omits Statement II and Statement IV, both of which are correct.
Option C is incorrect because although Statements II and IV are correct, Statement III is false, while Statement I has been wrongly omitted.
Option D is incorrect because it includes the incorrect Statement III. A single incorrect statement makes the entire combination incorrect.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB asks about non-uniform motion, remember that velocity depends on both speed and direction.

Ask yourself these questions:

• Has the speed changed?
• Has the direction changed?

If the answer to either question is 'Yes', then the velocity has changed, meaning acceleration is present and the motion is non-uniform.

Be careful with statements suggesting that slowing down means 'no acceleration.' Deceleration is simply acceleration acting opposite to the direction of motion.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, II and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine riding a bicycle.

If you pedal faster, your speed changes.

If you apply the brakes, your speed decreases.

If you ride around a bend without changing your speed, your direction changes.

In all these situations, your velocity changes, so your motion is non-uniform.

Therefore, Statements I, II and IV are correct, while Statement III is incorrect because slowing down is still acceleration.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion for a complete explanation of changing velocity, acceleration, deceleration, distance-time graphs, velocity-time graphs and practical JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional concept reviews and examination practice.

question:
An airport operations manager observes that immediately after touchdown, a passenger aircraft slows uniformly from 36 m s⁻¹ to 12 m s⁻¹ in 8 s during its landing rollout.

Which statement best describes the aircraft's motion during this interval?

options.A: The aircraft experiences a constant negative acceleration because its velocity decreases uniformly with time.
options.B: The aircraft moves with uniform motion because it covers the runway in a straight line.
options.C: The aircraft has zero acceleration because it is still moving forward throughout the rollout.
options.D: The aircraft's acceleration is positive because its direction of motion does not change.

answer_text: The aircraft experiences a constant negative acceleration because its velocity decreases uniformly with time.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of deceleration as a form of non-uniform motion. It requires you to recognise that a moving object can still be accelerating even when its speed is decreasing.
explanation.principle:
Non-uniform motion occurs whenever velocity changes with time.

Velocity is a vector quantity, meaning it has both magnitude (speed) and direction.

Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity.

The average acceleration is given by:

a = (v - u)/t

where:

• a = acceleration
• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity
• t = time taken

When the final velocity is less than the initial velocity, the calculated acceleration is negative. This negative acceleration is called deceleration or retardation. It does not mean the object moves backward; it simply indicates that the object's speed is reducing.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial velocity, u = 36 m s⁻¹
Final velocity, v = 12 m s⁻¹
Time, t = 8 s.

Step 2: Apply the acceleration formula.

a = (v - u)/t

= (12 - 36)/8

= -24/8

= -3 m s⁻².

Step 3: Interpret the result.

The negative sign shows that the aircraft is slowing down at a constant rate. Therefore, it experiences constant negative acceleration (deceleration).

Step 4: Select the option that correctly describes the motion.

Option A correctly explains that the aircraft undergoes constant negative acceleration because its velocity decreases uniformly with time.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because travelling in a straight line alone does not imply uniform motion. Uniform motion requires constant velocity, whereas the aircraft's velocity is decreasing.
Option C is incorrect because any change in velocity means acceleration is present. The aircraft is slowing down, so its acceleration is not zero.
Option D is incorrect because acceleration depends on the change in velocity, not merely the direction of motion. Since the speed is decreasing, the acceleration is negative rather than positive.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB Physics, remember that acceleration is any change in velocity.

A moving object that slows down still has acceleration.

Whenever the calculated acceleration is negative, the motion is described as deceleration or retardation. Do not confuse negative acceleration with moving backward.
explanation.final_answer: A. The aircraft experiences a constant negative acceleration because its velocity decreases uniformly with time.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine riding a bicycle and gently applying the brakes. You continue moving forward, but your speed becomes smaller every second.

Even though you have not changed direction, your velocity is changing because its magnitude is decreasing. That means you are accelerating in the negative direction, which is called deceleration.

The aircraft behaves in exactly the same way during landing.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion for a complete explanation of acceleration, deceleration, velocity changes, distance-time graphs, velocity-time graphs, and authentic JAMB-style worked examples. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice and concept reinforcement.

question:
During a training session at a smart manufacturing facility, four trainee technicians made the following observations about the motion of automated guided vehicles (AGVs):

Technician I: "A vehicle moving with constant speed around a curved path is in non-uniform motion because its direction changes continuously."

Technician II: "If a vehicle covers equal distances in equal time intervals, it must always be in non-uniform motion."

Technician III: "Whenever the velocity of a moving object changes, the object experiences acceleration."

Technician IV: "A vehicle that slows down while approaching a loading station is undergoing negative acceleration (deceleration)."

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question evaluates your overall understanding of the characteristics of non-uniform motion by asking you to identify which statements correctly describe situations involving changing velocity and acceleration.
explanation.principle:
Non-uniform motion occurs whenever the velocity of an object changes with time.

Velocity is a vector quantity, meaning it has both magnitude (speed) and direction.

Therefore, motion is non-uniform if:

• the speed changes,
• the direction changes,
• or both the speed and direction change.

Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity.

Mathematically,

Acceleration = Change in velocity ÷ Time taken

Whenever the velocity changes, acceleration is present. If the speed decreases, the acceleration is negative and is commonly called deceleration.

Objects in uniform motion generally cover equal distances in equal time intervals while maintaining constant velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Technician I's statement.

A vehicle moving at constant speed around a curved path changes its direction continuously. Since velocity depends on both speed and direction, its velocity changes even though its speed remains constant.

Therefore, Technician I is correct.

Step 2: Examine Technician II's statement.

Covering equal distances in equal time intervals is a characteristic of uniform motion, provided the direction also remains constant.

Therefore, this statement is incorrect.

Step 3: Examine Technician III's statement.

Acceleration occurs whenever velocity changes, whether due to an increase in speed, a decrease in speed or a change in direction.

Therefore, Technician III is correct.

Step 4: Examine Technician IV's statement.

When a vehicle slows down while approaching a loading station, its speed decreases with time. This is negative acceleration, also known as deceleration.

Therefore, Technician IV is correct.

Step 5: Determine the correct combination.

Technicians I, III and IV are correct, while Technician II is incorrect.

Hence, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because Technician II is wrong. Equal distances covered in equal time intervals describe uniform motion, not non-uniform motion.
Option C is incorrect because it omits Technician I, whose statement correctly recognises that changing direction alone produces non-uniform motion.
Option D is incorrect because it includes Technician II, whose statement incorrectly identifies a characteristic of uniform motion as non-uniform motion.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB Physics, remember that velocity depends on both speed and direction.

A change in either quantity means the velocity has changed, so acceleration exists.

Also remember:

• Equal distances in equal time intervals indicate uniform motion.
• Slowing down is still a form of acceleration called deceleration.
• Constant speed does not always mean uniform motion if the direction is changing.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, III and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine an automated factory vehicle.

If it turns around a bend, its direction changes, so its motion is non-uniform even if its speed stays the same.

If it slows down before stopping to collect materials, it is decelerating, which is negative acceleration.

Only Technician II is incorrect because equal distances covered in equal time intervals describe uniform motion, not non-uniform motion.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Non-uniform Motion to learn how changes in speed and direction affect velocity, understand acceleration and deceleration, interpret motion graphs, compare uniform and non-uniform motion, and practise additional JAMB-standard questions with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
A driver-training instructor asks trainees to determine the most appropriate equation of motion for calculating the final velocity of a vehicle moving with constant acceleration when its initial velocity, acceleration and time are known.

Which of the following equations should be used?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to identify the correct equation of motion for finding the final velocity of an object moving with constant acceleration when the initial velocity, acceleration and time are known.
explanation.principle:
The equations of motion (SUVAT equations) describe motion under constant acceleration.

The five standard variables are:

• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity
• a = acceleration
• t = time taken
• s = displacement

Each equation is designed for a particular combination of known and unknown quantities.

The first equation of motion is:

v = u + at

It directly relates the final velocity to the initial velocity, acceleration and time, without involving displacement.

Therefore, whenever u, a and t are known and v is required, this is the most suitable equation.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

The question provides:
• Initial velocity (u)
• Acceleration (a)
• Time (t)

The quantity required is the final velocity (v).

Step 2: Select the equation containing only these variables.

The equation v = u + at contains exactly the known quantities and the required quantity.

Step 3: Examine the remaining options.

Option B calculates displacement when final velocity is not required.

Option C is useful when time is not given.

Option D determines displacement using the average velocity.

Hence, Option A is the correct choice.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it is primarily used to determine displacement when the final velocity is not required.
Option C is incorrect because it is applied when time is absent from the available information.
Option D is incorrect because it is used to calculate displacement from the average of the initial and final velocities multiplied by time, not to determine the final velocity directly.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB Physics, do not memorise the equations blindly. First identify the known variables and the quantity to be found. Then choose the equation that contains only those variables. Correct equation selection is often the easiest way to avoid unnecessary calculations.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of the first equation of motion as the 'speed-update' equation.

It tells you how an object's velocity changes after accelerating for a given time.

If you already know the starting speed, the acceleration and the time, simply use:

v = u + at.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion to learn the meaning of each SUVAT variable, when each equation should be used, worked examples, common mistakes, and additional JAMB-style practice available in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

subtopic: Equations of Motion - First Equation of Motion (v = u + at)
question:
An electric commuter bus leaves a terminal and accelerates uniformly from an initial speed of 4 m s⁻¹ at 3 m s⁻² for 8 s.

What is the final velocity of the bus at the end of this interval?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the final velocity of an electric commuter bus moving with constant acceleration by applying the first equation of motion.
explanation.principle:
When an object moves with constant acceleration, its final velocity depends on three quantities:

• Initial velocity (u)
• Acceleration (a)
• Time of motion (t)

The first equation of motion expresses this relationship:

v = u + at

where:

• v = final velocity (m s⁻¹)
• u = initial velocity (m s⁻¹)
• a = acceleration (m s⁻²)
• t = time (s)

This equation is used whenever the displacement of the object is not required and the acceleration remains constant.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial velocity, u = 4 m s⁻¹
Acceleration, a = 3 m s⁻²
Time, t = 8 s

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

The quantity required is the final velocity, v.

Step 3: Select the appropriate equation.

Since u, a and t are given while displacement is not involved, use:

v = u + at

Step 4: Substitute the values.

v = 4 + (3 × 8)

v = 4 + 24

Step 5: Calculate the answer.

v = 28 m s⁻¹

Therefore, the bus attains a final velocity of 28 m s⁻¹.

The correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (24 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it considers only the increase in velocity (at = 24 m s⁻¹) and ignores the initial velocity of 4 m s⁻¹.
Option C (32 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect substitution or arithmetic error when applying the equation.
Option D (36 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it does not satisfy the equation v = u + at for the given values.
common_misconceptions: Some candidates forget to add the initial velocity after calculating at.
Others choose an inappropriate SUVAT equation even though all the required variables for v = u + at are available.
Another common mistake is confusing acceleration (m s⁻²) with velocity (m s⁻¹), leading to incorrect substitutions.
explanation.exam_tip: Before substituting values into any SUVAT equation, list the known variables (u, v, a, t and s). Then choose the equation that contains the required quantity while excluding any unknown variable you do not have. This strategy helps you select the correct equation quickly during the examination.
explanation.simple_explanation: The bus is already moving at 4 m s⁻¹. Every second, its speed increases by 3 m s⁻¹. After 8 seconds, its speed has increased by 24 m s⁻¹. Adding this increase to the initial speed gives 28 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion → First Equation of Motion (v = u + at) for a complete explanation of constant acceleration, how to identify the appropriate SUVAT equation, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice and exam-focused revision.

question:
As a metro train departs a station, it moves with an initial velocity of 6 m s⁻¹ and accelerates uniformly at 2 m s⁻² for 9 s.

What displacement does the train cover during this interval?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the displacement of a metro train moving with constant acceleration over a given time using the second equation of motion.
explanation.principle:
Displacement is the total distance covered in a specified direction.

When an object moves with constant acceleration and the final velocity is not required, the appropriate SUVAT equation is:

s = ut + ½at²

where:

u = initial velocity (m s⁻¹)
a = constant acceleration (m s⁻²)
t = time (s)
s = displacement (m)

This equation combines the distance travelled due to the initial velocity with the additional distance resulting from the acceleration.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial velocity, u = 6 m s⁻¹
Acceleration, a = 2 m s⁻²
Time, t = 9 s

Step 2: Write the appropriate equation.

s = ut + ½at²

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

s = (6 × 9) + ½ × 2 × 9²

Step 4: Evaluate each term.

6 × 9 = 54

9² = 81

½ × 2 × 81 = 81

Step 5: Add the two parts.

s = 54 + 81 = 135 m

Therefore, the displacement covered by the train is 135 m.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (81 m) is incorrect because it considers only the acceleration term and ignores the displacement due to the initial velocity.
Option B (108 m) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect substitution or arithmetic error when applying the equation.
Option D (162 m) is incorrect because it overestimates the displacement by adding an incorrect acceleration contribution.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever you are given the initial velocity, acceleration and time, but not the final velocity, the equation s = ut + ½at² is usually the most suitable choice.

Always calculate the ut term and the ½at² term separately before adding them.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine the train already has some starting speed when it leaves the station. It covers part of the journey because of that initial speed and even more because it keeps accelerating. Adding both contributions gives a total displacement of 135 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion to learn when and how to apply each SUVAT equation. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for worked examples, concept summaries and additional JAMB-style practice questions.

question:
A warehouse forklift accelerates uniformly while transporting a loaded pallet from the storage area to the dispatch section. Its speed increases from 11 m s⁻¹ to 35 m s⁻¹ in 8 s.

What is the acceleration of the forklift?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the constant acceleration of a warehouse forklift whose speed increases uniformly over a known time interval. Since the initial velocity, final velocity and time are given, the appropriate equation of motion should be selected to calculate the acceleration.
explanation.principle:
Acceleration is the rate at which velocity changes with time.

Whenever an object moves with constant acceleration, the first equation of motion relates the initial velocity, final velocity, acceleration and time.

The equation is:

v = u + at

where:

• v = final velocity (m s⁻¹)
• u = initial velocity (m s⁻¹)
• a = acceleration (m s⁻²)
• t = time taken (s)

If acceleration is the unknown quantity, the equation can be rearranged to:

a = (v − u)/t

This equation should only be used when the acceleration remains constant.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial velocity, u = 11 m s⁻¹
Final velocity, v = 35 m s⁻¹
Time, t = 8 s
Required quantity = acceleration, a.

Step 2: Select the appropriate equation.

Since u, v and t are known while a is unknown, use:

v = u + at

Rearranging gives:

a = (v − u)/t.

Step 3: Substitute the values.

a = (35 − 11)/8

= 24/8

= 3 m s⁻².

Step 4: State the answer.

The acceleration of the forklift is 3 m s⁻².

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (2 m s⁻²) is incorrect because it underestimates the rate of change of velocity and does not satisfy the given data.
Option B (2.5 m s⁻²) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect substitution or arithmetic error when applying the equation.
Option D (5.75 m s⁻²) is incorrect because it arises from using an incorrect relationship between the given quantities or dividing by the wrong value.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB questions involving the equations of motion, always begin by listing the known variables (u, v, a, t and s).

Then choose the equation that contains only the known quantities and the required unknown. This reduces unnecessary calculations and helps you avoid selecting the wrong formula.

Remember that the first equation of motion is the most suitable whenever displacement is not involved.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Acceleration tells us how quickly an object's speed changes.

The forklift's speed increases by:

35 − 11 = 24 m s⁻¹

This change happens over 8 seconds.

So,

Acceleration = 24 ÷ 8 = 3 m s⁻².

Hence, the correct answer is Option C.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion → First Equation of Motion (v = u + at) for a complete lesson on calculating acceleration, selecting the correct SUVAT equation and solving JAMB-style kinematics problems. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples, revision notes and exam-focused practice.

question: An airport baggage vehicle accelerates uniformly along a service lane. Its speed increases from 12 m s⁻¹ to 28 m s⁻¹ under a constant acceleration of 5 m s⁻². Assuming the acceleration remains constant throughout the motion, what distance does the vehicle travel during this interval?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the displacement of an object moving with constant acceleration when the time taken is not given. Since time is absent, you must identify and apply the appropriate equation of motion.
explanation.principle:
The equations of motion, also called the SUVAT equations, describe motion with constant acceleration.

The five quantities involved are:

• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity
• a = acceleration
• s = displacement
• t = time

When time is not provided, the most suitable equation is:

v² = u² + 2as

This equation directly relates velocity, acceleration and displacement without involving time.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial velocity, u = 12 m s⁻¹
Final velocity, v = 28 m s⁻¹
Acceleration, a = 5 m s⁻²
Displacement, s = ?

Step 2: Select the appropriate equation.

Since time is not given, use:

v² = u² + 2as.

Step 3: Substitute the values.

28² = 12² + 2 × 5 × s

784 = 144 + 10s.

Step 4: Solve for s.

784 − 144 = 10s

640 = 10s

s = 64 m.

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

The calculated displacement is 64 m.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (80 m) is incorrect. It results from an incorrect substitution or arithmetic error when applying the equation.
Option C (112 m) is incorrect. It may arise from using the wrong equation or squaring the wrong quantity.
Option D (128 m) is incorrect. It commonly results from doubling the correct displacement or mishandling the algebra.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a SUVAT question does not provide time, immediately think of the equation v² = u² + 2as. Before substituting values, always confirm that the acceleration is constant because the equations of motion are valid only under constant acceleration.
explanation.simple_explanation:
The vehicle's speed changes steadily, so the equations of motion can be used.

Since the time taken is unknown, the equation involving only velocity, acceleration and displacement is the best choice.

After substituting the given values, the distance travelled is 64 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion to learn how to select the correct SUVAT equation, solve displacement problems when time is absent, and practise additional JAMB-style worked examples. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for structured lessons and exam-focused practice.

question: During the final stage of a cycling race, a cyclist increases speed uniformly from 11 m s⁻¹ to 27 m s⁻¹ while maintaining constant acceleration. What is the cyclist's average velocity during this interval?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of how to calculate the average velocity of an object moving with constant acceleration. Rather than using displacement or time, you are required to apply the relationship between the initial and final velocities.
explanation.principle:
Before solving the problem, it is important to understand what average velocity means.

Average velocity is the total displacement divided by the total time taken.

For motion with constant acceleration, the velocity changes uniformly. Under this condition, the average velocity can be found by taking the arithmetic mean of the initial velocity and the final velocity.

The relationship is:

Average velocity = (u + v)/2

where:

• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity

This simplified relationship is valid only when the acceleration remains constant throughout the motion.

A beginner may ask, 'Why can we simply average the two velocities?'

This works because, under constant acceleration, the velocity increases by equal amounts in equal time intervals. Consequently, the midpoint between the initial and final velocities represents the average velocity over the entire interval.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial velocity, u = 11 m s⁻¹
Final velocity, v = 27 m s⁻¹

Step 2: Identify the quantity required.

Average velocity.

Step 3: Select the appropriate formula.

For constant acceleration:

Average velocity = (u + v)/2

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

Average velocity = (11 + 27)/2

= 38/2

Step 5: Perform the calculation.

Average velocity = 19 m s⁻¹

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (16 m s⁻¹) is incorrect. It results from an incorrect calculation of the average or the use of the wrong values.
Option B (18 m s⁻¹) is incorrect. This value may arise from an arithmetic error when adding or dividing the velocities.
Option D (22 m s⁻¹) is incorrect. It is not the arithmetic mean of the initial and final velocities and may result from using an inappropriate method.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question states that an object moves with constant or uniform acceleration and only the initial and final velocities are given, immediately recall:

Average velocity = (u + v)/2

Do not use this relationship if the acceleration is not constant unless specifically justified.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine a cyclist who starts this part of the race at 11 m s⁻¹ and gradually speeds up to 27 m s⁻¹ without changing the rate of acceleration.

Since the increase in speed is steady, the average velocity is simply halfway between the starting and ending velocities.

(11 + 27)/2 = 19 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion → Average Velocity under Constant Acceleration for a complete explanation of average velocity, its derivation, conditions for application, worked examples, and JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive concept reviews and examination-focused exercises.

question: A delivery motorcycle increases its speed uniformly from 5 m s⁻¹ to 29 m s⁻¹ while travelling along a straight road. If its constant acceleration is 4 m s⁻², how long does the motorcycle take to reach the final speed?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the time taken for an object moving with constant acceleration to increase its velocity from a known initial value to a known final value.
explanation.principle:
When an object moves with constant acceleration, its initial velocity (u), final velocity (v), acceleration (a) and time (t) are related by the first equation of motion:

v = u + at

This equation is used whenever displacement is not required.

Before using the equation, identify each variable:

• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity
• a = constant acceleration
• t = time taken

Since the question asks for time and provides the other three quantities, this is the most appropriate equation.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known quantities.

Initial velocity, u = 5 m s⁻¹
Final velocity, v = 29 m s⁻¹
Acceleration, a = 4 m s⁻²
Time, t = ?

Step 2: Select the appropriate equation.

v = u + at

Step 3: Make t the subject.

t = (v - u)/a

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

t = (29 - 5)/4

= 24/4

= 6 s

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

The correct option is B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (5 s) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect subtraction or arithmetic error.
Option C (7 s) is incorrect because it overestimates the time required and does not satisfy the equation v = u + at.
Option D (8 s) is incorrect because it gives a final velocity of 37 m s⁻¹ when substituted into the equation instead of the required 29 m s⁻¹.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever you are given initial velocity, final velocity and acceleration, and asked to determine time, immediately think of the first equation of motion:

v = u + at

Before substituting values, always identify the known and unknown variables. Choosing the correct SUVAT equation is often more important than the arithmetic itself.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine a delivery rider steadily increases speed without changing the rate of acceleration. If the motorcycle gains 24 m s⁻¹ in speed and the acceleration is 4 m s⁻², it gains 4 m s⁻¹ every second.

24 ÷ 4 = 6 seconds.

Therefore, it takes 6 seconds to reach the final speed.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion to learn how to identify the correct SUVAT equation, rearrange formulas, and solve JAMB-style kinematics problems involving velocity, acceleration, displacement and time. Continue practising with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and exam-focused exercises.

question:
An autonomous taxi is programmed to accelerate uniformly while merging onto a highway. During a diagnostic test, the onboard computer records the initial velocity, the final velocity and the time taken for the acceleration.

Which equation of motion should the computer use directly to determine the acceleration?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to select the most appropriate equation of motion based on the quantities already known. Instead of performing a calculation, you must identify the equation that directly relates the given variables.
explanation.principle:
The equations of motion, also called the SUVAT equations, describe motion with constant acceleration.

The five variables are:

• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity
• a = acceleration
• t = time
• s = displacement

Each equation is designed for a particular combination of known and unknown variables. Choosing the correct equation is often the most important step in solving a kinematics problem.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the quantities provided.

The autonomous taxi's computer knows:
• Initial velocity (u)
• Final velocity (v)
• Time (t)

The required quantity is acceleration (a).

Step 2: Look for the equation that contains only these variables.

The equation v = u + at relates u, v, a and t directly, without requiring displacement.

Step 3: Rearranging the equation gives:

a = (v - u)/t

Therefore, this equation is the most suitable choice.
Step 4: Conclude that Option A is correct because it directly connects all the known quantities with the unknown acceleration.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it relates displacement, initial velocity, acceleration and time. Final velocity is not included.
Option C is incorrect because it relates displacement, initial velocity, final velocity and acceleration, but it does not include time.
Option D is incorrect because it is used to determine displacement from average velocity under constant acceleration. It cannot directly determine acceleration.
explanation.exam_tip: Before substituting values into any SUVAT equation, first list the known quantities and identify the unknown. Then choose the equation containing all the known variables and excluding unnecessary ones. This strategy prevents using the wrong formula.
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of the equations of motion as different tools in a toolbox. If you already know the starting speed, ending speed and time, the correct tool is the first equation of motion because it directly links these quantities to acceleration.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion to learn how to identify known variables, choose the correct SUVAT equation, rearrange formulas, and solve JAMB-style kinematics problems. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and guided practice.

question:
A railway engineer reviews the motion of a passenger train using a velocity–time graph. The graph is described as follows:

• At time 0 s, the train is moving at 12 m s⁻¹.
• The velocity increases uniformly along a straight line to 42 m s⁻¹ after 10 s.

Based on the graph, what is the acceleration of the train during this interval?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to interpret a velocity–time graph described in words and determine the train's acceleration from the graph.
explanation.principle:
A velocity–time graph shows how velocity changes with time.

The gradient (slope) of a velocity–time graph represents the acceleration of the object.

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time and is given by:

a = (v − u)/t

where:

• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity
• t = time taken
• a = acceleration

Whenever the graph is a straight line, the acceleration is constant because the velocity changes uniformly with time.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial velocity, u = 12 m s⁻¹
Final velocity, v = 42 m s⁻¹
Time, t = 10 s

Step 2: Select the appropriate relationship.

Since the graph provides initial velocity, final velocity and time, use:

a = (v − u)/t

Step 3: Substitute the values.

a = (42 − 12)/10

= 30/10

= 3.0 m s⁻²

Step 4: Compare with the options.

The calculated acceleration is 3.0 m s⁻².

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (2.0 m s⁻²) results from an incorrect calculation of the change in velocity or division.
Option B (2.5 m s⁻²) is a plausible error obtained by using an incorrect time interval or arithmetic.
Option D (3.5 m s⁻²) overestimates the acceleration and does not agree with the slope of the described graph.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB, whenever a velocity–time graph is presented, remember:

• Gradient (slope) = acceleration.
• Area under the graph = displacement.

Always identify what the question is asking before choosing a formula.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine watching a train steadily increase its speed. If its speed rises from 12 m s⁻¹ to 42 m s⁻¹ in 10 seconds, the speed has increased by 30 m s⁻¹.

Since this increase happens evenly over 10 seconds, the train gains 3 m s⁻¹ of speed every second.

That means its acceleration is 3.0 m s⁻².
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion → Velocity–Time Graphs to learn how to determine acceleration from the gradient and displacement from the area under the graph, with additional JAMB-style worked examples available in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
A conveyor robot in an automated warehouse increases its speed uniformly. The velocity–time graph describing its motion is a straight line rising uniformly from 5 m s⁻¹ at 0 s to 17 m s⁻¹ at 8 s.

Using the information from the graph, what displacement does the robot cover during the 8-second interval?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to determine displacement from a velocity–time graph. Rather than reading the distance directly, you must recognise that the displacement is represented by the area under the velocity–time graph.
explanation.principle:
A velocity–time graph shows how velocity changes with time.

One of the most important facts in kinematics is that the area under a velocity–time graph represents the displacement of the object.

When the graph is a straight line, the area forms a trapezium.

The area of a trapezium is given by:

Area = ½ × (sum of parallel sides) × height

For a velocity–time graph:

• Parallel sides are the initial and final velocities.
• Height is the time interval.

Hence,

s = ½(u + v)t

where:

u = initial velocity,
v = final velocity,
t = time,
s = displacement.

This equation applies only when the acceleration is constant, giving a straight-line velocity–time graph.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

Initial velocity, u = 5 m s⁻¹
Final velocity, v = 17 m s⁻¹
Time, t = 8 s

Step 2: Select the appropriate relationship.

Since the motion is represented by a straight-line velocity–time graph, use:

s = ½(u + v)t

Step 3: Substitute the values.

s = ½(5 + 17) × 8

= ½ × 22 × 8

= 11 × 8

= 88 m

Step 4: Compare with the options.

The calculated displacement is 88 m.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (72 m) is incorrect. It results from using an incorrect average velocity or making an arithmetic error.
Option C (96 m) is incorrect. It may be obtained by incorrectly treating the final velocity as the average velocity throughout the motion.
Option D (136 m) is incorrect. It results from multiplying the final velocity directly by the time instead of finding the area under the graph.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB presents a velocity–time graph, immediately remember:

• Gradient = acceleration.
• Area under the graph = displacement.

For a straight-line graph, the area is that of a trapezium, leading to the equation:

s = ½(u + v)t.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine the conveyor robot gradually speeds up while carrying packages.

Since its speed is increasing steadily, it does not travel at just its starting speed or its ending speed throughout the journey. Instead, its average speed is halfway between the two speeds.

Average velocity = (5 + 17)/2 = 11 m s⁻¹

Distance travelled = Average velocity × Time = 11 × 8 = 88 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion → Velocity–Time Graphs to learn how gradients represent acceleration, areas represent displacement, and how these ideas connect to the SUVAT equations. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional JAMB-style worked examples and graph interpretation practice.

question:
During an emergency response exercise, a rescue vehicle begins moving at an initial velocity of 3 m s⁻¹ and accelerates uniformly at 5 m s⁻² for 7 s before reaching the incident location.

What total displacement does the rescue vehicle cover during this interval?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to solve a multi-step kinematics problem involving displacement during uniformly accelerated motion. You are given the initial velocity, acceleration and time, and you are required to determine the displacement covered.
explanation.principle:
When an object moves with constant acceleration, its displacement can be calculated using the second equation of motion:

s = ut + ½at²

where:

u = initial velocity (m s⁻¹)
a = constant acceleration (m s⁻²)
t = time (s)
s = displacement (m)

This equation is appropriate because the final velocity is not required.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial velocity, u = 3 m s⁻¹
Acceleration, a = 5 m s⁻²
Time, t = 7 s

Step 2: Write the appropriate equation.

s = ut + ½at²

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

s = (3 × 7) + ½ × 5 × 7²

Step 4: Perform the calculations.

3 × 7 = 21
7² = 49
½ × 5 × 49 = 122.5

Therefore,

s = 21 + 122.5 = 143.5 m

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

The displacement is 143.5 m.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (101.5 m) results from an incorrect substitution or omission of part of the acceleration term.
Option B (122.5 m) is obtained if only the term ½at² is calculated while ignoring the displacement due to the initial velocity (ut).
Option D (266 m) is obtained through an incorrect application of the formula or by adding unrelated quantities.
explanation.exam_tip: Before substituting into any SUVAT equation, list the known variables (u, v, a, t and s). Then choose the equation that excludes the unknown quantity you do not need. This greatly reduces calculation errors in JAMB examinations.
explanation.simple_explanation:
The rescue vehicle was already moving at 3 m s⁻¹ before it accelerated. Therefore, its total displacement comes from two contributions:

• the distance covered because of its initial speed (ut), and
• the extra distance gained due to acceleration (½at²).

Adding these two distances gives 143.5 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion → Calculating Displacement Using s = ut + ½at² for a step-by-step explanation of uniformly accelerated motion, worked JAMB-style examples, and additional practice questions in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
An airport operations manager observes that an airport shuttle increases its speed by the same amount every 5 seconds while travelling in a straight line.

Which conclusion best describes the shuttle's motion?

options.A: The shuttle is moving with constant acceleration.
options.B: The shuttle is moving with constant velocity.
options.C: The shuttle covers equal distances every second.

answer_text: The shuttle is moving with constant acceleration.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of what constant acceleration means in practical situations. Instead of carrying out calculations, you are required to interpret a real-life description of motion.
explanation.principle:
Acceleration is the rate at which velocity changes with time.

When an object's velocity changes by equal amounts in equal intervals of time, the object is said to have constant (uniform) acceleration.

A beginner may ask, 'Does constant acceleration mean constant speed?'

No.

Constant acceleration means the speed changes steadily. The speed may be increasing or decreasing, but the amount of change during each equal time interval remains the same.

The equations of motion (SUVAT equations) apply only when acceleration remains constant.

Therefore, recognising constant acceleration from a description of motion is an important skill before selecting or applying any SUVAT equation.
steps:
Step 1: Examine the description carefully.

The shuttle increases its speed by the same amount every 5 seconds.

Step 2: Interpret the meaning.

Equal changes in speed during equal intervals of time indicate that the acceleration is constant.

Step 3: Evaluate each option.

Option A correctly describes constant acceleration.

Option B is incorrect because constant velocity means both speed and direction remain unchanged.

Option C is incorrect because equal distances every second describe uniform velocity, not accelerating motion.

Option D is incorrect because the question states the increase in speed is steady, not that the acceleration itself is increasing.

Step 4: Select the best conclusion.

The shuttle is undergoing constant acceleration.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because a constantly increasing speed means the velocity is changing, so the velocity cannot be constant.
Option C is incorrect because an accelerating object generally does not cover equal distances in equal time intervals.
Option D is incorrect because increasing acceleration would produce larger and larger changes in speed during equal time intervals, which is not described in the question.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a JAMB question states that speed or velocity changes by equal amounts in equal intervals of time, immediately think of constant acceleration.

Remember:

• Constant velocity → no acceleration.
• Constant acceleration → velocity changes uniformly.
• SUVAT equations are valid only when acceleration is constant.
explanation.final_answer: A. The shuttle is moving with constant acceleration.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pressing the accelerator of a shuttle so smoothly that its speed increases by exactly the same amount every few seconds.

Since the increase in speed is regular, the shuttle has constant acceleration. This is the type of motion described by the equations of motion.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion to learn how constant acceleration is identified from motion descriptions before selecting the appropriate SUVAT equation. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional JAMB-style worked examples and concept reviews.

question:
An engineering apprentice is testing the motion of a prototype inspection vehicle in a laboratory. During one trial, the initial velocity, final velocity, acceleration and displacement are known, but the time taken is not measured.

Which of the following equations of motion is the most appropriate for determining the relationship among the known quantities without involving time?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to select the most appropriate SUVAT equation based on the quantities provided. Instead of performing a calculation, you must recognise which equation connects the known variables without introducing an unknown variable that is unavailable.
explanation.principle:
The equations of motion (SUVAT equations) describe motion with constant acceleration.

Before using any equation, always identify:

• the quantities that are known,
• the quantity to be determined, and
• any quantity that is unavailable.

The five standard SUVAT variables are:

• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity
• a = acceleration
• t = time
• s = displacement

Each equation of motion contains a different combination of these variables. Choosing the correct equation is often more important than carrying out the arithmetic.

The three principal equations are:

• First equation: v = u + at
• Second equation: s = ut + ½at²
• Third equation: v² = u² + 2as

Notice that only the third equation does not contain time (t). Therefore, it is the preferred equation whenever time is unknown or unavailable.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

The question states that the initial velocity (u), final velocity (v), acceleration (a) and displacement (s) are known.

Step 2: Identify the missing quantity.

The time (t) was not measured and is therefore unavailable.

Step 3: Examine each equation.

Option A: v = u + at

This equation contains time (t), which is unavailable. Therefore, it is not the best choice.

Step 4: Examine Option B.

s = ut + ½at²

This equation also contains time (t), so it cannot be used directly without first determining time.

Step 5: Examine Option C.

v² = u² + 2as

This equation contains only u, v, a and s.

It does not require time.

Therefore, it is exactly suited to the information provided.

Step 6: Examine Option D.

s = ((u + v)/2)t

Although this equation relates displacement and average velocity, it still requires time (t), which is unavailable.

Step 7: Select the correct answer.

Only Option C relates all the known quantities without involving time.

Therefore, Option C is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it requires the time taken for the motion. Since time is not available, the equation cannot be applied directly.
Option B is incorrect because it also depends on time. Applying it would first require determining time using another equation.
Option D is incorrect because average velocity multiplied by time gives displacement only when the time is known. Since time is missing, this equation is unsuitable.
common_misconceptions: Many students memorise the equations without understanding when each should be used.
Some candidates wrongly believe any SUVAT equation can be applied as long as the motion has constant acceleration.
A common examination mistake is choosing an equation that contains an unknown variable that is not given in the question.
explanation.exam_tip:
Before substituting any values into a formula, write down the known variables and identify the missing one.

Then choose the equation that contains only the known quantities and the required quantity.

A useful memory aid is:

• If time is missing, immediately consider the equation:

v² = u² + 2as
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine a laboratory technician records how fast a vehicle started, how fast it ended, how far it travelled and its acceleration, but forgets to record the time.

Instead of trying to estimate the missing time, the technician simply uses the only SUVAT equation that does not contain time:

v² = u² + 2as.

That is why Option C is the correct answer.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion → Selecting the Appropriate SUVAT Equation to learn how to identify known and unknown variables, choose the correct equation for different situations, and solve authentic JAMB-style questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for guided lessons, worked examples and comprehensive practice.

question:
During a robotics workshop, four trainee technicians discussed the equations of motion used to programme the movement of an automated guided vehicle.

Technician I: "The equations of motion are applicable only when the acceleration remains constant."

Technician II: "Every equation of motion contains all five SUVAT variables."

Technician III: "The equation v² = u² + 2as is particularly useful when time is not given."

Technician IV: "Negative acceleration may be used in the equations of motion to represent deceleration."

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question evaluates your understanding of the assumptions, limitations and correct application of the SUVAT equations rather than your ability to substitute values into formulas.
explanation.principle:
The equations of motion, commonly called the SUVAT equations, describe motion with constant acceleration.

The five variables used are:

• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity
• a = acceleration
• s = displacement
• t = time

Although five variables exist, each equation contains only some of them. This allows a suitable equation to be selected depending on the known and unknown quantities.

For example:

• v = u + at (does not contain displacement)
• s = ut + ½at² (does not contain final velocity)
• v² = u² + 2as (does not contain time)
• s = ((u + v)/2)t (uses average velocity for constant acceleration)

These equations are valid only when acceleration remains constant throughout the motion.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Technician I's statement.

The SUVAT equations are derived for uniformly accelerated motion. If acceleration changes continuously, these equations cannot be applied directly.

Therefore, Technician I is correct.

Step 2: Examine Technician II's statement.

This statement is incorrect.

No single equation of motion contains all five SUVAT variables. Each equation deliberately omits one variable so that it can be selected for different problem situations.

Step 3: Examine Technician III's statement.

The equation v² = u² + 2as does not include time. It is therefore the appropriate equation whenever time is unknown or unnecessary.

Technician III is correct.

Step 4: Examine Technician IV's statement.

Acceleration can be positive or negative. When an object slows down, its acceleration is negative relative to the chosen positive direction. This is commonly called deceleration.

Technician IV is correct.

Step 5: Determine the correct combination.

Technicians I, III and IV are correct, while Technician II is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Technician II, whose statement is false. The SUVAT equations do not all contain every variable.
Option C is incorrect because Technician II is incorrect, even though Technician IV is correct. It also omits Technicians I and III, which are correct.
Option D is incorrect because it includes Technician II, whose incorrect statement makes the combination invalid.
explanation.exam_tip:
When solving JAMB questions on equations of motion, first identify the known and unknown variables before choosing a formula.

Remember these key facts:

• SUVAT equations require constant acceleration.
• Not every equation contains every variable.
• v² = u² + 2as is the equation to use when time is absent.
• Negative acceleration represents deceleration.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, III and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Think of the SUVAT equations as different tools in a toolbox.

Each tool is designed for a particular job, so no single equation contains every variable. If the motion has constant acceleration, you simply choose the equation that matches the information given. When an object slows down, its acceleration is negative, which represents deceleration.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion to learn how each SUVAT equation is derived, when to use it, how to identify the appropriate formula from the given variables, and how to solve JAMB-style problems involving constant acceleration.

question:
During an emergency response drill, an autonomous rescue vehicle applies its braking system uniformly as it approaches a simulated accident scene. The vehicle is travelling at an initial speed of 32 m s⁻¹ and comes to rest after covering a distance of 64 m.

Assuming the deceleration remains constant throughout the braking process, what is the vehicle's acceleration?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to apply the third equation of motion to determine the constant acceleration (deceleration) of a vehicle that comes to rest over a known distance.
explanation.principle:
The equations of motion, also called the SUVAT equations, describe motion under constant acceleration.

Before choosing a formula, identify the known quantities.

Known:
• Initial velocity (u)
• Final velocity (v)
• Displacement (s)

Unknown:
• Acceleration (a)

Since time is not given, the appropriate equation is:

v² = u² + 2as

where:
• v = final velocity
• u = initial velocity
• a = acceleration
• s = displacement

When an object slows down, the acceleration is negative because it acts opposite to the direction of motion. This negative acceleration is called deceleration.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known values.

u = 32 m s⁻¹
v = 0 m s⁻¹
s = 64 m

Step 2: Select the appropriate equation.

Since time is not provided, use:

v² = u² + 2as

Step 3: Substitute the values.

0² = 32² + 2(a)(64)

0 = 1024 + 128a

Step 4: Solve for a.

128a = -1024

a = -1024 ÷ 128

a = -8 m s⁻²

Step 5: Verify the result.

The negative sign indicates that the vehicle is slowing down uniformly, which agrees with the braking situation described.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (−6 m s⁻²) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect substitution or arithmetic error when solving the equation.
Option C (−4 m s⁻²) is incorrect because it underestimates the braking rate and does not satisfy the equation v² = u² + 2as.
Option D (−2 m s⁻²) is incorrect because it represents a much smaller deceleration than required for the vehicle to stop within 64 m.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a motion question provides the initial velocity, final velocity and displacement but omits time, immediately think of the equation:

v² = u² + 2as.

Also remember that braking or slowing down is represented by a negative acceleration because it acts opposite to the direction of motion.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine riding in a vehicle that suddenly applies its brakes. The speed keeps reducing until the vehicle stops.

The rate at which the speed decreases is called the deceleration.

Since the question gives the starting speed, stopping speed and stopping distance, the third equation of motion is the quickest way to find the deceleration.

The calculation gives −8 m s⁻², showing that the vehicle loses 8 m s⁻¹ of speed every second.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion → Third Equation of Motion (v² = u² + 2as) for a complete lesson on selecting the appropriate SUVAT equation, solving braking problems, interpreting negative acceleration and practising additional JAMB-style questions. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed worked examples and examination tips.

question: A railway engineer observes that a high-speed train applies its braking system and continues moving forward while its speed decreases steadily at a constant rate. Which statement best describes the train's acceleration during this motion?

options.A: The train has positive acceleration because it is still moving forward.
options.B: The train has zero acceleration because its direction of motion has not changed.
options.C: The train has negative acceleration because its velocity decreases uniformly with time.
options.D: The train has increasing acceleration because the braking force opposes the motion.

answer_text: The train has negative acceleration because its velocity decreases uniformly with time.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of negative acceleration (also called deceleration) in uniformly accelerated motion. You are required to identify what happens to the acceleration of an object whose speed decreases steadily while it continues moving in the same direction.
explanation.principle:
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time.

Velocity includes both speed and direction. Whenever the velocity of an object decreases uniformly while it continues moving in the same direction, the acceleration acts opposite to the direction of motion. This is represented by a negative value when the forward direction is chosen as positive.

Negative acceleration is commonly called deceleration or retardation. It does not necessarily mean the object is moving backwards; it simply means the velocity is decreasing in the chosen positive direction.

The first equation of motion,

v = u + at,

shows that if the final velocity (v) is less than the initial velocity (u) during forward motion, the acceleration (a) must be negative.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what happens to the train.

The train continues moving forward, so its direction remains unchanged.

Step 2: Observe the change in speed.

Its speed decreases steadily, meaning its velocity decreases at a constant rate.

Step 3: Interpret the acceleration.

A steady decrease in velocity indicates constant negative acceleration (deceleration).

Step 4: Select the correct statement.

Only Option C correctly explains that the train experiences negative acceleration because its velocity decreases uniformly with time.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because moving forward does not automatically imply positive acceleration. An object can move forward while slowing down.
Option B is incorrect because acceleration depends on changes in velocity, not merely changes in direction. Since the speed is decreasing, the acceleration is not zero.
Option D is incorrect because the question states that the speed decreases steadily, indicating constant negative acceleration rather than increasing acceleration.
explanation.exam_tip:
A common JAMB misconception is to think that acceleration is always positive.

Remember:

• Increasing speed in the positive direction → Positive acceleration.
• Decreasing speed in the positive direction → Negative acceleration (deceleration).
• Constant speed in a straight line → Zero acceleration.

Always focus on how the velocity changes, not simply whether the object is moving.
explanation.final_answer: C. The train has negative acceleration because its velocity decreases uniformly with time.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine riding in a train that is approaching a station. Even though the train is still moving forward, it gradually slows down after the brakes are applied.

Because its speed is reducing steadily, the train has negative acceleration, also known as deceleration.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion → Constant Acceleration and Deceleration for a detailed explanation of positive and negative acceleration, braking motion, SUVAT equations, and JAMB-style worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional concept reviews and examination practice.

question:
A cargo drone is launched from rest and accelerates uniformly at 4 m s⁻² for 11 s along its take-off rail.

What final velocity does the drone attain at the end of the acceleration period?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the final velocity of an object that starts from rest and moves with constant acceleration using the first equation of motion.
explanation.principle:
When an object moves with constant acceleration, its initial velocity (u), final velocity (v), acceleration (a) and time (t) are related by the first equation of motion:

v = u + at

Before using this equation, it is important to understand each variable:

• u = initial velocity (speed at the beginning)
• v = final velocity (speed at the end)
• a = constant acceleration
• t = time taken

The equation applies only when the acceleration remains constant throughout the motion.

The phrase 'starts from rest' means that the initial velocity is zero.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial velocity, u = 0 m s⁻¹
Acceleration, a = 4 m s⁻²
Time, t = 11 s

Step 2: Identify the required quantity.

Required: Final velocity, v

Step 3: Select the appropriate equation.

Since displacement is not required, use:

v = u + at

Step 4: Substitute the given values.

v = 0 + (4 × 11)

v = 44 m s⁻¹

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

The calculated final velocity is 44 m s⁻¹.

Therefore, the correct option is B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (40 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect multiplication or substitution into the equation.
Option C (48 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it overestimates the increase in velocity and does not satisfy v = u + at for the given values.
Option D (52 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it assumes a larger acceleration or longer time than stated in the question.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a question states that an object 'starts from rest', immediately write u = 0 m s⁻¹.

Also identify which SUVAT variables are given before choosing an equation. If displacement is not involved, the first equation of motion, v = u + at, is usually the simplest choice.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine pushing a trolley that is initially stationary. As you keep pushing with a constant force, its speed increases steadily. Since the cargo drone starts from rest and accelerates uniformly, its speed after 11 seconds is found by adding the increase in speed (4 × 11 = 44) to its initial speed of zero.

Therefore, its final velocity is 44 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion → First Equation of Motion (v = u + at) for a complete explanation of constant acceleration, worked examples, common mistakes, and additional JAMB-style practice. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive revision and exam-focused questions.

question:
An automated warehouse robot begins moving along a straight track with an initial velocity of 9 m s⁻¹. It accelerates uniformly at 3 m s⁻² for 8 s while transporting a package.

What displacement does the robot cover during this time?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the displacement of an object moving with constant acceleration when its initial velocity, acceleration and time are known.
explanation.principle:
When an object moves with constant acceleration, its displacement can be determined using the second equation of motion:

s = ut + ½at²

where:

• s = displacement (m)
• u = initial velocity (m s⁻¹)
• a = constant acceleration (m s⁻²)
• t = time (s)

This equation is particularly useful when the final velocity is not required. The first part (ut) represents the distance that would have been covered if the object continued at its initial velocity, while the second part (½at²) accounts for the additional displacement due to acceleration.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

u = 9 m s⁻¹

a = 3 m s⁻²

t = 8 s

Step 2: Write the appropriate equation.

s = ut + ½at²

Step 3: Substitute the values.

s = (9 × 8) + ½ × 3 × (8²)

Step 4: Perform the calculations.

9 × 8 = 72

8² = 64

½ × 3 × 64 = 96

Therefore,

s = 72 + 96 = 168 m

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

The correct option is A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B (144 m) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect evaluation of the acceleration term.
Option C (120 m) is incorrect because it underestimates the contribution of acceleration and often arises from omitting the factor ½ incorrectly.
Option D (192 m) is incorrect because it overestimates the displacement by incorrectly multiplying the acceleration term.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever u, a and t are given and displacement is required, the first equation of motion is not appropriate. Immediately think of:

s = ut + ½at²

Always square the time before multiplying by the acceleration, and never forget the factor ½.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine the warehouse robot moving at 9 m s⁻¹ and gradually speeding up every second. Because it keeps accelerating, it covers more distance than it would have at a constant speed. Using the displacement equation for constant acceleration gives a total distance of 168 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion → Calculating Displacement Using s = ut + ½at² for detailed explanations, worked examples and additional JAMB-style practice. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive lessons and revision exercises.

question:
A maintenance engineer is testing an industrial inspection robot that moves in a straight line with constant acceleration. At the beginning of the test, the robot travels at 12 m s⁻¹. Eight seconds later, its speed has increased to 28 m s⁻¹.

Without first calculating the acceleration separately, which expression can be used directly to determine the displacement covered during the 8 s interval?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to analyse the information provided and select the most appropriate equation of motion rather than perform lengthy calculations. It focuses on recognising which SUVAT equation contains only the known quantities.
explanation.principle:
The equations of motion (SUVAT equations) describe motion with constant acceleration.

The five standard variables are:

• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity
• a = acceleration
• t = time
• s = displacement

A key examination skill is identifying the known and unknown quantities before choosing an equation.

When the initial velocity, final velocity and time are known, the equation

s = (u + v)t/2

is the most direct choice because it does not require the acceleration to be calculated first.

This equation is obtained from the fact that, under constant acceleration,

Average velocity = (u + v)/2

and

Displacement = Average velocity × Time.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Initial velocity, u = 12 m s⁻¹
Final velocity, v = 28 m s⁻¹
Time, t = 8 s

The required quantity is displacement, s.

Step 2: Examine the available equations.

Option A: s = (u + v)t/2
Contains only u, v and t, all of which are already known.

Therefore, it can be used immediately.

Step 3: Consider the remaining options.

Option B requires the correct form s = ut + ½at². The equation given omits the ½ and still requires the acceleration.

Option C is incomplete because the correct equation is v² = u² + 2as.

Option D is incorrect because the first equation of motion is v = u + at, not v = u + at².

Step 4: Conclude.

Since no separate calculation of acceleration is needed, the correct expression is

s = (u + v)t/2.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it omits the factor ½ in the second equation of motion and also requires the acceleration, which the question asks you to avoid calculating first.
Option C is incorrect because the correct third equation of motion is v² = u² + 2as. The expression given is incomplete.
Option D is incorrect because the first equation of motion is v = u + at. The time is not squared.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB, many questions assess equation selection rather than arithmetic. Always list the known variables first, then choose the equation containing exactly those variables and the unknown quantity. This saves time and reduces calculation errors.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine you know how fast a robot was moving at the beginning, how fast it was moving at the end and how long the journey lasted.

Since the acceleration is constant, you can first find its average speed using

(u + v)/2

Then multiply by the time taken to obtain the distance travelled. That is why Option A is the best choice.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Equations of Motion for a complete explanation of the four SUVAT equations, how to choose the correct equation, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice on equation selection, multi-step kinematics and examination strategies.

question:
A manufacturing engineer in a smart factory monitors the motion of an automated material-handling vehicle. The recorded velocity–time graph is described as follows:

• At time t = 0 s, the vehicle is moving at 12 m s⁻¹.
• Its velocity increases uniformly in a straight-line manner.
• At time t = 8 s, the velocity reaches 44 m s⁻¹.

Based on the information from the graph, what is the constant acceleration of the vehicle?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to interpret a velocity–time graph described in words and determine the constant acceleration of an automated vehicle. Instead of applying a memorised SUVAT equation directly, you must recognise that the slope (gradient) of a velocity–time graph represents acceleration.
explanation.principle:
Before solving the question, it is important to understand what a velocity–time graph represents.

A velocity–time graph shows how the velocity of an object changes with time.

A beginner may ask, 'How can acceleration be obtained from such a graph?'
