Option C correctly states that the object experiences constant downward acceleration throughout the entire motion, including at the highest point.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the acceleration due to gravity does not become zero at the highest point. Only the velocity becomes zero momentarily.
Option B is incorrect because the sensor slows down uniformly while moving upward. Its speed is not constant.
Option D is incorrect because gravity acts immediately after projection and continues acting throughout the upward and downward motion.
common_misconceptions: Confusing zero velocity with zero acceleration.
Assuming gravity acts only while the object is falling.
Believing a straight-line velocity-time graph represents constant velocity instead of constant acceleration.
Thinking acceleration begins only after the object starts descending.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB, remember that the slope of a velocity-time graph represents acceleration.

For vertical motion under gravity:

• Straight line → constant acceleration.
• Negative slope → downward acceleration.
• Velocity equals zero at maximum height.
• Acceleration remains equal to g at every stage of the motion.
explanation.final_answer: C. The sensor is under constant downward acceleration throughout the motion, including at the highest point.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine throwing a ball straight upward.

As it rises, it slows down because gravity is pulling it downward. At the highest point, the ball stops for a brief instant before falling back down.

Even though its velocity is zero at that instant, gravity has never stopped acting. This is why the acceleration remains constant throughout the entire motion.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity → Velocity-Time Graphs for detailed lessons on interpreting velocity-time graphs, identifying acceleration from graph gradients, understanding sign conventions, and solving JAMB-style graph interpretation questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and examination-focused practice.

subtopic: Motion under Gravity - Comparison of Upward and Downward Velocities
question:
During a cliff observation exercise, two environmental scientists neglect air resistance while studying the motion of a small sensor. The sensor is projected vertically upward from the edge of the cliff and later passes the same point on its way downward.

Which statement correctly compares the sensor's motion as it passes that same point?

options.A: The downward speed is equal to the upward speed, but the directions are opposite.
options.B: The downward speed is greater than the upward speed because gravity increases its speed continuously.
options.C: The upward speed is greater than the downward speed because energy is lost during ascent.
options.D: The downward speed is zero because the sensor has already reached its highest point.

answer_text: The downward speed is equal to the upward speed, but the directions are opposite.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of how the velocity of an object moving under gravity compares when it passes the same position during its upward and downward journeys, assuming air resistance is negligible.
explanation.principle:
When an object is projected vertically upward and air resistance is neglected, gravity is the only force acting on it. Gravity causes the object to slow down uniformly as it rises and speed up uniformly as it falls.

Because mechanical energy is conserved, the object has the same speed whenever it passes the same height. However, the direction of motion changes.

Remember that:

• Speed is the magnitude of velocity.
• Velocity includes both magnitude and direction.
• At the same vertical position, the magnitudes of the upward and downward velocities are equal, but their directions are opposite.
explanation.definitions:
Velocity is the rate of change of displacement and has both magnitude and direction.

Speed is the magnitude of velocity and has no direction.

Acceleration due to gravity (g) acts vertically downward at approximately 10 m s⁻² in most JAMB questions unless otherwise stated.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
As an object rises, gravity acts opposite to its motion, reducing its speed until it becomes momentarily zero at the highest point.

After reaching the highest point, gravity continues to act downward, causing the object to accelerate downward.

Therefore, whenever the object returns to a previously occupied height, its speed is the same as before, although its direction has reversed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the position being compared.

The sensor is compared at exactly the same point during its upward and downward motion.

Step 2: Recall the effect of gravity.

With negligible air resistance, gravity is the only force acting on the sensor throughout the motion.

Step 3: Compare the speeds.

Mechanical energy is conserved, so the speed at a particular height during ascent equals the speed at that same height during descent.

Step 4: Compare the directions.

During ascent, the velocity is upward.

During descent, the velocity is downward.

Thus, the velocities have equal magnitudes but opposite directions.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

Option A correctly states that the downward speed equals the upward speed, but the directions are opposite.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because although the object accelerates downward after reaching its highest point, its speed at the same height is not greater than it was during ascent when air resistance is neglected.
Option C is incorrect because the question assumes ideal motion with negligible air resistance, so no mechanical energy is lost.
Option D is incorrect because the speed is zero only at the highest point, not when the object passes the same point again during its descent.
common_misconceptions: Believing gravity makes the downward speed greater than the upward speed at the same height.
Confusing speed with velocity by ignoring direction.
Thinking acceleration due to gravity changes during the motion.
Assuming the object has zero speed everywhere after reaching its maximum height.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, whenever an object passes the same point during upward and downward motion under gravity and air resistance is neglected, remember:

• Speed is the same.
• Velocity is different because the direction changes.

Always distinguish between speed (magnitude only) and velocity (magnitude plus direction).
explanation.final_answer: A. The downward speed is equal to the upward speed, but the directions are opposite.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine throwing a ball straight upward.

As it comes back down and passes your hand again, it is moving just as fast as it was when it left your hand, provided air resistance is ignored. The only difference is that it is now moving downward instead of upward.

So the speeds are equal, but the velocities are opposite in direction.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity for a complete lesson on vertical projection, sign convention, conservation of mechanical energy, comparison of upward and downward motion, and JAMB-style worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed explanations, concept summaries, and additional exam-focused practice.

question:
During an aerospace technician training session, four trainees made the following statements about motion under gravity near the Earth's surface, neglecting air resistance:

Statement I: 'An object projected vertically upward has zero acceleration when it reaches its maximum height because its velocity is zero.'

Statement II: 'The acceleration due to gravity remains constant and acts downward throughout the object's motion.'

Statement III: 'If upward is taken as the positive direction, the acceleration due to gravity is represented by a negative value.'

Statement IV: 'As an object falls freely toward the ground, its speed increases uniformly because gravity provides a constant downward acceleration.'

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question evaluates your understanding of the fundamental principles governing motion under gravity. Instead of performing calculations, you must identify which statements correctly describe the behaviour of objects moving vertically under the influence of gravity.
explanation.principle:
Motion under gravity is a special case of uniformly accelerated motion in which the only significant force acting on the object is Earth's gravitational pull.

The acceleration due to gravity, denoted by g, is approximately 9.8 m s⁻² (or 10 m s⁻² in many JAMB questions) and always acts vertically downward regardless of whether the object is moving upward, downward or is momentarily at rest.

It is important to distinguish between velocity and acceleration.

• Velocity describes how fast an object moves and in what direction.
• Acceleration describes how quickly the velocity changes.

At the highest point of a vertical projection, the velocity becomes zero for an instant, but gravity continues to act. Therefore, the acceleration remains equal to g downward.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

This statement is incorrect.

Although the object's velocity is zero at the maximum height, gravity continues to act downward. Therefore, the acceleration is not zero; it remains equal to g downward.

Step 2: Examine Statement II.

This statement is correct.

Near the Earth's surface and neglecting air resistance, gravitational acceleration remains essentially constant and is always directed downward throughout the motion.

Step 3: Examine Statement III.

This statement is correct.

If upward is chosen as the positive direction, then gravity acts in the opposite direction. Consequently, the acceleration due to gravity is written as -g.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

This statement is correct.

During free fall, gravity continuously accelerates the object downward at a constant rate, causing its speed to increase uniformly.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Statement I is false.

Statements II, III and IV are correct.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Statement I, which wrongly assumes that acceleration becomes zero at the highest point.
Option C is incorrect because Statement I is false, even though Statements III and IV are correct.
Option D is incorrect because it also includes Statement I, making the combination incorrect.
common_misconceptions: Confusing zero velocity with zero acceleration at the maximum height.
Believing gravity acts only while an object is falling.
Thinking the sign of acceleration changes simply because the object changes direction.
Assuming acceleration always has the same sign as velocity.
explanation.exam_tip:
A favourite JAMB test point is the highest point of vertical motion.

Remember:

• Velocity = 0 at the highest point.
• Acceleration = g downward at all times.

Never confuse an object's velocity with its acceleration. A zero velocity does not imply zero acceleration.
explanation.final_answer: A. II, III and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine throwing a ball straight upward.

As it rises, it slows down until it stops for a brief moment at the highest point. Even though its speed becomes zero at that instant, gravity never stops pulling it downward. That pull causes the ball to start falling back toward the ground.

Therefore, Statements II, III and IV are correct, while Statement I is incorrect because gravity still acts at the highest point.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity for a complete discussion of gravitational acceleration, sign convention, vertical projection, maximum height, free-fall motion and common JAMB misconceptions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed lessons, worked examples and additional JAMB-standard practice questions.

subtopic: Motion under Gravity - Velocity Before Impact
question:
During a rescue training exercise, an emergency signalling capsule accidentally slips from a platform located 80 m above the ground. The capsule falls freely from rest, and air resistance is neglected. Take the acceleration due to gravity as 10 m s⁻².

What is the speed of the capsule just before it strikes the ground?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to determine the velocity of an object immediately before impact using the equations of uniformly accelerated motion under gravity.
explanation.principle:
When an object falls freely under gravity, it experiences a constant downward acceleration. Since the acceleration remains constant, the motion is an example of uniformly accelerated motion, allowing the use of the standard SUVAT equations.

For problems where the time of motion is not given, the most appropriate equation is:

v² = u² + 2as

where:

• v = final velocity
• u = initial velocity
• a = acceleration
• s = displacement.

Because the capsule is released from rest, its initial velocity is zero.
explanation.definitions:
Free fall is the motion of an object under the influence of gravity alone, with air resistance neglected.

Velocity is a vector quantity describing the rate of change of displacement, while speed is the magnitude of velocity. Since the question asks for speed just before impact, only the magnitude of the final velocity is required.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
For objects released from rest:

• Initial velocity, u = 0.
• Acceleration due to gravity, g = 10 m s⁻² (unless otherwise stated).
• The object continuously gains speed because gravity acts in the same direction as its motion.

Whenever height and final speed are given or required without time, the third equation of motion is usually the most suitable.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

u = 0 m s⁻¹

a = 10 m s⁻²

s = 80 m

Step 2: Select the appropriate equation.

Since time is not given:

v² = u² + 2as

Step 3: Substitute the values.

v² = 0² + 2 × 10 × 80

v² = 1600

Step 4: Find the square root.

v = √1600

v = 40 m s⁻¹

Step 5: Compare with the options.

The correct option is 40 m s⁻¹.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (20 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it significantly underestimates the increase in speed produced by gravity over an 80 m fall.
Option B (30 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it results from an incorrect calculation or the misuse of a kinematic equation.
Option D (50 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it overestimates the final speed. Substituting the values correctly gives 40 m s⁻¹.
common_misconceptions: Using the first equation of motion without knowing the time.
Confusing height with time of fall.
Substituting an incorrect value for gravitational acceleration.
Forgetting that an object released from rest has an initial velocity of zero.
Neglecting to take the square root after calculating v².
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, when a freely falling object starts from rest and the time is not provided, immediately think of the equation:

v² = u² + 2gs

This often leads directly to the required answer with minimal calculation.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine dropping a tool from a tall platform. Gravity keeps pulling it downward, making it move faster and faster. After falling through 80 m, its speed just before hitting the ground is 40 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity → Velocity Before Impact for a comprehensive lesson on free fall, selecting the correct SUVAT equation, sign conventions, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided practice and revision.

question: A physics laboratory demonstrates the vertical projection of a small metal sphere. The sphere rises until it reaches its highest point before descending under the influence of gravity. Ignoring air resistance, which statement correctly describes the sphere at its maximum height?

options.A: Both its velocity and acceleration are zero.
options.B: Its velocity is zero, but its acceleration remains equal to the acceleration due to gravity acting downward.
options.C: Its acceleration is zero because the sphere is momentarily at rest.
options.D: Its velocity and acceleration are both directed upward.

answer_text: Its velocity is zero, but its acceleration remains equal to the acceleration due to gravity acting downward.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of what happens to the velocity and acceleration of an object at the highest point of its vertical motion under gravity.
explanation.principle:
When an object is projected vertically upward, gravity continuously acts on it throughout its motion. Gravity does not stop acting simply because the object reaches its highest point.

The upward velocity decreases uniformly because gravity acts in the opposite direction to the motion. At the highest point, the object's instantaneous velocity becomes zero before it begins to fall back down. However, its acceleration remains equal to the acceleration due to gravity, directed vertically downward.

Near the Earth's surface, this acceleration is approximately 9.8 m s⁻², but JAMB commonly uses 10 m s⁻² unless otherwise stated.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the quantity that becomes zero at the highest point.

The object momentarily stops rising before descending. Therefore, its instantaneous velocity is zero.

Step 2: Consider whether gravity stops acting.

Gravity acts continuously on the object throughout its upward journey, at the highest point, and during its downward motion.

Step 3: Determine the acceleration.

Since gravity is always present, the acceleration remains equal to g and is directed vertically downward.

Step 4: Evaluate the options.

Option B correctly states that the velocity is zero while the acceleration remains equal to the acceleration due to gravity acting downward.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because only the velocity becomes zero at the highest point. The acceleration due to gravity is not zero.
Option C is incorrect because being momentarily at rest does not mean that acceleration is zero. Gravity continues to act downward.
Option D is incorrect because at the highest point the velocity is zero, not upward, and the acceleration is downward rather than upward.
common_misconceptions: Confusing zero velocity with zero acceleration.
Assuming gravity stops acting when an object reaches its highest point.
Believing that an object must be moving for acceleration to exist.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a JAMB question refers to the highest point of a vertically projected object, remember this key idea:

• Velocity = 0.
• Acceleration = g downward.

This distinction is one of the most frequently tested concepts in motion under gravity.
explanation.final_answer: B. Its velocity is zero, but its acceleration remains equal to the acceleration due to gravity acting downward.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine throwing a ball straight upward. For a brief instant at the top, the ball stops moving before falling back down. Although its speed is zero at that moment, the Earth's gravity is still pulling it downward. That downward pull is the acceleration due to gravity.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity → Maximum Height for a complete explanation of vertical projection, velocity at the highest point, acceleration due to gravity, sign convention, and additional JAMB-style worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for beginner-friendly lessons, concept summaries, and exam-focused practice.

question:
An automated drop-test facility records the motion of a metal sphere released from rest. The velocity-time graph is described as follows:

• The graph begins at the origin.
• It is a straight line with a constant positive slope.
• The velocity increases uniformly as time increases.
• Air resistance is neglected.

Which conclusion about the sphere's motion is correct?

options.A: The sphere is moving with constant velocity because the graph is a straight line.
options.B: The sphere is accelerating uniformly under the influence of gravity.
options.C: The sphere experiences decreasing acceleration as it falls.
options.D: The sphere reaches terminal velocity shortly after it is released.

answer_text: The sphere is accelerating uniformly under the influence of gravity.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to interpret a described velocity-time graph for an object moving under gravity. Rather than performing calculations, you are expected to identify what the shape of the graph reveals about the motion of the falling object.
explanation.principle:
A velocity-time graph shows how an object's velocity changes with time.

The slope (gradient) of a velocity-time graph represents the object's acceleration.

For an object falling freely near the Earth's surface and neglecting air resistance:

• Gravity provides a constant downward acceleration.
• The acceleration is approximately 10 m s⁻² unless otherwise stated.
• Because the acceleration is constant, velocity changes uniformly with time.

A straight-line velocity-time graph therefore indicates constant acceleration.

If the graph starts from the origin, it means the object was released from rest and then gained speed uniformly under gravity.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what the graph represents.

The graph is a velocity-time graph, so its slope represents acceleration.

Step 2: Examine the shape of the graph.

The graph is a straight line with a constant positive slope.

A constant slope means the acceleration remains constant throughout the motion.

Step 3: Relate the graph to motion under gravity.

Since the object is released from rest and air resistance is neglected, gravity is the only significant force acting on it.

Gravity provides a constant acceleration, causing the velocity to increase uniformly with time.

Step 4: Select the correct conclusion.

The graph describes uniform acceleration due to gravity.

Therefore, Option B is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because a straight line on a velocity-time graph represents constant acceleration, not constant velocity. Constant velocity would appear as a horizontal line.
Option C is incorrect because the graph shows a constant slope. If the acceleration were decreasing, the graph would gradually become less steep rather than remaining straight.

Option D is incorrect because terminal velocity occurs only when air resistance becomes significant and balances the weight of the object. The question specifically states that air resistance is neglected.
common_misconceptions: Confusing a straight-line velocity-time graph with constant velocity instead of constant acceleration.
Believing that gravity becomes weaker during the fall near the Earth's surface.
Assuming every falling object quickly reaches terminal velocity even when air resistance is ignored.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB Physics, always remember the meaning of the gradient of different graphs.

• Gradient of a displacement-time graph = velocity.
• Gradient of a velocity-time graph = acceleration.

Whenever a velocity-time graph is a straight line with constant slope, think immediately of uniform acceleration.
explanation.final_answer: B. The sphere is accelerating uniformly under the influence of gravity.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine dropping a metal ball from a height.

As it falls, gravity continuously pulls it downward, causing its speed to increase at the same rate every second.

A straight-line velocity-time graph with a constant slope is exactly what we expect for this type of motion.

Therefore, the correct conclusion is that the sphere is accelerating uniformly under gravity.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity → Graph Interpretation for a complete lesson on interpreting displacement-time and velocity-time graphs, understanding gradients, recognising uniform acceleration, and solving JAMB-style graph questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and exam-focused practice.

question:
At an engineering research centre, two identical test capsules are projected vertically upward at the same instant from the same platform. Capsule P is launched with an initial speed of 20 m s⁻¹, while Capsule Q is launched with an initial speed of 30 m s⁻¹. Air resistance is neglected and take g = 10 m s⁻².

Which of the following statements correctly compares the motion of the two capsules?

options.A: Capsule Q reaches a greater maximum height and remains in the air longer than Capsule P.
options.B: Both capsules reach the same maximum height because they experience the same gravitational acceleration.
options.C: Capsule P reaches the ground after a longer time because it slows down less rapidly.
options.D: Both capsules reach their maximum height at the same time since gravity acts equally on them.

answer_text: Capsule Q reaches a greater maximum height and remains in the air longer than Capsule P.

explanation.question_restate: This question compares the vertical motion of two objects projected upward with different initial speeds. It tests whether you understand how the initial velocity influences the maximum height reached and the total time spent in the air, even though both objects experience the same acceleration due to gravity.
explanation.principle:
When an object is projected vertically upward, gravity acts downward throughout its motion with a constant acceleration of approximately 10 m s⁻² (unless stated otherwise).

The object with the greater initial velocity:

• takes a longer time to slow down to zero velocity,
• reaches a greater maximum height,
• spends a longer total time in the air if both are projected from and return to the same level.

The acceleration due to gravity is the same for both objects, but their different initial speeds produce different heights and flight times.
explanation.definitions:
Initial velocity (u) is the speed with which an object begins its motion.

Maximum height is the highest point reached during upward motion, where the instantaneous velocity becomes zero.

Time to maximum height is the time taken for the upward velocity to reduce to zero under the action of gravity.
steps:
Step 1: Compare the initial velocities.

Capsule P: u = 20 m s⁻¹

Capsule Q: u = 30 m s⁻¹

Since 30 m s⁻¹ is greater than 20 m s⁻¹, Capsule Q starts with more upward speed.

Step 2: Compare the time taken to reach the highest point.

Using

t = u/g

Capsule P:

t = 20/10 = 2 s

Capsule Q:

t = 30/10 = 3 s

Therefore, Capsule Q continues rising for a longer time.

Step 3: Compare the maximum heights.

Using

h = u²/(2g)

Capsule P:

h = 20²/(2 × 10) = 400/20 = 20 m

Capsule Q:

h = 30²/(2 × 10) = 900/20 = 45 m

Capsule Q reaches a greater maximum height.

Step 4: Compare the total time of flight.

For motion returning to the same level,

Time of flight = 2u/g

Capsule P:

2 × 20/10 = 4 s

Capsule Q:

2 × 30/10 = 6 s

Capsule Q remains in the air longer.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

Only Option A correctly describes both comparisons.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because although both capsules experience the same gravitational acceleration, different initial velocities produce different maximum heights.
Option C is incorrect because both capsules slow down at the same rate (10 m s⁻²). Capsule P reaches the ground first because it has the smaller initial velocity.
Option D is incorrect because the time to reach maximum height depends on the initial velocity. Since the initial velocities are different, the times are also different.
common_misconceptions: Believing that equal gravitational acceleration means equal maximum height.
Assuming objects projected upward always remain in the air for the same duration.
Confusing equal acceleration with equal velocity.
Thinking gravity affects faster-moving objects differently.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever JAMB compares two vertically projected objects, first compare their initial velocities.

If gravity is the same for both:

• the object with the larger initial velocity reaches a greater height,
• takes longer to reach the highest point,
• and remains in the air for a longer time if both return to the same level.

Remember that gravity changes the velocity at the same rate for both objects, but it does not eliminate the effect of different launch speeds.
explanation.final_answer: A. Capsule Q reaches a greater maximum height and remains in the air longer than Capsule P.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine throwing two identical balls straight upward. One is thrown gently while the other is thrown much harder.

Although gravity pulls both balls downward equally, the harder-thrown ball travels higher before stopping, takes longer to come back down, and therefore stays in the air for a longer time.

This is exactly what happens to Capsule Q.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity for a complete lesson on vertical projection, maximum height, time to maximum height, time of flight, sign convention, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice questions and beginner-friendly explanations.

question:
During a routine inspection of an underground mining shaft, an engineer launches a small electronic sensor vertically upward from the bottom of the shaft with an initial speed of 26 m s⁻¹. Neglecting air resistance and taking the acceleration due to gravity as 10 m s⁻² downward, another trainee makes the following statements after 2 s of the motion:

Statement I: The sensor is still moving upward.

Statement II: The sensor's velocity at that instant is 6 m s⁻¹ upward.

Statement III: The sensor has risen 32 m above its launching point.

Statement IV: The acceleration of the sensor at that instant is zero because its upward speed has decreased.

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question combines conceptual understanding with numerical reasoning. You are required to determine the sensor's velocity and displacement after 2 s and then decide which statements about its motion are correct.
explanation.principle:
An object projected vertically upward moves under constant gravitational acceleration. Gravity always acts downward throughout the motion, even while the object is moving upward.

The equations of uniformly accelerated motion (SUVAT) are therefore applicable.

The symbols used are:

• u = initial velocity
• v = final velocity after time t
• a = acceleration
• s = displacement
• t = time elapsed

For upward motion with upward taken as positive:

• a = -g = -10 m s⁻²
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

u = 26 m s⁻¹

a = -10 m s⁻²

t = 2 s

Step 2: Determine the velocity after 2 s using the first equation of motion.

v = u + at

v = 26 + (-10 × 2)

v = 26 - 20

v = 6 m s⁻¹ upward.

Therefore, Statement II is correct.

Since the velocity is still positive, the sensor is still moving upward.

Therefore, Statement I is also correct.

Step 3: Determine the displacement after 2 s using the second equation of motion.

s = ut + ½at²

s = (26 × 2) + ½(-10)(2²)

s = 52 - 20

s = 32 m.

Therefore, Statement III is correct.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

Although the sensor's upward speed has reduced, gravity continues to act downward throughout the motion.

Hence, the acceleration remains -10 m s⁻² and is never zero before, at, or after the highest point.

Statement IV is incorrect.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Statements I, II and III are correct, while Statement IV is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because Statement IV is false, while Statement II and Statement III are true.
Option C is incorrect because it includes Statement IV, which incorrectly assumes that acceleration becomes zero when the object slows down.
Option D is incorrect because Statement I and Statement II are also correct. The object is still moving upward with a velocity of 6 m s⁻¹.
common_misconceptions: Many students believe that acceleration becomes zero whenever an object slows down. Under gravity, the acceleration remains constant at g downward.
Some learners wrongly assume that reaching a lower speed means the object has stopped moving upward. The object continues upward until its velocity becomes zero.
Another common mistake is using +10 m s⁻² instead of -10 m s⁻² when upward is taken as the positive direction.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a question involves vertical motion, first decide the positive direction and apply that sign convention consistently. Remember that gravity always acts downward throughout the motion unless stated otherwise.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, II and III only
explanation.simple_explanation: The sensor is thrown upward at 26 m s⁻¹. After 2 seconds, gravity has reduced its speed by 20 m s⁻¹, leaving it moving upward at 6 m s⁻¹. During that time it has travelled 32 m upward. Even though it is slowing down, gravity is still acting downward with an acceleration of 10 m s⁻², so the acceleration is not zero.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity to study vertical projection, sign convention, constant gravitational acceleration, displacement, velocity-time relationships, and fully worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for comprehensive concept reviews and additional practice.

subtopic: Motion under Gravity - Reverse Calculation of Initial Velocity
question: During a sports science experiment, an athlete throws a training ball vertically upward. The ball comes momentarily to rest at its highest point after 2.8 s. Assuming the acceleration due to gravity is 10 m s⁻² and neglecting air resistance, what was the initial velocity of the ball?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the initial velocity of a ball projected vertically upward when the time taken to reach its maximum height is known. Instead of calculating the final velocity, you are working backwards to find the unknown initial velocity.
explanation.principle:
When an object is projected vertically upward, gravity acts continuously downward with a constant acceleration. As a result, the object's upward velocity decreases uniformly until it becomes zero at the highest point.

The motion is still an example of uniformly accelerated motion, so the standard equations of motion apply.

The first equation of motion is:

v = u + at

where:

• v = final velocity (m s⁻¹)
• u = initial velocity (m s⁻¹)
• a = acceleration (m s⁻²)
• t = time (s)

For upward motion, if upward is taken as the positive direction, then the acceleration due to gravity is negative:

a = -g = -10 m s⁻².

At the highest point, the velocity becomes zero, but the acceleration remains 10 m s⁻² downward.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

Final velocity, v = 0 m s⁻¹

Time, t = 2.8 s

Acceleration, a = -10 m s⁻²

Initial velocity, u = ?

Step 2: Select the appropriate equation.

Since velocity, acceleration and time are involved, use:

v = u + at

Step 3: Substitute the known values.

0 = u + (-10 × 2.8)

Step 4: Simplify.

0 = u - 28

Therefore,

u = 28 m s⁻¹

Step 5: Compare with the options.

Option C gives 28 m s⁻¹, which agrees with the calculated value.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (14 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it underestimates the required launch speed. An object projected upward at 14 m s⁻¹ would reach its highest point after only 1.4 s.
Option B (22 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because it does not satisfy the given time of 2.8 s before the velocity becomes zero.
Option D (38 m s⁻¹) is incorrect because such an initial velocity would require 3.8 s to reach the highest point, not 2.8 s.
common_misconceptions: Thinking that both velocity and acceleration become zero at the highest point. Only the velocity is zero; gravity continues to act downward.
Using +10 m s⁻² instead of -10 m s⁻² when upward is chosen as the positive direction.
Substituting the initial velocity as zero instead of the final velocity.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever a vertically projected object reaches its highest point, remember two important facts:

• Velocity = 0 m s⁻¹.
• Acceleration = 10 m s⁻² downward (or -10 m s⁻² if upward is positive).

In reverse-calculation questions, begin by identifying which quantity is unknown and then choose the SUVAT equation that directly relates the known variables.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine throwing a ball straight upward. As it rises, gravity slows it down every second until it stops briefly before falling back.

If it takes 2.8 s to stop and gravity reduces its speed by 10 m s⁻¹ every second, then the ball must have started with:

10 × 2.8 = 28 m s⁻¹.

Therefore, the initial velocity is 28 m s⁻¹.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity → Reverse Calculations of Vertical Projection for a detailed explanation of determining unknown initial or final velocities using the equations of motion. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional JAMB-standard worked examples, concept summaries and practice questions.

question:
During a university mechanics tutorial, four students analysed the sign convention used for motion under gravity. They made the following statements:

Student I: "If upward is chosen as the positive direction, the acceleration due to gravity is represented as -g throughout the motion."

Student II: "At the highest point of the motion, both the velocity and the acceleration due to gravity become zero."

Student III: "Changing the positive direction changes only the sign used in the equations, not the actual direction in which gravity acts."

Student IV: "Provided the chosen sign convention is applied consistently, either upward or downward may be taken as the positive direction."

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of sign convention in vertical motion under gravity. Rather than performing calculations, it tests whether you can correctly interpret how the direction chosen as positive affects the mathematical representation of velocity, displacement and acceleration.
explanation.principle:
Motion under gravity is a special case of uniformly accelerated motion in which the acceleration is provided by Earth's gravitational field.

The acceleration due to gravity always acts vertically downward, regardless of whether the object is moving upward, downward or is momentarily at rest.

The numerical value of the acceleration remains approximately 10 m s⁻² (unless stated otherwise), but its sign depends entirely on the coordinate system chosen.

For example:

• If upward is taken as the positive direction, then a = -g.

• If downward is taken as the positive direction, then a = +g.

The important rule is to choose one sign convention and use it consistently throughout the solution.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Student I's statement.

If upward is chosen as the positive direction, gravity acts downward, opposite to the positive direction.

Therefore, the acceleration is represented as -g.

Student I is correct.

Step 2: Examine Student II's statement.

At the highest point of the motion, the velocity becomes zero only for an instant.

However, gravity continues to act downward with the same magnitude.

The acceleration is therefore not zero.

Student II is incorrect.

Step 3: Examine Student III's statement.

Changing the positive direction only changes the mathematical sign assigned to quantities.

It does not alter the physical direction in which gravity acts.

Student III is correct.

Step 4: Examine Student IV's statement.

Either upward or downward may be selected as the positive direction.

The key requirement is that the chosen sign convention must be maintained consistently throughout the calculation.

Student IV is correct.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Students I, III and IV are correct, while Student II is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Student II, whose statement is false. Although the velocity is zero at maximum height, the acceleration due to gravity is not.
Option C is incorrect because it omits Student I, which correctly describes the sign of gravity when upward is taken as the positive direction.
Option D is incorrect because it includes Student II, whose misconception is one of the most common errors made in questions on vertical motion.
common_misconceptions: Believing that gravity disappears when an object reaches its highest point.
Thinking that zero velocity automatically means zero acceleration.
Assuming the sign of gravity is fixed regardless of the coordinate system chosen.
Changing sign conventions halfway through a calculation.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever solving questions on vertical motion, first decide which direction is positive before substituting values into the equations of motion.

Remember:

• Gravity always acts downward.
• The sign of g depends on your chosen coordinate system.
• At the highest point, velocity is zero but acceleration remains equal to g downward.

JAMB frequently tests these ideas through conceptual questions rather than calculations.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, III and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine throwing a ball straight upward.

When the ball reaches its highest point, it stops rising for a brief moment, so its velocity becomes zero. However, gravity is still pulling it downward, causing it to start falling again.

Whether you call upward or downward the positive direction is your choice, but once you choose it, you must use it consistently throughout the problem.

That is why Students I, III and IV are correct, while Student II is not.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity → Sign Convention to learn how positive and negative directions affect calculations involving vertical motion. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed explanations, worked examples, common examination mistakes and additional JAMB-style practice questions.

question:
During a scheduled maintenance exercise on a wind turbine, an engineer accidentally releases a small inspection tool from a service platform. A colleague standing nearby remarks:

Statement I: 'As the tool falls, its speed increases because gravity provides a constant downward acceleration.'

Statement II: 'At every instant during the fall, the acceleration of the tool remains approximately 10 m s⁻² downward, provided air resistance is neglected.'

Statement III: 'The velocity of the tool increases by equal amounts in equal time intervals while it is falling.'

Statement IV: 'The gravitational acceleration acting on the tool becomes zero just before it reaches the ground because its speed is greatest.'

Assuming air resistance is negligible, which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question presents a real-life case study involving a tool falling from a wind turbine. Rather than performing calculations, it tests your understanding of how gravity influences the velocity and acceleration of a freely falling object.
explanation.principle:
Motion under gravity is a special case of uniformly accelerated motion. Near the Earth's surface and in the absence of air resistance, every freely falling object experiences a constant downward acceleration due to gravity.

For JAMB questions, this acceleration is usually taken as:

• g = 10 m s⁻² (unless otherwise stated).

This means:

• Gravity always acts vertically downward.
• The acceleration remains constant throughout the motion.
• The object's downward velocity increases uniformly with time.
• Acceleration does not disappear simply because the object is moving faster.
explanation.definitions:
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time.

Velocity is the speed of an object in a specified direction.

Free fall refers to motion in which gravity is the only force acting on the object (air resistance neglected).

Uniform acceleration means the velocity changes by equal amounts during equal intervals of time.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

As the tool falls, gravity acts in the same direction as its motion. Consequently, the speed increases continuously.

Statement I is correct.

Step 2: Examine Statement II.

Ignoring air resistance, gravity provides a constant downward acceleration of approximately 10 m s⁻² throughout the entire fall.

Statement II is correct.

Step 3: Examine Statement III.

Because the acceleration is constant, the velocity increases by equal amounts during equal time intervals.

For example, if the velocity increases by 10 m s⁻¹ in the first second, it also increases by another 10 m s⁻¹ in the next second.

Statement III is correct.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

The acceleration due to gravity does not become zero just because the object is moving very fast or is about to reach the ground. Gravity continues to act until the object makes contact with the ground.

Statement IV is incorrect.

Step 5: Determine the correct combination.

Statements I, II and III are correct, while Statement IV is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Statement IV, which wrongly assumes gravity disappears just before impact. It also omits Statements II and III, which are correct.
Option C is incorrect because although Statements II and III are correct, Statement IV is false while Statement I has been incorrectly excluded.
Option D is incorrect because Statement IV is false. Including an incorrect statement makes the entire combination incorrect.
common_misconceptions: Believing gravity becomes zero when an object reaches its highest speed.
Thinking acceleration depends on the speed of the object.
Confusing constant acceleration with constant velocity.
Assuming an object falls at a constant speed simply because gravity is constant.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB, questions on motion under gravity often distinguish between velocity and acceleration.

Remember:

• Velocity changes during free fall.
• Acceleration due to gravity remains constant.
• At no point before impact does gravity stop acting on the object.

Whenever you encounter statements suggesting that acceleration becomes zero because an object is moving fast, treat them with caution—they are usually misconceptions.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, II and III only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine dropping a spanner from a wind turbine. As it falls, gravity keeps pulling it downward with the same acceleration all the way until it hits the ground.

Because of this constant pull:

• The spanner moves faster and faster.
• Its velocity increases uniformly.
• Gravity never stops acting before impact.

Therefore, only Statements I, II and III are correct.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity for a complete lesson on gravitational acceleration, vertical motion, sign convention, velocity changes during free fall, and JAMB-style worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional concept reviews and examination practice.

question:
An aerospace engineer is using a lunar mission planning simulator to compare the vertical motion of a probe launched upward from the Moon and from the Earth. Air resistance is neglected.

The engineer makes the following statements:

I. A probe projected upward with the same initial speed will remain in the air longer on the Moon than on the Earth.

II. A probe projected upward with the same initial speed will reach a greater maximum height on the Moon than on the Earth.

III. The acceleration due to gravity always acts opposite to the direction of motion of the probe.

IV. The equations of uniformly accelerated motion can be applied to the probe's motion on both the Moon and the Earth, provided the appropriate value of gravitational acceleration is used.

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question evaluates your understanding of how different gravitational fields affect vertical motion. Rather than performing calculations, you must determine which statements correctly describe motion under gravity on the Earth and the Moon.
explanation.principle:
Motion under gravity is a special case of uniformly accelerated motion in which the acceleration is provided by gravity.

The acceleration due to gravity (g) is approximately 10 m s⁻² on Earth for JAMB calculations, while it is much smaller on the Moon (about 1.6 m s⁻²).

A smaller gravitational acceleration means that:

• an upward-moving object slows down more gradually,
• it takes longer to reach its highest point,
• it reaches a greater maximum height for the same launch speed.

The same SUVAT equations remain valid because the acceleration is still constant, although its value changes from one celestial body to another.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

Because gravity on the Moon is weaker, the upward speed decreases more slowly. Therefore, the probe spends a longer time rising and a longer total time in flight.

Statement I is correct.

Step 2: Examine Statement II.

A weaker gravitational acceleration allows the probe to travel farther upward before its velocity becomes zero.

Therefore, the maximum height reached is greater on the Moon.

Statement II is correct.

Step 3: Examine Statement III.

Gravity always acts vertically downward toward the centre of the attracting body.

During upward motion, gravity acts opposite to the motion.

However, during downward motion, gravity acts in the same direction as the motion.

Therefore, it is incorrect to say that gravity always acts opposite to the direction of motion.

Statement III is false.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

The equations:

v = u + at

s = ut + ½at²

v² = u² + 2as

remain applicable whenever the acceleration is constant. The only change is the value of g used for the Moon or the Earth.

Statement IV is correct.

Step 5: Determine the correct combination.

Statements I, II and IV are correct, while Statement III is incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because Statement III is false and Statement II, which is correct, has been omitted.
Option C is incorrect because it includes Statement III, which incorrectly claims that gravity always acts opposite to the direction of motion.
Option D is incorrect because not all four statements are correct. Statement III is false.
common_misconceptions: Believing that gravity disappears or changes direction depending on whether an object is moving upward or downward.
Assuming the equations of motion apply only on Earth.
Thinking that weaker gravity changes the form of the equations instead of only changing the value of the acceleration.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB compares motion on different planets or the Moon, remember that the governing equations remain the same if the acceleration is constant. Focus on how the magnitude of gravitational acceleration affects the time of flight, maximum height and rate of change of velocity.
explanation.final_answer: A. I, II and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine throwing the same ball upward on the Earth and on the Moon.

Because the Moon's gravity is much weaker, the ball slows down more gradually, rises higher and stays in the air longer. Gravity always pulls downward, not always opposite to the direction of motion. The same motion equations are still used; only the value of gravitational acceleration changes.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity for a detailed explanation of vertical projection, gravitational acceleration on different celestial bodies, sign convention, and applications of the SUVAT equations. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional JAMB-standard worked examples and practice questions.

question:
An aerospace engineer launches a monitoring capsule vertically upward from a test platform with an initial speed of 60 m s⁻¹. Neglecting air resistance and take the acceleration due to gravity as 10 m s⁻².

What is the vertical displacement of the capsule from its point of projection after 9 s?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to analyse the complete motion of a capsule projected vertically upward. You must recognise that by 9 s, the capsule has already reached its highest point and has started descending. Therefore, the solution involves more than simply substituting into one formula.
explanation.principle:
Motion under gravity consists of two phases:

• Upward motion, during which gravity reduces the velocity uniformly.
• Downward motion, during which gravity increases the downward velocity uniformly.

The acceleration due to gravity acts throughout the motion.

Useful equations include:

v = u + at

s = ut + ½at²

v² = u² + 2as

The choice of equation depends on the information required.
steps:
Step 1: Determine the time taken to reach the highest point.

v = u + at

0 = 60 − 10t

10t = 60

t = 6 s

Therefore, after 9 s the capsule has already been descending for:

9 − 6 = 3 s.

Step 2: Calculate the maximum height.

Using

v² = u² + 2as

0 = 60² + 2(−10)s

20s = 3600

s = 180 m.

Step 3: Calculate the distance fallen during the last 3 s.

The capsule starts descending from rest.

Distance fallen = ½gt²

= ½ × 10 × 3²

= 45 m.

Step 4: Determine the final displacement.

Displacement = Maximum height − Distance fallen

= 180 − 45

= 135 m upward.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

135 m upward corresponds to Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B results from subtracting an incorrect downward distance from the maximum height.
Option C is the maximum height reached, not the displacement after 9 s.
Option D results from adding distances instead of determining the net displacement.
common_misconceptions: Stopping the solution after calculating the maximum height.
Forgetting that gravity acts after the object reaches the highest point.
Confusing total distance travelled with displacement.
Using the upward initial velocity again during the downward motion instead of recognising that the descent begins from rest.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever the given time exceeds the time required to reach maximum height, divide the motion into two stages:

1. Upward journey.
2. Downward journey.

This approach greatly reduces errors in JAMB questions on vertical motion.
explanation.simple_explanation: The capsule rises for 6 s until its velocity becomes zero. It then falls for 3 s. It reaches a maximum height of 180 m and falls 45 m before the 9 s interval ends. Therefore, it is still 135 m above its launch point.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for detailed lessons on vertical projection, maximum height, sign convention, displacement, time of ascent and integrated JAMB-style worked examples.

question:
During a quality assurance meeting at an aerospace manufacturing facility, four trainee engineers made the following statements about the vertical motion of objects under gravity (neglect air resistance).

Statement I: 'At the highest point of a vertically projected object, both its velocity and acceleration are zero.'

Statement II: 'The acceleration due to gravity remains constant and acts downward throughout the motion.'

Statement III: 'The same equations of uniformly accelerated motion can be applied to vertical motion provided a consistent sign convention is maintained.'

Statement IV: 'An object projected vertically upward slows down because gravity acts in the direction opposite to its upward motion.'

Which of the following combinations is correct?

explanation.question_restate: This question reviews the major concepts covered in motion under gravity by asking you to evaluate four statements about vertically projected objects and identify the correct combination.
explanation.principle:
Motion under gravity is a special case of uniformly accelerated motion in which the only significant acceleration is the constant acceleration due to gravity, denoted by g.

Near the Earth's surface, gravity acts vertically downward throughout the entire motion, whether the object is moving upward, momentarily at rest at its highest point, or moving downward.

The standard equations of uniformly accelerated motion remain applicable:

v = u + at

s = ut + 1/2 at²

v² = u² + 2as

s = ((u + v)/2)t

The only requirement is that a consistent sign convention be used throughout the solution.
steps:
Step 1: Examine Statement I.

'At the highest point of a vertically projected object, both its velocity and acceleration are zero.'

This statement is incorrect.

At the highest point, the velocity becomes zero only momentarily before the object begins to descend. However, gravity continues to act downward with constant acceleration g. Therefore, the acceleration is not zero.

Step 2: Examine Statement II.

'The acceleration due to gravity remains constant and acts downward throughout the motion.'

This statement is correct.

Ignoring air resistance, gravity has an approximately constant value (usually taken as 10 m s⁻² in JAMB questions) and always acts vertically downward.

Step 3: Examine Statement III.

'The same equations of uniformly accelerated motion can be applied to vertical motion provided a consistent sign convention is maintained.'

This statement is correct.

Vertical motion under gravity is simply uniformly accelerated motion. Whether upward or downward is chosen as positive, the equations remain valid as long as the sign convention is applied consistently.

Step 4: Examine Statement IV.

'An object projected vertically upward slows down because gravity acts in the direction opposite to its upward motion.'

This statement is correct.

Since gravity acts downward while the object initially moves upward, its speed decreases uniformly until its velocity becomes zero at maximum height.

Step 5: Identify the correct combination.

Statement I is incorrect.

Statements II, III and IV are correct.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it includes Statement I, which wrongly claims that acceleration becomes zero at the highest point.
Option C is incorrect because it also includes the incorrect Statement I.
Option D is incorrect because it includes Statement I while omitting the correct Statement III.
common_misconceptions: Believing gravity disappears when an object reaches its maximum height.
Confusing zero velocity with zero acceleration.
Thinking different equations are required for upward and downward motion.
Assuming gravity acts only while an object is falling.
explanation.exam_tip:
In JAMB examinations, remember this important distinction:

• At maximum height:
  - Velocity = 0
  - Acceleration = g downward

Whenever you encounter statements about vertical motion, first determine whether they refer to velocity or acceleration. These two quantities are frequently confused by candidates.
explanation.final_answer: A. II, III and IV only
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine throwing a ball straight upward.

As it rises, gravity keeps pulling it downward, causing it to slow down. At the highest point, the ball stops for an instant, so its velocity is zero. However, gravity has not stopped acting—it is still pulling the ball downward. That is why the ball immediately begins to fall.

Therefore, Statements II, III and IV are correct, while Statement I is incorrect because acceleration is never zero during the motion (ignoring air resistance).
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Linear Motion → Motion under Gravity for a complete lesson on vertical projection, acceleration due to gravity, sign convention, maximum height, time of ascent, velocity before impact, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional concept reviews and examination practice.
